answer:
Yes — there is a fairly strong research literature on this, and the broad pattern is pretty consistent:

**The day-to-day quality of the work itself** — especially whether it is **interesting, autonomous, meaningful, and uses your skills** — tends to predict job satisfaction and wellbeing **more strongly than pay level does**, once pay is already “good enough” or perceived as fair.

That does **not** mean salary or advancement don’t matter. They do. But the evidence generally says that for sustained wellbeing, **intrinsic job characteristics** matter a lot.

## What the research tends to find

### 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually broken into things like:

- **autonomy**: how much control you have
- **skill variety**: how much the job uses different skills
- **task significance**: whether the work feels important
- **task identity**: whether you can see a whole piece of work through
- **feedback**: whether you can tell how well you’re doing

This goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analyses found these features are strongly related to:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **burnout/strain**

A key review/meta-analysis here is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — a large meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Their general conclusion: work design features like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback have **substantial associations** with satisfaction and motivation.

In plain English: people usually care a lot about whether the job is actually engaging to do.

---

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with wellbeing
There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**, which overlaps with “interesting” but isn’t identical.

Meta-analytic work suggests that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- more **work engagement**
- less **psychological distress**
- less desire to quit

Representative source:

- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work.

So if your current work feels deeply interesting and important, research would treat that as a serious wellbeing asset, not a luxury.

---

### 3. Pay matters — but usually less than people expect
There are two slightly different literatures here:

#### a) Pay and job satisfaction
A well-known meta-analysis found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively small**.

- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  The overall message is roughly: **higher pay helps, but not that much by itself**.

A common interpretation is:
- pay matters a lot when it is **too low**
- pay matters when it is seen as **unfair**
- but once compensation is already strong, **additional increments have diminishing psychological returns**

#### b) Income and happiness more broadly
The broader happiness literature finds that income does improve wellbeing on average, but people often **overestimate** how much a marginal income gain will improve their day-to-day emotional life.

Relevant work includes:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)**

Those papers differ on whether there is a strict “plateau,” but they agree on something important for your case: **income is not irrelevant, but it is far from the only driver of wellbeing**, especially for already high-income professionals.

---

### 4. People often overweight salary/status and underweight daily experience
This is one of the more robust findings from decision research and behavioral economics.

People are often not great at predicting what will make them happier. In particular, they tend to overweight highly visible variables like:

- salary
- title
- prestige
- “being in charge”

and underweight:
- what the work feels like at 11am on a random Tuesday
- whether they enjoy the tasks
- whether the role fits their strengths
- whether the work environment supports autonomy and focus

This is related to research on **affective forecasting** and the **focusing illusion**.

Relevant names:
- **Kahneman**
- **Gilbert**
- **Wilson**

So if you’re trying to predict long-run satisfaction, research suggests the “how will this feel most days?” question is usually more diagnostic than the “which offer sounds more impressive?” question.

---

### 5. Leadership can increase satisfaction — but only if it fits what you actually want
This part is especially relevant because you said leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted.

The strongest framework here is **person-job fit** / **person-environment fit**:

- people are more satisfied when the job fits their values, motives, and identity
- people are less satisfied when the role is high-status but mismatched to what they actually enjoy

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit and work outcomes

This matters because “leading a team” is not just an extrinsic reward. If leadership is a real intrinsic aspiration for you, it may add:

- meaning
- identity fit
- growth
- autonomy
- impact

That can materially change the calculation.

On the other hand, research on promotions/managerial roles is more mixed than many people assume:
- promotions often bring **more stress**
- more **role conflict**
- more **meetings/coordination**
- often less time on the work you were originally good at and enjoyed

So leadership improves wellbeing mainly when it is not just a prestige move, but a **good fit**.

---

### 6. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness matter a lot
A large body of research under **Self-Determination Theory** finds that wellbeing at work depends heavily on whether the job supports three psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — I have meaningful control
- **competence** — I’m able to use and grow my abilities
- **relatedness** — I feel connected/respected

Meta-analytic work shows that when these needs are satisfied at work, people tend to have:

- better wellbeing
- more engagement
- better motivation
- less burnout

Representative reference:
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** on basic psychological needs at work

This is another reason “interesting work” often beats “slightly more money”: interesting work often also satisfies **autonomy + competence**.

---

## So, do people who stay in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?

**On average: yes, usually.**

More precisely:

People in jobs that are more:
- interesting
- meaningful
- autonomous
- skill-using
- growth-oriented

tend to report more:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- sense of purpose

and less:
- burnout
- withdrawal
- turnover intention

compared with people in jobs that mainly offer stronger extrinsic rewards but worse day-to-day work quality.

---

## Do they regret that choice?

This is the one place where the literature is weaker.

I’m **not aware of a clean, famous line of research specifically on regret after choosing a more interesting job over a higher-paid less interesting one**.

But the closest relevant evidence suggests:

1. people often **misforecast** the benefits of salary/status  
2. intrinsic factors like meaning, autonomy, and fit are more important for sustained satisfaction than many people realize  
3. choosing in line with one’s intrinsic motives/identity usually predicts better outcomes than choosing primarily for extrinsic reasons

So the best research-based answer is:

- there is **good evidence** that more interesting/meaningful work is associated with greater wellbeing
- there is **some evidence** that people overvalue extrinsic gains when deciding
- there is **less direct evidence** on long-term regret specifically

---

## The most relevant nuance in your case
Your choice is not simply:

- interesting work vs more money

It is more like:

- **interesting individual-contributor research work**
vs
- **less interesting domain, but team leadership + somewhat more money**

Research would say that the second option could still win **if leadership itself is intrinsically meaningful to you**.

That’s not hand-wavy — it follows from:
- person-job fit
- autonomy/competence/meaning research
- evidence that valued aspirations matter

So the research-based question is not just:
> “Is the work less interesting?”

It’s:
> “Which role gives me the better combination of autonomy, competence, meaning, and fit over the next few years?”

---

## If I had to compress the literature into one sentence
**For long-run job satisfaction, the content of the work usually matters more than a modest pay increase — but a role that fulfills a deeply valued goal like leadership can be worth a lot if it genuinely fits you.**

---

## Representative papers / areas to look at
If you want to read actual papers, these are good anchors:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976/1980)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** — psychological need satisfaction at work
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — outcomes of meaningful work meta-analysis
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and wellbeing
- **Gilbert / Wilson / Kahneman** — affective forecasting, focusing illusion

If you want, I can do a **more literature-review style follow-up** with:
1. exact findings,  
2. stronger vs weaker evidence, and  
3. a short decision framework grounded in the research rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a bit of research relevant to this, even if there isn’t a single clean study of “interesting IC role vs slightly higher-paid less-interesting team lead role.”

### Short version

The best-supported finding is:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — especially **interesting work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, and fit with your motives** — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day work well-being than pay differences**, once pay is already good/fair.

That does **not** mean salary or leadership opportunities don’t matter. They do. But the literature is fairly consistent that:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work predicts higher satisfaction and engagement**
2. **Pay has a positive but usually smaller effect on job satisfaction**
3. **Leadership roles help only when they fit what the person actually wants and is suited for**
4. People often **overweight salary/prestige** and **underweight daily experience**

So if the question is, “Do people who stay in more interesting work tend to be happier?” — **on average, yes**, according to organizational psychology.  
If the question is, “Do they regret it?” — there is **less direct regret research**, but the broader evidence suggests people’s actual well-being tracks the quality of daily work more than they often predict beforehand.

---

## 1) Interesting work is strongly linked to satisfaction

A classic and still influential line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analytic work such as:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design

That literature shows that jobs with more:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **problem solving / knowledge characteristics**

are associated with higher:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- sometimes performance as well

This is probably the closest direct literature to “interesting work.”  
In practical terms: jobs that are intellectually engaging and let you use/stretch your abilities are consistently better for motivation and satisfaction.

### Why this matters
A lot of “interestingness” is not just fun — it overlaps with:
- challenge
- learning
- competence
- novelty
- agency

Those are psychologically important, not just nice-to-have.

---

## 2) Meaningful work has robust links to well-being

There’s also a newer literature on **meaningful work**, which overlaps with but is not identical to “interesting work.”

A useful reference is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes

The general result: people who perceive their work as meaningful tend to report higher:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction
- organizational commitment

and lower:

- turnover intentions
- burnout / strain

The associations are usually **moderate to strong**, not trivial.

### Important nuance
“Interesting” and “meaningful” are different.

- A job can be interesting but not deeply meaningful.
- A job can be meaningful but not very interesting.
- The best outcomes often come when both are present.

For research/engineering roles, meaning often comes from:
- working on important problems
- being around strong colleagues
- building things that matter
- learning quickly

If the Google role is “less interesting,” the question is whether it compensates with **meaning, impact, autonomy, growth, or leadership fit**.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

One of the most cited findings here is that the link between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is real but **fairly small**.

A standard reference is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

The broad takeaway is:

- Higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction
- **But the relationship is much weaker than many people intuitively assume**

In meta-analytic terms, the effect is usually described as **small**.

That fits a broader well-being literature:
- income improves well-being, especially when moving from not-enough to enough
- but at already high income levels, additional gains often have **diminishing psychological returns**
- relative comparisons and fairness matter a lot

### For your case
If the offer is **“a bit higher salary”** rather than life-changing money, the literature would generally predict that:
- the salary bump may help somewhat
- but it is unlikely to dominate the effect of a large change in day-to-day work quality

Put crudely:  
**an extra increment of compensation often matters less than whether you enjoy what you spend most of your waking hours doing.**

---

## 4) Leadership can increase satisfaction — but only if it matches the person

This part is subtler.

You mentioned that **leading a small team is something you always wanted**. Research would treat that as important, because **person-job fit** matters a lot.

A classic meta-analysis is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

General finding:
- better fit between the person and the role predicts higher job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to leave

So if you have a genuine motivation to lead, that is not a minor detail.  
It may satisfy motives around:
- influence
- growth
- status
- mentoring
- broader leverage

And that could offset some loss of interesting technical content.

### But there’s a warning from the literature
There’s also a lot of evidence that being a good individual contributor and being happy/effective as a manager are not the same thing.

Related research on promotions and management often shows:
- organizations systematically promote strong ICs into management
- managerial work is often very different from what those people actually enjoy
- mismatches can reduce satisfaction and effectiveness

A well-known example from labor economics/management research is evidence related to the **Peter Principle**:
- people are often promoted based on current-role performance, not role fit for the next role

So the relevant research question is not “is management good?”  
It is:

**“Do I actually want the daily activity of management?”**

That means:
- 1:1s
- hiring
- performance conversations
- conflict resolution
- cross-functional coordination
- resource allocation
- less hands-on building
- less uninterrupted deep work

People often say they want “leadership” when they really want:
- influence
- ownership
- strategic input
- mentorship

Those can sometimes be obtained without taking a full management role.

Research would suggest this distinction matters a lot.

---

## 5) Self-determination research strongly favors autonomy, mastery, and relatedness

Another very relevant framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; applied to work by Gagné, Deci, and others).

Core claim:
People function better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Across many studies, jobs that support these needs are associated with better:
- motivation
- performance
- persistence
- well-being

This matters because “interesting work” often supports:
- **competence**: you’re learning and using your abilities
- **autonomy**: you care about the work enough to engage deeply
- **relatedness**: often stronger in mission-driven or intellectually strong environments

A less interesting but more managerial role might raise autonomy and relatedness, but lower competence-related enjoyment if you like technical problem solving more than people-management.

So from an SDT perspective, the question is:
**Which role better supports your core needs in actual daily practice?**

---

## 6) People often mispredict what will make them happier

There is also a large literature on **affective forecasting** and the **focusing illusion**.

Relevant names:
- **Daniel Kahneman**
- **Gilbert & Wilson**
- **Schkade & Kahneman**

One of the durable findings is that people often:
- overemphasize salient differences like pay, prestige, title
- underemphasize routine experiential differences like:
  - how the workday feels
  - how often they are in flow
  - whether the work is draining
  - whether they like the problems they are solving

This is highly relevant to career choices.

In other words:
When imagining the Google offer, it is easy to focus on:
- “higher salary”
- “team lead”
- “Google”

But your experienced well-being will depend much more on:
- What does a Tuesday afternoon feel like?
- How many hours per week are intrinsically engaging?
- Do I leave work energized or depleted?
- Do I admire the problems and people around me?

Research suggests those “boring daily details” matter more than people expect.

---

## 7) What about regret specifically?

This is the part where evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong literature that cleanly shows:
- “people who choose more interesting work later regret it less than people who choose higher pay/leadership”

But several adjacent findings matter:

### What is well supported
People tend to regret or leave jobs when there is:
- low fit
- low meaning
- low engagement
- low autonomy
- low fairness
- burnout

and they tend to stay and report higher satisfaction when work is:
- intrinsically motivating
- meaningful
- growth-supportive
- aligned with values and interests

So while “regret” itself isn’t measured as often, the predictors of later dissatisfaction are fairly well known.

### One practical implication
If you take a less-interesting job mainly for extrinsic reasons, and the daily work does not fit you, the literature would predict higher risk of:
- disengagement
- lower satisfaction
- wanting to switch again

If you stay in an interesting role but feel blocked on growth you deeply value, then **that** can also become a source of dissatisfaction.

So the research answer is not “always choose interesting work”; it’s closer to:

> **Choose the role with the better long-run fit on intrinsic motivation, autonomy, meaning, and personal motives — and do not overrate moderate pay differences.**

---

## 8) If I had to summarize the evidence in one sentence

If compensation is already strong, then **the day-to-day quality of the work itself** — especially **interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit** — usually matters **more for happiness and satisfaction** than incremental pay, while **leadership opportunities matter a lot only if you genuinely want the actual work of leading**.

---

## 9) A research-based way to compare your two options

Not personal advice — just translating the literature into variables that actually predict outcomes.

Rate each role on:

1. **Intrinsic interest**
   - How often will the core work itself be engaging?

2. **Autonomy**
   - How much control will you have over what/how you work?

3. **Competence growth**
   - Which role makes you better at things you care about?

4. **Meaning / task significance**
   - Which work feels more worth doing?

5. **Role fit**
   - Does the daily activity match your motives?

6. **Leadership fit**
   - Do you want status/title, or the actual craft of management?

7. **Social environment**
   - Team quality, trust, manager quality, peers

8. **Compensation**
   - Is the extra money materially meaningful, or mostly symbolic?

9. **Option value**
   - Which role keeps more attractive future paths open?

That framework is much closer to what the literature says than a simple “salary vs interest” tradeoff.

---

## 10) If you want actual papers to start with

A solid shortlist:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
  *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
  *Self-determination theory and work motivation.*

- For the forecasting/misprediction angle:
  - **Schkade & Kahneman (1998)** on the focusing illusion
  - broader work by **Gilbert & Wilson** on affective forecasting

- For income/well-being:
  - **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
  - **Killingsworth (2021)** and later reconciliation work with Kahneman/Mellers

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more precise literature review** with 8–12 papers and the specific findings most relevant to your situation, or  
2. help turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the research** rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs. slightly higher salary.” The closest literatures are on:

- **meaningful/interesting work**
- **intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation**
- **job design** (autonomy, variety, challenge, significance)
- **pay and well-being**
- **person–job fit**

## Short version

Across many studies, **doing work that feels interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and aligned with your values tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than modest differences in pay**, especially once income is already high enough that financial strain is not the issue.

That does **not** mean “always choose the more interesting job.” Two big qualifiers:

1. **Leadership itself may be an intrinsic value** for you, not just a status bump.
2. The research suggests that **fit** matters a lot: people do best when the job matches what they personally value most.

Also, **direct research on “later regret” is surprisingly thin**. The field has much better evidence on job satisfaction, burnout, engagement, and turnover than on explicit regret.

---

# What the research says

## 1) Interesting/meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

A substantial literature finds that **meaningful work** is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **psychological well-being**

and negatively associated with:

- **burnout**
- **turnover intentions**
- **psychological distress**

A useful meta-analysis here is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, on **meaningful work** and related outcomes.

The broad result is that the link between meaningful work and positive work outcomes is **moderate to strong**, stronger than many people expect.

### Important nuance
“Interesting” is not exactly the same as “meaningful,” but in practice they overlap a lot in knowledge work. Work tends to feel more satisfying when it is:

- intellectually engaging
- significant
- aligned with one’s identity
- rich in learning/growth

---

## 2) Job design research: autonomy, variety, challenge, and significance matter a lot

One of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology is that people are happier in jobs with:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to use and develop competence**

Classic theory: **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**

Major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**

Their review found that work design characteristics like autonomy and task variety have **substantial relationships** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower strain

If by “interesting” you mean “I actually like spending my day doing the work,” then this literature is very relevant. **How the work feels hour-by-hour matters a lot.**

---

## 3) Intrinsic motivation tends to beat extrinsic motivation for well-being

From **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci, Ryan and others), the most robust finding is that people function better when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When those needs are satisfied, people tend to report higher:

- vitality
- engagement
- job satisfaction
- general well-being

There’s also a broader literature showing that prioritizing **intrinsic goals** (growth, contribution, mastery, relationships) over **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status) is generally associated with better well-being.

That does **not** mean money/status are unimportant. It means that if a decision mainly improves extrinsic rewards while reducing intrinsic satisfaction, the net effect on happiness is often smaller than people predict.

Relevant reviews/meta-analyses include work by:

- **Van den Broeck et al.** on need satisfaction at work
- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)** reviewing self-determination theory in work organizations

---

## 4) Pay matters — but usually less than people think, especially at high income levels

There are two separate questions here:

### A. Does more pay improve well-being?
Yes, but with **diminishing returns**.

The income–well-being literature consistently finds that:
- low-to-moderate income increases matter a lot, especially if they reduce stress/insecurity
- at already high incomes, additional salary usually has **smaller marginal effects**

The exact shape is debated:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** suggested a plateau in day-to-day emotional well-being
- **Killingsworth (2021)** argued well-being keeps rising, though with nuance
- later discussions/reanalyses show the truth is more complex than a single plateau

But the consensus is still: **once basic and comfort needs are well met, incremental salary increases usually have less impact than job quality variables**.

### B. Is pay strongly related to job satisfaction?
Only **weakly to moderately**.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on **pay and job satisfaction**

Main result: **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**. In plain English: people care about pay, but it explains much less of whether they like their job than many assume.

Also, **pay fairness** often matters more than absolute pay.

So:
- going from underpaid to fairly paid matters a lot
- going from very well paid to slightly more well paid often matters much less

---

## 5) Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

A very important literature here is **person–environment fit**, especially:

- **person–job fit**
- **person–organization fit**

Meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

Result: fit is strongly related to:
- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit

This is probably the most relevant point for your case.

If you deeply value:
- intellectual novelty
- technical depth
- research excitement

then losing those may cost more than average.

If you deeply value:
- building teams
- mentoring
- having broader leverage
- leadership identity

then gaining those may matter more than average.

The literature does **not** support a one-size-fits-all answer. It strongly supports the claim that **alignment with your own values and motives predicts later satisfaction**.

---

## 6) What about leadership roles specifically?

Research on promotions/leadership is more mixed.

Leadership roles can increase:
- status
- compensation
- autonomy
- influence
- sense of impact

But they can also increase:
- stress
- administrative burden
- role conflict
- time fragmentation
- distance from the work one actually enjoys

So the question is not “leadership = better or worse.”
It is more like:

**Does this leadership role increase or decrease the things that drive your well-being?**

If leading a small team is something you’ve “always wanted,” then that is not just an external prestige factor — it may be a real intrinsic aspiration tied to competence, autonomy, and contribution. Research would treat that as important.

---

# Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of strong, famous longitudinal studies specifically showing:
- “people who stayed in the more interesting job later regretted it less,” or
- “people who chose money/leadership later regretted it more.”

What we do have are **proxy outcomes**:

- later job satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- engagement
- well-being

On those proxies, the literature generally favors:
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- intrinsic motivation
- value fit

over modest pay differences.

So the research-based statement would be:

> There is strong evidence that people are typically more satisfied and psychologically healthier in jobs they find meaningful, interesting, and autonomy-supportive; there is much weaker evidence that modest additional pay produces comparable gains in happiness once income is already high.

That is not the same as saying they never regret it. Just that the measurable outcomes we do have point that way.

---

# One especially relevant implication for your situation

Your case is not really:

- **interesting work** vs **more money**

It is more like:

- **interesting technical/research work**
vs
- **leadership opportunity + somewhat more money + less interesting content**

Research suggests you should compare at least **four** dimensions, not two:

1. **Day-to-day intrinsic enjoyment**
   - How much do you enjoy the actual work itself?

2. **Autonomy / control**
   - Which job gives you more real control over how you spend your time?

3. **Growth in the direction you value**
   - Is your deeper aspiration “be a stronger researcher” or “become a leader of researchers”?

4. **Fit with your identity**
   - Which role feels more like who you want to become?

That framing is very consistent with the literature.

---

# If I had to summarize the research in one sentence

For people who are already well compensated, **the quality of the work itself — especially interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit with personal values — tends to matter more for long-run job satisfaction and well-being than a modest salary increase**.

But if **leading a team** is genuinely one of your core intrinsic goals, then that leadership opportunity may count as part of the “interesting/meaningful” side rather than the “extrinsic” side.

---

# Representative references

A few good anchor points:

- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
  *A meta-analytic investigation of the relation of meaningful work to well-being and career-related variables.*

- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
  *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
  *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M. (2017).**  
  *Self-determination theory in work organizations: The state of a science.*

- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010).**  
  *High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.*

- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021).**  
  *Experienced well-being rises with income, even above \$75,000 per year.*

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. turn this into a **very practical decision framework grounded in the literature**, or  
2. give you a **more academic summary with actual effect sizes** where they’re reasonably well established.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**On average, people are more satisfied and psychologically better off in jobs that are interesting, meaningful, and autonomous, and these factors usually predict job satisfaction more strongly than moderate pay differences do.**  
Direct evidence on **later regret** is thinner, but the evidence we do have points in the same direction: better intrinsic fit tends to predict higher career satisfaction and lower turnover/burnout.

A compact summary of the literature:

## 1) “Interesting work” is not a fluffy variable; it is one of the central predictors of job satisfaction

The classic line of research here is **job design**.

### Job Characteristics Model
**Hackman & Oldham (1970s)** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they offer:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These aren’t exactly “interestingness,” but together they capture a lot of what people mean by interesting, engaging work.

### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, on work design

From memory, their results showed that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback** have **moderate to fairly strong associations** with:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal intentions

In practical terms: **the day-to-day content of the work matters a lot**.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

There is a substantial literature showing that **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction**, but the relationship is **real and modest**, not dominant.

A commonly cited meta-analysis:
- **Judge et al. (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction

The basic finding: the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but relatively small** compared with many intrinsic job features.

So the literature does **not** say “money doesn’t matter.”  
It says more like:

> **Money matters, but daily work experience often matters more.**

This becomes especially relevant when comparing **two already very good jobs** rather than poverty vs stability.

---

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly linked to wellbeing

A related literature looks at **meaningful work**, which overlaps with “interesting work” but is not identical.

A useful meta-analysis:
- **Allan et al. (2019)** on meaningful work

General result:
- meaningful work is associated with **higher job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction / wellbeing**
- greater **commitment**
- lower **turnover intentions**

Again, the signal is fairly consistent: **if the work feels worthwhile and engaging, people tend to do better psychologically**.

---

## 4) Fit matters a lot: the best job is not universally the highest-paid or most interesting one, but the one that fits your motives

This is the **person-job fit / person-organization fit** literature.

A key meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

General result:
- better fit predicts **higher job satisfaction**
- higher organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit
- better performance, to a degree

This matters for your specific case because your choice is not just:
- interesting work vs more money

It is also:
- **individual contributor work vs leading a small team**

If **leading a team is something you genuinely want**, that counts as **intrinsic fit**, not just extrinsic status.

In other words, the literature would not say:
- “Always choose the more technically interesting job”

It would say:
- “Choose the job with the better mix of intrinsic motivation, autonomy, fit, and meaning.”

For some people, leadership is intrinsically energizing. For others, it is mostly meetings, people problems, and reduced time on craft.

---

## 5) Self-determination research strongly favors intrinsic over extrinsic motivation

Another relevant framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan and a huge later literature).

Very broadly, people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

That tends to align more with:
- interesting/challenging work
- ownership
- growth
- good colleagues

than with:
- compensation alone
- status alone

Related work by **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations found that prioritizing extrinsic goals like money/status/image over intrinsic ones is often associated with **lower wellbeing**.

That doesn’t mean “never choose money.”  
It means **if the main reason to switch is an extrinsic bump, the wellbeing payoff is often smaller than people forecast**.

---

## 6) There is also “revealed preference” evidence: people often accept less pay for more interesting or meaningful work

Labor economists talk about **compensating differentials**: people are often willing to trade some salary for desirable non-pay job attributes.

One especially relevant paper:
- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**

The basic finding was that scientists were willing to accept lower pay for the opportunity to do more scientifically valued work.

That’s not exactly your situation, but it is highly relevant: **in knowledge work, interestingness itself has labor-market value**. People will often “pay” for it.

So the fact that the Google offer has to pay more is not surprising; it may partly reflect the fact that the work itself is less intrinsically attractive to you.

---

## 7) What about regret specifically?

This is where the literature gets weaker.

I do **not** recall strong, famous studies directly asking:
- “Do people later regret taking the higher-paying but less interesting job?”
vs
- “Do they regret staying in the more interesting one?”

Most research measures:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover
- life satisfaction
- career satisfaction

rather than explicit retrospective regret.

But the indirect evidence suggests:
- **intrinsically satisfying work** tends to produce more durable satisfaction
- **pay/promotion gains** often produce a boost, but people partly adapt
- misfit in day-to-day work tends to wear on people because it is experienced repeatedly

So while I can’t give you a clean “regret” statistic, the best-supported inference is:

> **People seem to do better over time when their work remains interesting, meaningful, and aligned with their motives, even if another option offers somewhat more money.**

---

## 8) The management angle is genuinely important

Your case is unusual because the less interesting role also offers something many people value a lot: **leadership responsibility**.

Research on management/leadership transitions is more mixed than the literature on interesting work.

What’s fairly well-supported:
- leadership roles can increase **autonomy, status, influence**
- but also increase **role overload, stress, ambiguity, politics**
- and reduce time spent on the work one originally liked

So the key empirical question is not:
- “Is management better?”

It is:
- **“Am I the kind of person for whom leadership itself is intrinsically rewarding?”**

If yes, then the Google option may not be “less interesting” in the deeper psychological sense; it may simply be **interesting in a different way**.

If no, then the combination “more pay + less interesting work + more managerial burden” is exactly the sort of tradeoff the literature suggests can lower satisfaction.

---

## 9) If I compress the evidence into one practical sentence

A research-faithful summary would be:

> **At high levels of baseline compensation, differences in daily work quality—interest, autonomy, meaning, fit, and relationships—usually matter more for sustained happiness than a moderate salary increase.**

That is probably the cleanest answer to your question.

---

## 10) If you want a research-based way to think about your own choice

The literature suggests comparing these factors, roughly in this order:

1. **Daily task interest / skill use**  
   Will I enjoy what I actually do each day?

2. **Autonomy and control**  
   Will I have agency over work, priorities, and methods?

3. **Fit with identity and motives**  
   Am I more fulfilled by building/researching, or by leading/coordinating?

4. **Meaning / significance**  
   Does the work feel worthwhile?

5. **People and manager quality**  
   Huge predictor of job experience.

6. **Growth path**  
   Is the role growing me in the direction I actually want?

7. **Compensation**  
   Important, but usually not the main driver once both options are already strong.

---

## Bottom line

**Yes, there is a substantial body of research suggesting that more interesting/intrinsically motivating work tends to be associated with higher job satisfaction and wellbeing than a modest increase in pay alone.**  
**There is less direct evidence on long-run regret**, but the best available evidence points the same way: **intrinsic fit tends to age better than purely extrinsic gains**.

If you want, I can do a second pass that is **more citation-heavy**, with a short annotated list of papers and the specific findings most relevant to your case.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on this, with one important caveat: it mostly studies **job satisfaction, well-being, engagement, burnout, turnover, and career satisfaction**—not “regret” in the everyday sense. On those outcomes, **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work tends to matter a lot**, and typically **more than modest differences in pay**.

## What the evidence says

### 1. “Interesting work” is strongly associated with job satisfaction and well-being
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually broken into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **variety / challenge**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / learning**
- **fit with your interests and values**

These are some of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.

A major meta-analysis of work design research found that characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback** had **moderate-to-large correlations** with job satisfaction and internal motivation—often around **r ≈ .30 to .50** depending on the specific feature.  
**Reference:** Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007), *Journal of Applied Psychology*.

That’s a big effect by social-science standards.

### 2. Meaningful work is also strongly linked to happiness at work
A newer literature on **meaningful work** finds that people who see their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- better **overall well-being**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intentions**

Meta-analytic estimates are again generally **moderate**, often larger than pay effects.  
**Reference:** Allan et al. (2019/2020 meta-analytic work on meaningful work outcomes).

So if by “interesting” you mean “I’m intellectually engaged and the work feels worth doing,” the literature is strongly favorable to that.

### 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect
There is a robust literature showing that **pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction**.

A well-known meta-analysis found the correlation between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is only around **r ≈ .15**. That is real, but small.  
**Reference:** Judge et al. / Williams et al. meta-analytic work on pay and job satisfaction.

In plain English: people do prefer more money, but once you’re already well-paid, **a bit more salary usually does not buy a large increase in day-to-day work happiness**.

This fits the broader happiness literature too: income helps, especially when it relieves constraints or anxiety, but **marginal gains shrink**, and people adapt.

### 4. Person-job fit matters a lot
Another strong finding is that people are happier when the job fits what they value and enjoy.

Meta-analyses on **person-job fit** and **needs-supplies fit** show fairly strong relationships with job satisfaction—often stronger than objective rewards like salary or title.  
**Reference:** Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005), meta-analysis on fit.

This matters for your case because the decision is not simply “interesting vs not interesting.” It’s also:

- **interesting individual-contributor work**
vs
- **less interesting domain but more leadership opportunity**

If leading a team is something you deeply value, that can itself be part of “fit.”

### 5. Intrinsic motives generally predict better well-being than extrinsic motives
Across self-determination theory and related work, pursuing **intrinsic goals**—interest, growth, mastery, meaningful contribution, close relationships—tends to be associated with better well-being than prioritizing **extrinsic goals** like money, status, and prestige.

That does **not** mean money or status are bad. It means that when people choose primarily on extrinsic grounds, they tend to do worse on well-being than when their work satisfies intrinsic motives.  
**References:** Deci & Ryan / self-determination theory literature; Kasser & Ryan on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations.

This is one of the most relevant findings to your question.

---

## What about regret specifically?

Here the evidence is weaker.

There is much less research directly measuring “I chose the interesting job and later regretted it” versus “I chose the higher-status job and regretted it.” The field more commonly measures:

- job satisfaction
- career satisfaction
- turnover intention
- burnout
- mental health
- meaning

That said, the available evidence suggests:

- People who end up in work that is **more meaningful, more autonomous, and better aligned with their interests** tend to report **higher sustained satisfaction**.
- Gains from **salary and promotion** are often **smaller and more prone to adaptation**.
- Promotions/management roles can be positive, but they often come with **higher stress, longer hours, and administrative burden**, so they are **not automatically a well-being upgrade**.

So the best research-based summary is not “interesting work always wins,” but rather:

> **Daily work quality and intrinsic fit usually predict happiness more strongly than modest increases in pay or status.**

---

## How this maps to your exact dilemma

You have at least three separable factors:

1. **Interestingness of the work**
2. **Leadership opportunity**
3. **Higher pay**

Research suggests:

- **Interestingness / meaning / autonomy**: big positive effect
- **Slightly higher pay**: positive, but usually small
- **Leadership opportunity**: potentially very important if it matches a genuine personal motive, but mixed if it mainly means people management on less interesting problems

So from the literature’s perspective, the key question is not just:
- “OpenAI work is more interesting; Google pays more”

It is:
- “Does leading a small team satisfy a deep intrinsic goal of mine enough to outweigh lower intellectual interest in the work itself?”

Because **wanting to lead** can also be an intrinsic value, not just an extrinsic one.

---

## Rough ranking of what tends to matter most for satisfaction

Very roughly, based on the literature:

1. **Meaning / interest / autonomy / fit**
2. **Relationships and manager quality**
3. **Workload and stress**
4. **Growth/learning**
5. **Pay level**  
6. **Title/status alone**

That ordering is not universal, but it’s a decent summary of the research.

---

## Bottom line

If you want the most research-grounded answer:

- **Yes**, people in more interesting/meaningful/autonomy-rich jobs tend to be happier on average.
- The effect of interesting/meaningful work on job satisfaction is usually **substantially larger** than the effect of a modest salary increase.
- Research on “regret” specifically is limited, but studies on intrinsic vs extrinsic motives suggest that choosing based mainly on money/status is **less likely** to maximize long-term satisfaction than choosing based on interest, meaning, and fit.
- The main complication in your case is that **leading a team** may itself be an intrinsically important source of meaning and growth.

## If you want a very short evidence-based rule of thumb

If the pay difference is only “a bit higher,” and both jobs are already financially excellent, then the literature would usually predict:

> **The more interesting role is better for happiness unless the leadership opportunity is itself a major source of meaning and fit for you.**

If you want, I can give you a **research-based decision checklist** that translates these findings into a practical scorecard for your exact choice.

answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology is:

**People are usually happier in work they find interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to their skills/interests.**  
**Pay matters, but its direct relationship to job satisfaction is typically much smaller.**

A few robust findings:

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to job satisfaction

The classic line of research here is the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses on **work design**.

### What matters most in jobs
Jobs tend to be more satisfying when they provide:
- **skill variety**
- **task significance** (“this work matters”)
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- often also **learning/growth**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Reviewed a very large literature on work design.  
  **Result:** motivational job characteristics like autonomy, task significance, task variety, etc. were **substantially related** to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions.

This is one of the strongest and most replicated findings in work psychology:  
**the day-to-day qualities of the work itself matter a lot.**

---

## 2) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another strong line of evidence is **fit**: whether the job matches your interests, values, and abilities.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  **Result:** person-job fit and person-organization fit are reliably associated with **higher job satisfaction**, **stronger commitment**, and **lower intention to quit**.

- In the more specific **vocational interest congruence** literature, the effect is usually **positive but more modest**: if your interests match the work, satisfaction tends to be higher.

So if one role is plainly “more interesting” to you, the literature would generally predict:
- higher satisfaction
- lower burnout/withdrawal
- less desire to leave later

---

## 3) Meaningful work is associated with better wellbeing

There is also a large “meaningful work” literature.

Across reviews and meta-analytic work, **perceiving work as meaningful** is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- higher life satisfaction
- lower burnout and turnover intentions

This literature is not always perfectly causal, but it is very consistent.

A useful conceptual summary is:
- people do better when work feels like **more than just compensation**
- meaning/interest is not a luxury variable; it is a major predictor of wellbeing

---

## 4) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

There is strong evidence that **pay has a positive relationship with job satisfaction**, but it is usually **small to modest**, especially compared with work design and fit.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis  
  **Result:** pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is **not large**.

So, roughly:
- if pay is too low, unfair, unstable, or financially constraining, it matters a lot
- once pay is already high/comfortable, **extra money tends to buy less additional job satisfaction than people predict**

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means:
- money helps
- but it usually does **not fully offset** doing work you find less engaging or less meaningful

---

## 5) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals: intrinsic tends to win for wellbeing

A large literature in **Self-Determination Theory** distinguishes:
- **intrinsic motives/goals**: interest, growth, mastery, contribution, relationships
- **extrinsic motives/goals**: money, status, image

The broad result is:

**When people orient more strongly toward intrinsic goals, they tend to have better wellbeing.**  
**When extrinsic goals dominate, wellbeing is often worse or no better, even if those goals are attained.**

This does not mean salary/status are bad. It means they are usually **weaker predictors of sustained wellbeing** than:
- autonomy
- competence/growth
- relatedness
- meaning

Relevant reviews:
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** on self-determination theory at work
- broader need-satisfaction research at work consistently finds that autonomy, competence, and relatedness predict wellbeing and motivation strongly

---

## 6) What about regret?

Direct research on **career-choice regret** is thinner than research on satisfaction, fit, and turnover. But the pattern is fairly clear:

People tend to regret choices more when the new role produces:
- lower fit
- lower meaning
- less autonomy
- less use of strengths
- poorer manager/culture
- higher stress without compensating purpose

And people tend to be less likely to look back negatively when they chose a role that better matched:
- interests
- values
- growth goals

So while I wouldn’t claim “people who pick the more interesting job never regret it,” the evidence does suggest:

**On average, choosing a job for intrinsic qualities predicts better long-run satisfaction than choosing mainly for extrinsic gains.**

---

# Important nuance for your case: leadership may itself be an intrinsic gain

Your situation is not simply:

- **interesting work** vs **money**

It is more like:

- **interesting work now**
vs
- **somewhat less interesting domain work, but a chance to lead**, which you say you’ve always wanted

That matters because **leading a team** may satisfy intrinsic needs too:
- autonomy
- impact
- growth
- mastery in a new domain
- social connection
- identity

So the research would not treat “leadership” as purely extrinsic.  
If leading people is deeply meaningful to you, that can be a **real intrinsic benefit**, not just a title.

In other words, the scientifically relevant comparison is probably:

### Option A: more intellectually interesting individual-contributor work
vs
### Option B: less interesting subject matter, but more leadership, impact, and growth toward a desired identity

The research does **not** say A always beats B.  
It says the better long-run outcome usually comes from the role that gives you more of the core ingredients:
- interest
- meaning
- autonomy
- competence/growth
- fit with values
- good social environment

---

# What the evidence would predict in a high-income case like yours

Since both jobs are presumably already very well paid, the literature would generally predict:

- the **incremental salary increase** is likely to have **limited effect** on happiness by itself
- the **quality of the work itself** is likely to matter more
- if the Google role is truly “much less interesting” and leadership does not compensate for that, it may reduce satisfaction
- if leading a team is something you deeply value and the role gives you real autonomy/impact, it could absolutely outweigh lower technical interest

So the empirical question is not “interesting work or salary?”  
It is closer to:

**Will the leadership role increase or decrease your total intrinsic satisfaction?**

---

# If you want the most evidence-based factors to compare, rank these

If I were translating the literature into a decision rubric, I’d compare the two jobs on:

1. **Interest / intrinsic motivation**
2. **Meaning / task significance**
3. **Autonomy**
4. **Growth / learning**
5. **Person-role fit**
6. **Manager / team quality**
7. **Workload / stress / hours**
8. **Compensation and fairness**
9. **Future option value** (which role opens better future paths)
10. **Identity fit** (“which version of myself do I want to become?”)

That list is closer to the actual research than just comparing salary/title.

---

# Bottom line from the literature

If you want the most defensible summary:

- **Yes**, there is a lot of research suggesting that **interesting/meaningful work is a major driver of job satisfaction and wellbeing**
- **Yes**, those effects are often **stronger than the effect of higher pay**
- **No**, the evidence does not support the idea that higher salary alone usually makes up for less interesting work, especially once compensation is already high
- **But** in your case, **leadership aspiration** could be a major intrinsic benefit, so the decision is not reducible to “interesting vs money”

---

## A few key references to look up
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — self-determination theory and work motivation  
- **Hackman & Oldham** — job characteristics model  
- On income and wellbeing more generally: **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**; **Killingsworth et al. (2023)** for a later update

If you want, I can give you a **more academic version with 10–15 papers and one-sentence findings for each**, or a **decision matrix explicitly grounded in these literatures**.

answer:
Yes. The broad research picture is:

1. **Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction and well-being.**
2. **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once income is already high enough to remove financial stress.**
3. **Fit matters a lot**: if leading people is something you deeply want, that can itself be an important source of meaning/satisfaction.
4. **Direct evidence on “regret” in exactly this tradeoff is limited**; most of the evidence is indirect, via long-run well-being, adaptation, and person-job fit.

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest job-related predictors of satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” usually gets broken into components like:

- **skill variety**
- **challenge / complexity**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **opportunities to learn**

The classic work here is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analytic work—especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**—found that these kinds of job design features are **consistently associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower burnout/withdrawal**.

A useful summary of the literature is:

- jobs with **more autonomy, variety, and significance** tend to produce **substantially better day-to-day work experience**
- these effects are not tiny; they are usually **stronger than the effect of pay level on job satisfaction**

So if by “interesting” you mean “I get to work on intellectually engaging, meaningful problems with room for learning and autonomy,” the research is very favorable to that.

## 2) Meaningful work also predicts broader well-being, not just “liking your job”

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Reviews and meta-analyses by authors such as **Allan and colleagues** find that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **burnout**

This matters because the question is not only “Will I like the job?” but “Will I be happier overall?” Work spills over into life. There is also a known moderate link between **job satisfaction and life satisfaction**.

So, on average, people in work they find meaningful/interesting do tend to report being happier overall—not just happier while at work.

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic job quality once you’re already well-paid

The pay literature is more nuanced than “money doesn’t matter.”

- It **does** matter.
- But the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is usually **modest**, not huge.

A key meta-analysis here is **Judge et al. (2010)**, which found that pay and job satisfaction are positively related, but the effect is **much smaller** than many people intuitively expect.

The income/well-being literature in economics/psychology says something similar:

- more income generally improves life evaluation
- but for **day-to-day emotional well-being**, the gains are smaller and show **diminishing returns**
- this is especially relevant at already high income levels

For someone already in a highly paid research role, a **“bit higher salary”** is less likely—according to the literature—to dominate long-run well-being than major differences in:

- how interesting the work is
- autonomy
- learning
- meaning
- whether the role fits your values

## 4) People adapt relatively quickly to extrinsic gains; they adapt less to bad daily experience

This is one of the more important findings for your situation.

There is a lot of evidence for **hedonic adaptation**: people often get used to higher pay, prestige, titles, etc. The initial boost may fade.

By contrast, the **ongoing daily texture of the work**—whether it is interesting, engaging, frustrating, empty, or meaningful—keeps showing up every day. That tends to have a more durable effect on experienced well-being.

So the literature would generally predict that:

- **a modest salary increase** may feel very good at first, but people often adapt
- **doing less interesting work every day** can remain costly for well-being over time

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means the psychological literature tends to treat **daily work quality** as a more persistent determinant of satisfaction than a moderate pay delta.

## 5) Leadership opportunity is a real counterweight, because “fit” matters a lot

This is the biggest caveat in your case.

You said that **leading a small team is something you always wanted to do**. Research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**—for example **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**—shows that alignment between a role and your motives/values strongly predicts satisfaction and commitment.

That means:

- if you have a strong motive for **leadership, influence, mentoring, building a team**
- then the management opportunity is not just an extrinsic perk
- it may itself be an **intrinsically meaningful** aspect of the role

So the relevant research-based comparison is not simply:

> interesting research vs more money

It may be:

> intellectually interesting individual-contributor work vs less interesting domain but higher fit with a real leadership aspiration

That is a much closer tradeoff.

## 6) What does the research say about promotions / becoming a manager?

The literature here is mixed.

Some findings:

- **Promotions** often increase job satisfaction, at least somewhat.
- But part of that effect comes from **higher pay, status, and autonomy**.
- Promotions can also bring **more stress, longer hours, role ambiguity, and less direct time on the work you actually enjoy**.

For people who genuinely want to lead, this can be worth it. For people who mostly like the idea of leadership but derive most pleasure from deep technical work, management can disappoint.

The research supports the view that **being a manager is not universally better**; it is better mainly when it matches your motives and strengths.

## 7) On “regret”: surprisingly little direct evidence

There is much less direct research on:

> “Do people regret staying in a more interesting job instead of taking a somewhat higher-paying leadership role?”

I wouldn’t claim the literature has a clean answer to that exact question.

What it does support is this indirect conclusion:

- People’s long-run satisfaction tends to depend heavily on **intrinsic job quality** and **fit**
- People often **overweight salary/status** when forecasting happiness
- People often **underestimate adaptation** to extrinsic rewards
- Misfit between role and values is a common source of dissatisfaction

So if you want the most defensible research-based statement, it is:

> **On average, more interesting/meaningful work is associated with greater long-run job satisfaction and well-being than modest differences in pay; however, if the alternative role better fits a strong leadership aspiration, that fit can materially change the equation.**

## 8) The practical research-based takeaway for your exact case

If I translate the evidence into your scenario, without giving a personal opinion:

- If the Google offer is **only a bit higher salary**, and you are already well-compensated, the literature would usually predict that **salary alone should not be the deciding factor**.
- If the Google work is **meaningfully less interesting**, that is a real predicted cost to long-run satisfaction.
- The strongest argument *for* the Google role, from a research perspective, is **not the salary**; it is the possibility that **leading a team fulfills an important intrinsic goal** for you.
- So the key evidence-based question is probably:

> Which is more central to your long-run well-being:  
> **continuing highly interesting research**, or **moving into a role with stronger leadership fit**?

That is exactly the kind of tradeoff where person-job fit research matters most.

---

## If you want concrete papers to look at

A short starter list:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — work design / Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design features
- **Judge, T. A., et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit
- **Allan, B. A., and colleagues** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being
- **Deci & Ryan / self-determination theory**, and work-based reviews of autonomy, competence, relatedness
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and well-being
- **Kosteas (2010)** — promotions and job satisfaction

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-page evidence brief** with actual paper titles and a “what each paper implies for your decision” summary.

answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology and behavioral economics is:

- **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is consistently associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- **Higher pay also helps, but usually less than people expect, especially once compensation is already high enough to remove financial strain.**
- **Direct research on “later regret after choosing the more interesting job vs the better-paid leadership job” is relatively sparse.** Most evidence is indirect.

A good way to think about the literature is that “interesting work” is usually studied under nearby constructs:
- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** like autonomy, skill variety, task significance
- **person–job fit / values fit**

## 1) What the research most strongly says

### A. Job design matters a lot
A foundational result in organizational psychology is that jobs tend to be more satisfying when they have:
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- a sense of **whole, identifiable work**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976), and later meta-analyses broadly support it.

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that work design features such as **autonomy**, **task significance**, and knowledge/skill demands are robustly related to **job satisfaction**, **intrinsic motivation**, and lower withdrawal/turnover outcomes.

So if by “interesting” you mean “the work itself is intellectually engaging and feels important,” research says that tends to matter a lot for happiness at work.

### B. Meaningful work is strongly linked to satisfaction and well-being
A large literature finds that people who experience their work as meaningful report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- greater **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often lower **burnout**, though that relationship can be more complex

A useful summary:
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** meta-analyzed meaningful work and found **moderate positive associations** with job satisfaction and well-being outcomes.

This is one of the clearest findings relevant to your question: on average, people do seem happier when their work feels meaningful/interesting.

### C. Autonomy is especially important
Across many studies, **autonomy** is one of the strongest job-level predictors of well-being.

This also fits **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan): people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that support these needs tend to produce better motivation and well-being than jobs driven mainly by external rewards.

So if the leadership role would give you **more real autonomy and impact**, that matters; it’s not “just title/status.”

### D. Person–job fit and values fit matter
Another strong finding: people are happier when their jobs fit their interests, values, and strengths.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis found that person-job and person-organization fit are meaningfully associated with **job satisfaction**, **commitment**, and lower intent to quit.

This is important for your case because:
- “interesting research” is one kind of fit
- “leading a small team, which I always wanted” is another kind of fit

Research would not treat leadership only as an extrinsic perk. If managing people is a real personal value or aspiration, it can contribute to well-being through competence, agency, and meaning.

---

## 2) How does this compare to salary?

### A. Pay matters, but usually less than non-pay job quality
The research on pay and satisfaction generally finds a **positive but modest** relationship.

A classic meta-analysis:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that:
- once basic needs and comfort are covered,
- differences in **work quality** often explain more of day-to-day job satisfaction than pay differences do.

In other words: a **small-to-moderate salary increase** often does less for happiness than people predict, especially among already highly paid professionals.

### B. Income and happiness: real effect, diminishing marginal value
In broader well-being research:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found that higher income improved life evaluation, while emotional well-being showed diminishing returns.
- Later work by **Killingsworth (2021)** argued that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, though not necessarily linearly.
- A later reconciliation suggested the picture is nuanced rather than a simple plateau/no-plateau story.

But for your decision, the practical takeaway is still:
- **more money helps**
- but **the marginal well-being gain from “a bit higher salary” is often smaller than the gain/loss from job content, autonomy, fit, and meaning**

### C. People adapt somewhat to income gains
There’s also evidence of **adaptation** and **social comparison** in income effects:
- people partly get used to higher income
- satisfaction depends partly on relative comparisons, not just absolute pay

That tends to weaken the long-run happiness boost of a modest salary increase.

---

## 3) Is there evidence people later regret choosing the more interesting work?

This is where the literature is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous line of research specifically asking:
> “Do people later regret choosing a more interesting job over a somewhat higher-paying promotion/leadership role?”

What we do have is indirect evidence:

### Indirect evidence pointing toward “interesting work usually ages well”
People tend to do better long-term when their work provides:
- meaning
- autonomy
- fit with interests/values
- opportunities for growth

Deficits in those areas are associated with:
- burnout
- disengagement
- turnover intentions
- lower satisfaction

So if someone gives up genuinely interesting, meaningful work for a role that is materially better compensated but noticeably less engaging, the literature suggests that this **often does not produce higher happiness**, unless the new role compensates through other important intrinsic factors like:
- autonomy
- leadership/impact
- growth
- identity fit

### But there is an important caution
“Interesting work” is not always pure upside.

There is also research on:
- **calling orientation**
- high identification with work
- overcommitment / exploitation / burnout

People who find work deeply meaningful may also tolerate:
- worse boundaries
- more stress
- more identity entanglement

So the best-supported statement is not “more interesting is always better.” It’s more like:

> On average, meaningful/interesting work predicts higher satisfaction, but only if the role is also sustainable and doesn’t undermine autonomy, health, or other core values.

---

## 4) A subtle point relevant to your case: leadership is not just “less interesting + more money”

Your description has **three** separable variables, not two:

1. **Interestingness of the work itself**
2. **Compensation**
3. **Leadership/management opportunity**

Research would treat #3 as psychologically important in its own right.

If “leading a small team is something you always wanted,” then that is likely not merely an extrinsic reward. It may satisfy:
- autonomy
- competence
- identity
- status/impact motives
- developmental goals

So the real tradeoff may not be:
> interesting work vs money

It may be:
> technical interest vs leadership aspiration

And research on **person–environment fit** suggests that whichever aligns better with your deeper values and self-concept is more likely to support long-term satisfaction.

---

## 5) What the evidence would predict in a case like yours

Very cautiously, the literature would predict:

### If the Google role is:
- only **slightly** higher paid,
- clearly **less interesting**,
- and leadership is more nominal than real,

then the research would **not** expect it to make most people happier than staying in a more interesting job.

### If the Google role offers:
- a real first step into leadership,
- substantially more autonomy and scope,
- strong mentorship/managerial support,
- and leadership is a genuine personal aspiration,

then the well-being difference is much less obvious, because the new role may satisfy another major set of intrinsic needs.

### If your current compensation is already very high and secure
then research generally suggests that **incremental money** should be weighted less than:
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- fit
- growth
- quality of manager/team
- sustainability

---

## 6) The best-supported bottom line

If I strip this down to the most research-grounded answer:

- **Yes, people in more interesting/meaningful/autonomous jobs do tend, on average, to report higher job satisfaction and often better overall well-being.**
- **The effect of pay on satisfaction is real but usually smaller than people expect.**
- **Direct evidence about later regret is limited, but the broader evidence suggests that sacrificing interestingness for a modest pay increase often does not buy much happiness.**
- **However, if the alternative role better fits a major intrinsic aspiration—like leading people—then it may be more satisfying despite being less interesting technically.**

---

## 7) Papers/research lines worth looking at

If you want a reading list, these are good starting points:

### Work design / interesting work
- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.

### Meaningful work
- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010).** On the meaning of work: A theoretical integration and review.
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.

### Autonomy / intrinsic motivation
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** Self-Determination Theory work across many papers/books.
- Related workplace need-satisfaction meta-analyses under SDT are also relevant.

### Person-job fit
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.

### Pay and happiness / pay and job satisfaction
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010).** High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.
- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021).** Experienced well-being rises with income, even above high income levels.

If you want, I can also do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more technical summary with effect sizes and stronger caveats**, or  
2. turn this into an **evidence-based decision checklist** specifically for your situation.
