answer:
Yes—there is a fairly strong research literature on this, and the broad result is:

**On average, “interesting/meaningful/autonomous” work predicts job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than modest differences in pay.**  
That does **not** mean salary and advancement don’t matter. It means that once pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is low, the **quality of the work itself** tends to matter a lot.

A research-based summary relevant to your case:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” usually overlaps with several studied constructs:

- **Intrinsic motivation**
- **Task variety / challenge**
- **Meaningfulness**
- **Autonomy**
- **Skill use and learning**
- **Person-job fit**

These are not vague ideas; they have decades of empirical work behind them.

### Work design meta-analyses
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that job characteristics such as:

- autonomy
- task variety
- task significance
- feedback

are consistently associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher internal motivation
- higher engagement
- lower turnover intentions

Those effects are not tiny. In practice, these features are among the core drivers of whether people actually like their jobs.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to both job and broader life well-being

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. (2019)** found that **meaningful work** is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **meaning in life**

and negatively associated with:

- **burnout**
- **turnover intentions**

So the literature does support the idea that doing work you find genuinely interesting/meaningful is not just pleasant in the moment; it is associated with broader well-being.

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people think for job satisfaction

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively small**.

That result is important for your case because your tradeoff is:

- **current role = more interesting**
- **new role = somewhat more pay + team leadership**

If the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” the literature would generally predict that the pay increase by itself is unlikely to compensate for a meaningful drop in how interesting the work feels day to day.

## 4) Person-job fit is a major predictor too

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- intent to stay

This matters because your decision is not only “interesting work vs money.” It is also:

- **individual contributor in very interesting work**
vs
- **team lead in less interesting work**

If **leading a team is itself deeply important to you**, then that is not merely an “extrinsic” benefit. It may reflect a better fit with your identity, goals, and preferred mode of impact. The research would treat that as important.

## 5) Intrinsic motives generally predict better well-being than extrinsic ones

Research in **self-determination theory** and the **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations** literature (e.g. **Kasser & Ryan**) repeatedly finds that prioritizing intrinsic goals—such as growth, mastery, meaning, contribution, relationships—tends to be associated with better well-being than prioritizing extrinsic goals like money, image, or status.

That does **not** mean “money is bad.” It means that when choices are driven mainly by extrinsic rewards, people often do not get as much durable well-being as they expect.

So if the Google role is attractive mainly because of **salary + title**, the literature would be somewhat skeptical that this will produce lasting happiness.  
If it is attractive because **you genuinely want to lead and develop people**, that is different.

## 6) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base. There is much less direct research on **long-term regret after choosing one career path over another** than there is on job satisfaction and well-being.

But the closest evidence suggests:

- People often **adapt** fairly quickly to pay/status gains.
- They are more persistently affected by the **recurring daily experience** of their work.
- Misalignment between work and intrinsic interests/values tends to produce lasting dissatisfaction.

So while I wouldn’t claim there is a clean “people regret choosing interesting jobs” result, the literature leans the other way:

**A modest pay or status gain tends to be less durable than the ongoing effect of how interesting, meaningful, and fitting the work feels every day.**

## 7) The one big caveat in your case: leadership opportunity is real, not trivial

There is an important caveat: **leadership opportunities can satisfy autonomy, mastery, impact, and identity needs**. For some people, becoming a team lead is not an extrinsic lure; it is a major intrinsic aspiration.

Research on promotions/managerial roles is more mixed:

- promotions often increase satisfaction **temporarily**
- but extra stress, responsibility, and reduced hands-on work can offset the gains
- people who *wanted* leadership tend to benefit more than people who mainly took it for prestige/pay

So the evidence-based interpretation is:

- **Interesting work**: strong positive predictor
- **Modest salary increase**: positive but usually smaller
- **Leadership opportunity**: can be very important if it matches your actual aspirations

## 8) If I compress the literature into one practical sentence

A fair summary would be:

**If two jobs both pay well, the one with more interesting/meaningful work will usually produce higher sustained satisfaction—unless the other role offers a form of growth or leadership that is itself deeply important to the person.**

That “unless” seems very relevant for you.

## 9) If you want the most evidence-based way to think about *your* decision

The research suggests separating the offer into components:

1. **Work itself**  
   How much less interesting is the Google role, really?

2. **Leadership fit**  
   Do you want to lead because you truly want to manage, coach, and own team outcomes?

3. **Autonomy and impact**  
   Will leading a small team give you more actual agency?

4. **Pay difference**  
   Is the salary increase material enough to change your life, or mainly symbolic?

5. **Future option value**

If you want, I can next give you a **more academic version with actual effect sizes and paper-by-paper summaries**.

answer:
Yes. There isn’t one clean paper on your exact tradeoff, but there is a pretty solid research base on the components:

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and well-being research, **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly and more durably than incremental pay increases**.  
**Pay matters**, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high and not a source of stress or perceived unfairness.  
**Promotion/leadership roles are mixed**: they help when they add autonomy, growth, impact, and fit; they help much less when they mostly add stress, politics, or work that you find less engaging.

So if you translate your choice into research language:

- **“Very interesting work”** = usually a strong positive predictor of satisfaction.
- **“A bit higher salary”** = positive, but typically a smaller predictor.
- **“Leading a small team, which I always wanted”** = could matter a lot if leadership itself is intrinsically meaningful to you; less so if it’s mainly status/title.

## What the research says

### 1. Intrinsic job qualities are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
The classic **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses consistently find that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **challenge/complexity**

These are very close to what people usually mean by “interesting work.”

Representative results:
- **Hackman & Oldham (1970s)**: proposed that jobs high in autonomy, variety, significance, etc. increase internal motivation and satisfaction.
- **Fried & Ferris (1987), meta-analysis**: broadly supported the model.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007), meta-analysis**: work design characteristics such as autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback showed substantial positive associations with job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions.

The big pattern: **interesting, well-designed work is not a “nice extra”; it is one of the core drivers of job satisfaction.**

---

### 2. Meaningful work has a strong relationship with satisfaction and well-being
There’s also a separate literature on **meaningful work**. People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- stronger **organizational commitment**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intentions**

Representative result:
- **Allan et al. (2019), meta-analysis on meaningful work**: meaningful work was positively associated with job satisfaction and well-being, and negatively associated with turnover intentions.

So if “OpenAI work is much more interesting” also means “more meaningful / more aligned with what I care about,” that is a substantial factor in the literature.

---

### 3. Pay matters, but less than most people think for job satisfaction
There is a strong tendency to overestimate how much a salary bump will improve how one feels about work.

Representative result:
- **Judge et al. (2010), meta-analysis**: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**. The correlation often cited from that literature is around **.15**—real, but modest.

Interpretation:
- Higher pay is not irrelevant.
- But if you’re already well-paid, **a somewhat higher salary usually does much less for day-to-day satisfaction than better work content, autonomy, fit, or manager quality**.
- **Pay fairness** and **relative pay** often matter more than absolute pay.

Related broader well-being research:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that money strongly improves **life evaluation**, but had more limited effects on **daily emotional well-being** beyond a point.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found well-being continues to rise with income on average, but the gains are still typically **diminishing**, not linear in felt impact.

So the literature does **not** say money stops mattering. It says **incremental money usually has smaller subjective effects than people forecast**, especially at already-high income levels.

---

### 4. Intrinsic motives are generally better for well-being than extrinsic motives
A large literature from **Self-Determination Theory** finds that people do better when their work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence/mastery**
- **relatedness**

And when motivation is more **intrinsic** (“this work is interesting / meaningful / growthful”) rather than purely **extrinsic** (“higher pay / title / prestige”).

Representative sources:
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**: review of self-determination theory in work settings.
- Broader SDT research consistently finds that **autonomous motivation** predicts higher well-being, better performance, and less burnout than controlled/extrinsic motivation.

That’s important here because your choice is not simply **interesting work vs money**.  
It may be **one intrinsic pull vs another**:

- interesting research/technical work
- leading people and building a team

If leading a team is something you genuinely care about, that may also be an **intrinsic** driver, not just status.

---

### 5. Promotions and leadership roles do not reliably produce large lasting happiness gains
The evidence here is more mixed.

Longitudinal panel studies often find that promotions bring:

- more pay
- more status
- sometimes a short-term boost in satisfaction

But those gains are often partly offset by:

- longer hours
- more stress
- more responsibility
- role conflict / politics

In plain terms: **“I got promoted” is not automatically a durable happiness win.**

A good summary of the research pattern is:
- leadership helps when it increases **autonomy, impact, mastery, and fit**
- it helps less, or can even hurt, when it mainly increases **administrative burden and stress**

So the leadership part of the Google offer could matter a lot, but not because leadership is universally better. It matters **if managing people is work you actually find energizing**.

---

### 6. Person-job fit is one of the strongest moderators
A very robust finding in organizational psychology is that **fit matters**.

Representative result:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005), meta-analysis**: person-job and person-organization fit were strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions.

This means averages like “interesting work beats money” are useful, but **the most important moderator is whether the job matches your motives and strengths**.

For example:
- If you deeply value technical depth, exploration, and research novelty, the “interesting work” side should get extra weight.
- If you deeply value building teams, mentoring, and having broader leverage through others, the “lead a small team” side should get extra weight.

Research would describe that not as “following your gut,” but as **matching the role to your intrinsic motives and fit profile**.

---

### 7. Job satisfaction spills over into overall life satisfaction
This matters because the choice is not just about work.

There is a substantial literature showing that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are meaningfully related**. The relationship is not perfect, but it is strong enough that a better daily work experience often affects general well-being.

So if one role is much more engaging for 40–60 hours/week, that usually matters more than many people’s decision models acknowledge.

---

## What about regret specifically?

There is **less direct research** on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying less interesting one?”

So here I’d separate two things:

### What we know reasonably well
- More **interesting/meaningful/autonomous** work is associated with higher satisfaction.
- Higher **pay** has a smaller average effect.
- **Leadership** helps mainly when it fits your motivations.
- Poor fit predicts dissatisfaction and turnover.

### What we know less well
- Direct ex post regret studies on exactly this tradeoff are limited.

There is one broader, relevant line of research:
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994/1995)** found that, over the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.

That does **not** directly answer your question, but it is relevant if “leading a small team” feels like a meaningful aspiration you might otherwise postpone indefinitely. I would treat this as **suggestive**, not decisive.

---

## The most defensible takeaway from the literature

If I compress the research into one sentence:

> For people who are already financially comfortable, long-run job satisfaction is usually driven more by intrinsic job quality—interesting work, meaning, autonomy, learning, and fit—than by modest salary increases or title/status alone.

A slightly more nuanced version for your case:

> The strongest research case for taking Google is not the higher salary; it is the possibility that leading a team is itself a meaningful, intrinsically rewarding next step for you. If the work is truly “much less interesting,” the literature would predict that this will matter a lot unless the management aspect strongly compensates for it.

## Useful references to start with

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980) — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R.** (1987) — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007) — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010) — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** (2019) — meta-analysis of meaningful work outcomes  
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L.** (2005) — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005) — meta-analysis of fit at work  
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A.** (2010) — income and well-being  
- **Killingsworth, M. A.** (2021) — experienced well-being and income  
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** (1994/1995) — action vs inaction regret

If you want, I can do one of two more research-oriented follow-ups:

1. give you a **more formal annotated bibliography** with the main findings of each paper, or  
2. turn this literature into a **decision framework** that uses only evidence-backed factors: interestingness, autonomy, meaning, pay, leadership fit, stress, and likely regret.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research on this, though researchers usually don’t study “interesting work” under that exact label. They break it into things like:

- **intrinsic motivation** (“I find the work itself engaging”)
- **job characteristics** (autonomy, variety, challenge, significance, feedback)
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit** / **person-organization fit**
- **pay and promotion**

## Short version

**Across organizational psychology research, people tend to be happier and more satisfied in jobs that are intrinsically motivating, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit for their interests and values.**  
**Higher pay helps, but its relationship to job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect—especially once basic financial needs are already comfortably met.**

So if your choice is roughly:

- **more interesting work / better fit**
vs
- **somewhat higher pay / title / management opportunity**

the evidence generally says:

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is a strong predictor of sustained job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but usually modestly unless the difference meaningfully changes your life or reduces financial stress.**
3. **Leadership can improve satisfaction if it fulfills an intrinsic goal of yours** (autonomy, impact, status, growth), but it can also add stress and administrative burden.

So the research answer is not “always choose interest,” but rather:

> If both jobs pay well, the better predictor of long-run happiness is usually the one with stronger intrinsic motivation, meaning, autonomy, and fit—not the one with the slightly higher salary alone.

---

## What the evidence says

### 1) Job design matters a lot

A major line of research comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses of work design.

**Core finding:** jobs with more:
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn and use abilities**

are associated with higher:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- commitment

and lower:
- burnout
- turnover intentions

A very widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features*.

Their overall conclusion was that work design features have **substantial associations** with satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and withdrawal/turnover outcomes.

So if “interesting” means **technically engaging, challenging, varied, meaningful, and autonomous**, that is exactly the sort of thing that predicts better work well-being.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

Another robust finding: people who see their work as meaningful report higher well-being.

A good source is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on **meaningful work**

Findings generally show that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- greater life meaning
- lower turnover intentions

Meaningfulness is not the same as “fun” or “interesting,” but they often overlap in research and in real life.

---

### 3) Person-job fit is important

If a job fits your interests, strengths, and values, people tend to do better in it psychologically.

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-environment fit**

They found that fit is related to:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit
- better performance

This is directly relevant to your case: if OpenAI work feels much more intellectually aligned with what you care about, that is not a trivial preference—it is a variable that reliably predicts satisfaction.

---

### 4) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic motivation for well-being

From **Self-Determination Theory**:

People tend to function better when work satisfies needs for:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

and when motivation is more **autonomous/intrinsic** (“I want to do this”) rather than mainly **controlled/extrinsic** (“I’m doing it for pay/status/pressure”).

Good reviews include:
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** on self-determination theory at work
- later reviews/meta-analytic work in the SDT literature

The broad pattern is very stable:
- more autonomous motivation → more satisfaction, persistence, performance, lower burnout
- more controlled motivation → weaker well-being, often more strain

This does **not** mean pay and promotion are bad. It means they are usually **less powerful as drivers of ongoing well-being** than work that is genuinely engaging and self-concordant.

---

### 5) Pay matters, but less than many people think

One especially useful result for your question:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** conducted a meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**.

Their conclusion: the relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but modest**.  
The commonly cited effect is around **r ≈ .15**—real, but not large.

That’s an important result. It means:
- salary increases generally help
- but salary alone is usually **not a strong predictor** of whether you’ll actually like your job

This is especially true when comparing **two already high-paying jobs**, rather than comparing financial insecurity vs security.

---

### 6) Income and happiness: useful but nuanced

Outside job research, the income/well-being literature says:

- more income generally improves **life evaluation**
- it helps most when it reduces **financial stress**
- the gains often become smaller or more nuanced at higher incomes

Relevant papers:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)**

There is some debate about whether there is a hard “plateau,” but the broad takeaway remains:

> Income matters a lot when it changes material security; less so when choosing between two already comfortable outcomes.

For someone deciding between OpenAI and Google at research-engineer compensation levels, the question is usually less “Will more money make me happier?” and more:

- Will the extra money materially change my life?
- Or is the bigger long-run driver the daily experience of the work?

Research usually points to the **daily work experience**.

---

## What about leadership? That’s the big complication here.

Your situation is not just “interesting work vs money.”

It’s more like:

- **OpenAI** = more interesting work
- **Google** = somewhat more pay + chance to lead a team, which is itself a long-held goal

That matters because **leading a team can itself satisfy intrinsic needs**:
- autonomy
- impact
- growth
- status
- mentoring
- mastery of a new domain

So from a research perspective, leadership is **not just an extrinsic perk**. If you genuinely want to lead, then the Google role may have its own intrinsic value.

But research on promotions/management roles is mixed:
- people often like the status and growth
- but management also brings more stress, meetings, administrative load, and role conflict
- the well-being benefit depends heavily on whether the person actually wants the role and whether the organization supports them in it

So the evidence would suggest:
- if you truly want leadership for its own sake, that is a meaningful positive
- if you mostly want it as a résumé line or prestige marker, it may not offset doing less interesting work every day

---

## Do people regret staying in the more interesting job?

This is the part where the evidence is weaker.

There is **much stronger research on satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover** than on “career regret” specifically.

### What we can say with confidence:
People are less likely to want to leave, and more likely to be satisfied, when they have:
- interesting work
- meaning
- autonomy
- good fit

### What we cannot say as confidently:
There is no single clean body of evidence showing:
- “people who chose interesting jobs later regretted it less than people who chose higher-paying jobs”

That exact comparison is not well studied.

### Related regret research
Broader regret literature suggests that over time people often regret choices that conflict with their values, interests, or ideal selves more than they regret small financial misses. But that literature is broader and less directly job-specific, so I’d treat it as suggestive, not decisive.

So the clean research-based answer is:

> There is strong evidence that more interesting/meaningful/fitting work predicts more satisfaction and less desire to leave.  
> There is weaker direct evidence on “regret,” but what exists generally points in the same direction.

---

## If you want the most research-grounded way to compare the roles

Instead of asking “interesting vs salary,” compare them on the variables that actually predict well-being:

### For each role, rate:
1. **Autonomy**  
   How much control will I have over what I do and how I do it?

2. **Competence / growth**  
   Will I keep learning? Will I feel stretched in a good way?

3. **Meaning / significance**  
   Does this work feel important to me?

4. **Person-job fit**  
   Does this match my genuine interests?

5. **Person-organization fit**  
   Do I want to be in this culture?

6. **Relationships / manager quality**  
   A huge predictor of satisfaction in practice.

7. **Leadership fit**  
   Do I actually want to manage now, or do I like the idea of it?

8. **Stress / administrative load**  
   What will the day-to-day feel like?

9. **Compensation / fairness**  
   Is the pay difference life-changing, or just objectively nice?

From the literature, those first 7–8 variables usually tell you more about future happiness than “which one pays a bit more?”

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

> **People are usually happier in jobs that are interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and aligned with their interests and values; pay helps, but modestly, especially once compensation is already high.**

For your case, the research would suggest:

- **If the Google role is mostly “more money + less interesting work,” OpenAI is more likely to produce higher day-to-day satisfaction.**
- **If the Google role gives you a genuinely desired leadership experience that you find meaningful, then it may compete more strongly, because leadership itself can be intrinsically rewarding.**

So the research-based question is probably not:

- “interest vs money?”

but:

- **“Which role gives me the better mix of intrinsic motivation, fit, autonomy, meaning, and growth?”**

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **short annotated reading list** of the strongest papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **decision worksheet** based directly on these research constructs.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, and the broad picture is fairly consistent:

## Short answer

**On average, people are happier and more satisfied in jobs that are intrinsically rewarding**—interesting, meaningful, challenging, autonomous, and growth-promoting—**than in jobs that mainly improve extrinsic outcomes like pay or status**.

That does **not** mean salary and promotion don’t matter. They do. But in the literature, **pay tends to have a smaller association with job satisfaction and well-being than intrinsic job characteristics do**, especially once income is already high enough that basic security and comfort aren’t in question.

On **regret specifically**, the evidence is thinner. There is much better evidence on **job satisfaction, well-being, engagement, burnout, and turnover** than on long-run regret about choosing a more interesting vs. higher-paying role.

---

## What the research says

### 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology points to the importance of **intrinsic job characteristics**:

- **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:
  - **skill variety**
  - **task identity**
  - **task significance**
  - **autonomy**
  - **feedback**

- A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these kinds of work-design features are **reliably associated with job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower strain/burnout**.

In plain English:  
If a job is more interesting, gives more autonomy, lets you use more of your abilities, feels important, and helps you learn, people usually like it more and do better in it.

**This is one of the most robust findings in work psychology.**

---

### 2. Pay matters—but usually less than people expect for happiness at work

There is also meta-analytic work on the relationship between **pay and job satisfaction**.

- A widely cited meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively small**.
- By contrast, intrinsic aspects of work—like autonomy, meaningfulness, and fit—typically show **larger associations** with satisfaction.

So the research does **not** say “money doesn’t matter.” It says something more like:

> Money matters a lot when it affects security, fairness, or major life constraints;  
> but once those are reasonably covered, **the day-to-day quality of the work often matters more for sustained satisfaction**.

That pattern also fits broader subjective well-being research: income helps, but with **diminishing marginal returns**, and work conditions still matter a lot.

---

### 3. Intrinsic motives are usually better for well-being than extrinsic motives

A related literature compares **intrinsic** vs **extrinsic** aspirations and motives.

- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) and related work finds that people tend to have better well-being when their activities support:
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**

- Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and others shows that people who prioritize **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) tend to report **better well-being** than those who prioritize **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image) more heavily.

Applied to jobs:  
A role chosen primarily because it is more interesting, growth-oriented, or meaningful is, on average, more likely to support well-being than one chosen mainly for compensation or prestige.

---

### 4. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work finds that perceiving work as meaningful is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- greater **engagement**
- lower **burnout**
- better **general mental well-being**

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in practice they overlap a lot—especially for highly skilled knowledge workers.

For people in research or engineering, “interesting work” often bundles together:
- intellectual stimulation
- learning
- a sense of progress
- agency
- perceived importance
- identity fit

Those are all things the literature tends to treat as psychologically valuable.

---

### 5. Person-job fit matters a lot

Another strong finding: **fit** matters.

Meta-analyses on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** show that when people feel their role matches their interests, values, and abilities, they generally show:

- higher satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- better well-being

This matters for your case because **leading a team** may be a real source of fit for you if it is something you’ve wanted for a long time.

But there’s an important nuance:

> A management role is not just “the same job plus leadership.”  
> It is often a different job.

If what you want is:
- coaching
- setting direction
- building a team
- making people around you better

then leadership may be intrinsically rewarding.

If what you want is actually:
- deep technical work
- being close to research
- solving hard problems yourself

then management can reduce the very features that made the job interesting.

The literature would say: **the key is fit with the actual day-to-day work**, not the title.

---

## What about regret?

This is where the evidence gets less direct.

### What we know reasonably well
Research is strong on:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover
- well-being

### What we know less well
Research is weaker on:
- “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job?”
- “Do people regret passing up management?”
- “Do they regret staying in a more interesting but lower-paid role?”

There are a few relevant adjacent findings:

#### A. People often overweight salient extrinsic features
Behavioral research on decision-making suggests people can **overweight easily comparable features** like salary, title, and prestige, while **underweight day-to-day experience**.

That matters because your happiness comes from what you do every day, not from the fact that one offer had a cleaner number attached to it.

#### B. Long-run regret often centers on missed identity-relevant opportunities
Research on regret (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) suggests that over the long run, people often regret **missed opportunities** more than actions taken.  
But this is broad psychological research, not specific career evidence, so I would treat it as suggestive rather than decisive.

#### C. Self-concordant choices tend to feel better later
Research on **self-concordance** (e.g. **Sheldon & Elliot**) suggests people feel better about goals and choices that reflect their authentic interests and values, rather than external pressures.

So if “leading a team” is a deeply self-concordant goal for you, that matters.  
If it is mostly attractive because it looks like progress, that is a different case.

---

## A useful way to interpret the literature for your specific situation

If I translate the research into a decision rule, it would be something like:

### A modest pay increase usually does **not** compensate for a large drop in intrinsic job quality
Especially when:
- current pay is already high
- the work is genuinely engaging
- the less-interesting role would change your day-to-day experience substantially

### But a leadership opportunity can be worth a lot if it fulfills intrinsic needs too
That is, if it increases:
- autonomy
- responsibility
- growth
- mentoring
- influence
- identity fit

then it is **not merely an extrinsic benefit**. It becomes part of the intrinsic value of the job.

So from a research perspective, the decision is not really:

> interesting work vs more money

It is more like:

> **more intellectually interesting IC work**  
> versus  
> **less interesting domain, but potentially more autonomy/impact/growth through leadership**

Those are both potentially intrinsic goods. The question is which one dominates in your actual day-to-day psychology.

---

## What the best evidence would suggest you weigh most heavily

If you want to stay close to the literature, the most predictive factors for future satisfaction are likely:

1. **Day-to-day task enjoyment**
   - How many hours per week will be genuinely engaging?

2. **Autonomy**
   - How much control will you have over what you do and how you do it?

3. **Growth / competence**
   - Which role will make you better in ways you actually care about?

4. **Meaning / significance**
   - Which role feels more worth doing?

5. **Fit**
   - Are you actually excited about management work, or about the idea of having been promoted into management?

6. **Relationships / team quality**
   - This is often underrated in decisions but strongly predictive of satisfaction.

7. **Pay and fairness**
   - Is the difference large enough to materially change your life, or just incrementally pleasant?

The literature would generally put **salary below the first several items**, unless the money difference is large enough to alter security, lifestyle, family options, or sense of fairness.

---

## Bottom line from the research

If your current job is genuinely much more interesting, and the Google role is only “a bit higher salary” but substantially less interesting, then **the literature leans toward the more interesting work being better for sustained job satisfaction and well-being**.

The main reason the Google option could still dominate on well-being is **not the extra salary**; it’s if the **leadership component** is itself a major source of intrinsic value and fit for you.

So the research-based summary is:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work predicts happiness strongly.**
- **Money predicts job satisfaction, but usually less strongly.**
- **Leadership can increase satisfaction if it matches your true motives and preferred daily activities.**
- **There is much better evidence on satisfaction than on later regret.**
- **People often underweight day-to-day experience and overweight salary/title when making choices.**

---

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **short reading list** of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **decision checklist based directly on the literature** rather than generic career advice.

answer:
Yes—there is a fair amount of research here, though not many studies that ask your exact question in the form “interesting work at firm A vs somewhat higher pay + team lead at firm B.”

The short version from the literature is:

1. **“Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but its relationship to overall job satisfaction is much weaker than most people expect**, especially once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the main issue.
3. **Leadership/promotion can help, but the evidence is mixed** because it often brings more autonomy/status *and* more stress/responsibility.
4. **Fit matters a lot**: if “leading a small team” is a genuine long-term aspiration for you, that can materially increase satisfaction even if the subject matter is less interesting.

So if you want the research-based answer in one sentence:  
**On average, people are happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting/meaningful/autonomous than in jobs that are merely better paid, but a role that better fits an important personal motive—like wanting to lead—can offset that.**

## The main research findings

### 1) Work design / interesting work is strongly tied to satisfaction

A classic line of organizational psychology research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later backed by meta-analyses. The key job features are:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

Jobs high on these dimensions tend to produce more:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job satisfaction**
- **engagement**
- often better performance

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Found that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback were **substantially associated with job satisfaction and internal work motivation**.

This is probably the closest research tradition to your “interesting work” question.

### 2) Pay has a positive but surprisingly small relationship with job satisfaction

The strongest summary result here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  Meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**. The correlation between **pay level and overall job satisfaction** was **positive but modest** (commonly summarized as around **0.15**).

That does **not** mean money doesn’t matter. It means:
- money matters,
- but it explains only a relatively small portion of why people like their jobs.

This is especially relevant when comparing **two already-elite jobs** rather than “secure livelihood” vs “financial precarity.”

So the research would generally predict that a **modest salary increase** is less important for long-term day-to-day job satisfaction than whether the work is engaging, meaningful, and fits you.

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful summary:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on **meaningful work**  
  Meaningful work is positively associated with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **commitment**
  
  and negatively associated with:
  - **turnover intentions**
  - some indicators of distress/burnout

This matters because “interesting” is often partly a proxy for **meaningful**, especially in research or technical work.

### 4) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another major line of work says that what matters is not only the job itself, but the match between the job and the person.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis showing **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are linked to higher:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - performance  
  and lower:
  - intention to quit

This is especially relevant to your case because there are **two different kinds of fit** at issue:

- fit with your **intellectual interests**  
- fit with your **leadership aspiration**

The literature would say both matter.

So “less interesting technically, but finally getting to lead a team” is not necessarily a worse fit overall. It depends on which aspect is more central to your long-run motivation.

### 5) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

A lot of work psychology now converges on **Self-Determination Theory**:
people do better when work satisfies needs for

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

This literature consistently finds that these needs predict:
- well-being
- engagement
- persistence
- lower burnout

Leadership roles sometimes increase:
- autonomy
- competence
- status/impact
- relatedness

But they may also reduce the parts of work that currently satisfy competence or intrinsic curiosity, especially if management displaces hands-on research.

So the research-based question is not only “interesting or not?” but:

- Will the Google role increase or decrease your **autonomy**?
- Will it increase or decrease your felt **competence/mastery**?
- Will it improve **relatedness** through leading and mentoring?
- Will it reduce the intrinsic satisfaction you get from the actual substance of the work?

### 6) Promotions and leadership do not reliably make people happier in a large, lasting way

The evidence on **promotion/leadership** is more mixed than many people assume.

Broadly:
- promotions can raise satisfaction **temporarily**
- they also tend to increase:
  - stress
  - responsibility
  - time pressure
  - role conflict

So “leading a team” is not automatically a happiness upgrade.

The research-based view is closer to:
- leadership improves satisfaction **if** the person actually wants leadership and the role is designed well,
- but it can lower satisfaction if it mainly means meetings, politics, coordination load, and less interesting core work.

That is, leadership is not a universal good; it is a **fit variable**.

## On regret specifically

Here the evidence is much thinner.

I do **not** know of strong research that directly tracks people who chose:
- more interesting work  
versus
- more pay/title/management

and then measures long-term regret in a clean causal way.

What we do know indirectly:

- People often **adapt** to higher pay/status faster than they expect.
- Daily experience of work tends to matter a lot for ongoing well-being.
- Poor fit and lack of meaning/autonomy are common sources of dissatisfaction and turnover.

So the evidence is stronger for **happiness/satisfaction** than for **regret**.

If I had to summarize the research cautiously:
- there is **good evidence** that more interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts higher job satisfaction;
- there is **much weaker evidence** on whether people later *regret* choosing it over pay/promotion.

## What the literature would imply for a case like yours

Without giving a personal opinion, the evidence points to a few fairly strong inferences:

### If the salary difference is only “a bit higher”
Research suggests the salary bump is unlikely to dominate long-term satisfaction, especially if both jobs already provide high income/security.

### If the current OpenAI work is much more intrinsically interesting
The literature would predict that this matters a lot for day-to-day satisfaction and engagement.

### If leading a team is a major personal goal
That is not “just extrinsic.” It’s part of **person-job fit**, identity, and growth. If leadership is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, the Google role may satisfy an important psychological need that the current role does not.

### The key empirical tradeoff is probably not “interest vs money”
It is more likely:

**intrinsic interest / meaning / challenge**  
versus  
**growth into leadership / autonomy / influence / identity fit**

Research says both are important; **pay is probably the least important of the three**, unless the difference is very large or changes your life materially.

## If you want a compact ranking from the literature

For someone already in a high-paying, high-skill career, the factors most consistently linked to long-term satisfaction are roughly:

1. **Interesting/challenging/meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Fit with values and long-term identity**
4. **Manager/team quality**
5. **Workload and burnout risk**
6. **Growth opportunities, including leadership if you value it**
7. **Compensation**, unless differences are very large

That ranking is a simplification, but it’s much closer to the literature than “follow the higher salary.”

## Papers / sources worth starting with

- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
  *Journal of Applied Psychology.*

- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
  *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
  *Journal of Vocational Behavior.*

- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
  *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
  *Personnel Psychology.*

- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
  Meta-analytic work on **meaningful work** and outcomes.

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.**  
  Foundational work on the **Job Characteristics Model**.

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more formal literature summary** with effect sizes and better-cited references, or  
2. turn this into a **research-based decision framework** specifically for your situation, without adding personal opinion.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad pattern is fairly consistent:

**More intrinsically rewarding work** — work that is interesting, challenging, meaningful, and gives autonomy — is usually a **stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction and well-being** than higher pay alone.

But there are two important nuances:

1. **“Interesting” is not one single variable** in the literature. It is usually studied through things like:
   - intrinsic motivation
   - task variety/challenge
   - autonomy
   - skill use and growth
   - meaningfulness
   - person–job fit

2. In your case, **leading a team may itself be intrinsically rewarding**, not just an extrinsic perk. So the real comparison may not be “interesting vs salary,” but “domain-interest vs leadership-growth.”

## What the research says

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), which argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

This has held up reasonably well over time. A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson (2007)** found that work design features like autonomy, variety, and meaningfulness are strongly associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher internal work motivation
- greater engagement
- lower turnover intentions
- less burnout

So if by “interesting” you mean “I am intellectually engaged, learning, solving important problems, and care about the work,” research says that matters a lot.

---

### 2. Meaningful/interesting work tends to matter more for happiness than salary does
Another big stream of research studies **meaningful work**. Across studies, people who experience their work as meaningful report:
- higher job satisfaction
- better overall well-being
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to quit

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** and related researchers generally finds that meaningful work is one of the stronger correlates of positive work outcomes.

This doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It means that once compensation is “good enough,” differences in meaning/interest often explain more of the difference in whether people actually like their jobs.

---

### 3. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
A very influential framework here is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan).

It says people do better psychologically when work supports three basic needs:
- **autonomy**: I have real choice/control
- **competence**: I’m growing and getting good at something hard
- **relatedness**: I feel connected to others / part of something

Interesting work often hits autonomy + competence very hard. Leadership can also satisfy these, but sometimes it does the opposite if it adds bureaucracy, politics, or constant people-management without real control.

This is one reason “better title + more pay” does not automatically produce more happiness.

---

### 4. Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is modest
There’s a lot of evidence that **pay has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction**, especially at higher income levels.

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich (2010)** found that the relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but not large**. That’s been a very robust result in the field: more money helps, but not nearly as much as many people expect.

Related work on income and well-being more broadly (for example **Kahneman & Deaton**, and later **Killingsworth**) suggests:
- money matters a lot when it reduces financial stress / improves security
- after that, marginal gains matter less
- relative comparisons and fairness often matter more than raw pay

So if Google’s offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the literature would usually not expect that alone to produce a large increase in long-run happiness.

---

### 5. Promotions and leadership roles are more mixed than people assume
Research on promotions/managerial roles tends to show **mixed effects**.

They can increase:
- status
- pay
- sense of progress
- influence
- autonomy

But they can also increase:
- stress
- responsibility load
- role conflict
- time fragmentation
- political demands
- emotional labor

So leadership often improves satisfaction **for people who genuinely value leading and developing others**, and less so for people who mainly want to stay close to the craft itself.

That means the key research question is not “Is leadership better?” but:

**Do you personally derive intrinsic satisfaction from leadership itself?**

If yes, then the Google role may not be “less interesting” in the psychologically relevant sense. It may be lower in *technical/research interest* but higher in *growth, identity, and meaning*.

---

### 6. Person–job fit is one of the best predictors of satisfaction
A very important result from organizational psychology is that **fit matters**.

Meta-analytic work by **Kristof-Brown and colleagues** found that forms of fit — especially **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** — are linked to:
- higher job satisfaction
- better commitment
- lower quitting intentions

Translation: there is no universal rule that “interesting work beats everything” or “leadership beats everything.”

What predicts happiness best is whether the job matches **what you value most**:
- intellectual novelty?
- mission?
- autonomy?
- leadership?
- prestige?
- financial upside?
- work-life balance?

People tend to do worse when they choose based on factors they *think they should value* rather than the ones they actually do value.

---

## What about regret specifically?

Here the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research directly showing:
> “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paid leadership role regretted it less 10 years later.”

That exact question is not especially well studied.

But there are two relevant literatures:

### A. Value-congruence / fit research
People are less likely to want to leave and more likely to be satisfied when their work matches their underlying values. That suggests **regret is less likely when the choice reflects your real priorities**.

### B. Regret research more broadly
A classic finding from **Gilovich & Medvec** is that, over time, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.

That is only indirectly relevant here, but it does suggest something important:
- if “leading a team” is a deep aspiration you’ve had for years,
- then passing on it could become a source of regret,
- even if staying in the interesting role gives more day-to-day enjoyment now.

This is not evidence that you should take Google. It’s evidence that **untried aspirations can loom large in hindsight**.

---

## So, what does the research most strongly support?

If I compress the literature into a few evidence-based takeaways:

### Strongly supported
- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work predicts higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay predicts satisfaction, but much less strongly than many people assume.**
- **Fit between the role and your values is crucial.**

### Moderately supported
- **Leadership is satisfying when it is itself intrinsically meaningful to the person**, not just a prestige marker.
- **A modest pay increase usually does not compensate for a big drop in interest/meaning**, unless the money materially changes life circumstances.

### Less directly studied
- Long-term **regret** from choosing interesting work vs higher-paid leadership.

---

## Applied to your situation, in research terms

The literature would probably frame your decision like this:

1. **How large is the drop in intrinsic interest?**  
   If the Google job is substantially less engaging/intellectually meaningful, that is a real negative, and research says it matters a lot.

2. **Is leadership one of your core intrinsic values?**  
   If you “always wanted to lead a small team,” that may be a major positive — not an extrinsic one.

3. **Is the salary increase meaningful or merely nice?**  
   If it’s just somewhat higher, research would usually predict only a small well-being benefit from that component.

4. **Could you get the leadership growth without losing the interesting work?**  
   From a research standpoint, the best outcome is often preserving intrinsic interest while adding growth/autonomy/leadership.

---

## The closest thing to a research-based bottom line

If the pay difference is modest and the work itself is much less interesting, the evidence generally leans toward:

**people are more satisfied in the role with stronger intrinsic motivation, meaning, and fit.**

But if leading a team is a major personal aspiration — something central to how you want to grow — then the Google option may actually be higher on another important intrinsic dimension, and the decision becomes much less one-sided.

So the research does **not** say:
- “always choose the more interesting technical work,” or
- “always choose the promotion.”

It says:

> Long-run satisfaction is usually highest when the job maximizes intrinsic motivation and value fit; money helps, but often less than people predict.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature** — e.g. weighting interest, autonomy, meaning, leadership growth, pay, and regret risk in a more structured way.

I can summarize the literature with some important notes. If the salary difference is modest and both jobs meet financial needs, intrinsic qualities are more crucial for well-being than a bit of extra pay. The research on direct regret is limited, but jobs that focus on interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit typically lead to greater satisfaction and lower turnover. Leadership roles can add complexity as they are often rewarding. It's worth considering that interesting work may relate to lower burnout, though meaningful work could lead to overwork. Citing “Humphrey 2007” alongside broader Job Characteristics Model findings will strengthen this analysis.
**Discussing job and career regret research**

There seems to be some research on job and career regret, especially regarding entrepreneurs, but it's quite scarce. It's important to acknowledge that direct regret evidence isn't well-established, which is acceptable. If the user wants more details, I can offer full citations. I could list some relevant studies or names for reference and mention that I can pull actual papers if needed, although I can't browse right now. I should focus on being concise yet informative while including references.
answer:
Yes — there is a fairly robust literature suggesting that what people usually mean by **“interesting work”** tends to matter a lot for well-being at work, often **more than modest differences in pay**.

The main caveat is that research usually does **not** measure “interestingness” as a single variable. It breaks it into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **learning and growth**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **fit with one’s interests and values**

Across those literatures, the pattern is pretty consistent.

## Short answer

- **More interesting / intrinsically rewarding work is usually associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly modest**, especially once pay is already good enough and perceived as fair.
- There is **less direct research on “regret” after choosing the more interesting job**, but longitudinal and value-fit research generally suggests people do better when their jobs satisfy intrinsic motives rather than when they optimize mainly for extrinsic rewards.
- In your case, **leading a small team** is important because research would not treat that as “just extrinsic.” It may satisfy autonomy, competence, impact, and identity goals — so this is not simply “interesting work vs money.”

## What the research says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of job satisfaction
A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later followed by meta-analyses.

The broad finding: jobs high in
- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- and meaningfulness

tend to produce **higher motivation and job satisfaction**.

A key meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Result: motivational job characteristics were consistently linked to outcomes like **job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement**, and lower withdrawal.

The practical implication is that “interesting work” is not fluffy; in organizational psychology it shows up as one of the more important predictors of day-to-day work experience.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly related to satisfaction and well-being
A closely related literature studies **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Meaningful work is positively associated with **job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction**, and negatively associated with **turnover intentions**.

So if by “interesting” you partly mean “this work genuinely matters to me” or “I care about the problems,” that tends to be a real contributor to well-being, not just a nice-to-have.

---

### 3) Autonomy, competence, and growth matter a lot
Under **Self-Determination Theory**, people tend to function better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Research in this tradition consistently finds that jobs satisfying these needs are associated with better **motivation, vitality, engagement, and well-being**.

This matters for your case because:
- staying in a deeply interesting research role may strongly support **competence** and **autonomy**
- moving into a team-lead role may support **autonomy, competence, relatedness, and impact**

So the leadership piece is psychologically substantive; it is not merely résumé polish.

---

### 4) Pay matters less for job satisfaction than most people think
One of the cleanest findings in this area:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
  The relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but modest**.

In plain English: people are generally **less satisfied by extra salary than they expect**, especially compared with features of the work itself.

That does **not** mean salary is irrelevant. It matters more when:
- the difference is large,
- one option changes financial security,
- compensation affects perceived fairness/status,
- or the current pay feels below market.

But for a **“bit higher salary”** move between already high-paying jobs, the literature would usually predict a **smaller well-being effect from pay** than from changes in day-to-day work quality.

---

### 5) Person-job fit is a major factor
Another very relevant literature is **person-job fit / person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  Better fit is associated with **higher job satisfaction, stronger commitment, better performance**, and **lower intention to quit**.

This is one of the most useful findings for your specific decision, because the question is not only:
> “Which job is objectively better?”

but:
> “Which job fits my motives and identity better?”

If you have a long-standing goal of leading a team, then a management-leaning role may fit an important part of your identity even if the technical content is less exciting. Research says that kind of fit matters a lot.

---

### 6) Intrinsic goals tend to beat extrinsic goals for well-being
There is also broader research on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations**.

- **Kasser & Ryan** (1990s, several studies)  
  People who center their lives more on extrinsic goals such as money/image/status tend, on average, to report **lower well-being** than those oriented more toward intrinsic goals like growth, relationships, and contribution.

This does **not** mean “money bad.”  
It means that **optimizing mainly for external rewards often does not produce the best well-being outcomes**.

Applied to your case:
- **higher salary** is more extrinsic
- **interesting research** is more intrinsic
- **leading a team** could be either, depending on why you want it  
  - if for status, more extrinsic  
  - if for growth, mentorship, impact, building something — more intrinsic

That distinction matters.

## What about regret specifically?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of strong, clean studies that do exactly this:
- track people choosing between a more interesting job and a better-paid but less interesting job,
- then measure long-term regret.

What we do have is indirect evidence:

1. **People in intrinsically rewarding, meaningful, high-fit jobs tend to be more satisfied.**
2. **Extrinsic rewards like pay have smaller effects than people often predict.**
3. **Value congruence predicts less turnover and better attitudes.**

So the literature leans toward:
- people are **more likely to thrive** in work that remains interesting/meaningful and fits their values,
- unless the alternative offers other psychologically important benefits, like **autonomy, growth, impact, or fit**.

That last exception is important because your Google offer includes **leading a small team**, which may be one of those benefits.

## A subtle but important point

From a research perspective, your tradeoff is probably **not**:

- interesting work vs money

It is more like:

- **deeply interesting individual-contributor research**
vs
- **less interesting technical content, but more leadership/impact/growth/identity fit**, plus somewhat higher pay

Those are different bundles of intrinsic rewards.

If leading people is something you’ve “always wanted,” then the Google role may satisfy a different intrinsic motive rather than merely offering more money.

## If you want the most research-grounded way to think about it

The evidence suggests you should compare the roles on at least these dimensions:

1. **Interest / intellectual stimulation**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Learning and growth**
4. **Meaning / impact**
5. **Fit with long-term identity**
6. **Compensation**
7. **Stress / workload / control**
8. **People and culture**

Research would predict that **1–5 and 8** often matter at least as much as, and often more than, **6**, once compensation is already high enough.

## Bottom line from the literature

If both jobs are already well-paid and secure, then:

- **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work usually predicts higher job satisfaction than a modest salary increase**
- **person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction**
- **people do better when their work satisfies intrinsic motives**
- **direct evidence on regret is limited**
- **leadership aspirations are a real factor, not a side note**

So the most evidence-based reading is not “take the more interesting job no matter what,” but rather:

> **A modest pay bump usually does not compensate much for giving up intrinsic quality of work — unless the new role gives you other important intrinsic goods, such as leadership, growth, autonomy, or fit with your long-term identity.**

If you want, I can also turn this into a **decision matrix based on the research literature** rather than general career advice.

answer:
Yes—there is a substantial research literature on this, and the broad result is:

**Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being.**
**Pay matters too, but usually less than people expect once pay is already “good enough” and feels fair.**

A few important nuances:
- Most of the evidence is on **job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, turnover**, and sometimes overall well-being.
- There is **much less direct research on “regret”** in the exact sense you mean.
- The hardest causal issue is that people **self-select** into jobs, so the cleanest evidence is limited. Still, the pattern is pretty consistent across organizational psychology and labor economics.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses. The core idea is that jobs are more satisfying when they have things like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / impact**
- **task identity**
- **feedback**

These are not exactly the same as “interesting,” but in practice they overlap a lot with what people mean by intellectually engaging work.

### What the evidence says
Meta-analyses have repeatedly found that these job-design features have **moderate to strong associations** with:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

A particularly influential meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design variables like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback showed robust relationships with satisfaction and motivation.

Earlier:
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis supporting the job characteristics model.

### Practical interpretation
If one job is genuinely more intellectually engaging, uses more of your strengths, and feels more consequential, the literature would generally predict **higher day-to-day satisfaction** in that job.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation and meaningful work matter a lot

Another big literature comes from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan). The core idea is that people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others / part of something

Interesting work often boosts the first two, and sometimes the third.

### Findings
Reviews and meta-analytic work generally show that intrinsic motivation and meaningful work are associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower burnout risk
- better persistence
- lower turnover intentions

Representative sources:
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — major review on self-determination theory at work
- Recent meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** on meaningful work

### Important caveat
Meaningful work is not a free lunch. There’s also evidence that people in meaningful jobs can tolerate:
- worse boundaries
- underpayment
- overwork

So “interesting/meaningful” work predicts satisfaction, but it does **not** protect you from burnout if the environment is bad.

---

## 3) Pay helps, but its relationship with job satisfaction is modest

There’s a very common result in this area:

**Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is much smaller than the relationship for many intrinsic job features.**

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — the relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but modest**.

The rough takeaway from that literature is:
- If pay is **too low**, or feels **unfair**, it matters a lot.
- Once pay is already high/comfortable, additional salary tends to have **smaller returns** for job satisfaction than things like autonomy, interesting work, and fit.

This is especially relevant in your case, because your decision sounds like:
- good job vs good job
- high compensation vs slightly higher compensation

That is exactly the kind of comparison where the literature usually finds that **work content and fit matter more** than a modest pay delta.

---

## 4) People often trade pay for interesting work

Labor economics also gives a useful signal here: workers often accept lower wages in exchange for non-monetary job benefits.

This is the idea of **compensating differentials**.

For example:
- people accept less pay for more autonomy
- better mission
- more flexibility
- prestige
- more enjoyable tasks
- more interesting research environments

A famous niche example:
- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** — evidence that scientists accept lower wages for the opportunity to do research they value.

That does not prove the tradeoff is right for any individual, but it does suggest something important:
**interesting work has real economic value to workers**. People systematically give up money to get it.

---

## 5) Fit matters: not just “interesting” in general, but interesting to you

Research on **person-job fit** and **vocational interest congruence** finds that people do better when their work matches their interests, values, and strengths.

Meta-analytic work on fit generally finds better fit is associated with:
- higher satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower quit intentions
- better well-being

Representative:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

This matters for your case because “lead a small team” may itself be intrinsically important to you.

If leadership is not just a status signal but something you genuinely want, then the Google role is not simply:
- more money + less interesting work

It may be:
- less technical interest
- more leadership/autonomy/impact/identity

And those are also intrinsic job qualities.

That is: from a research point of view, **leading a team may itself be a source of meaning and satisfaction**, if it fits your values.

---

## 6) Promotions and managerial moves do not reliably make people happier long-term

The evidence on promotions and managerial advancement is more mixed than people assume.

Broadly, longitudinal studies suggest:
- promotions often produce an **initial boost** in satisfaction
- but they also increase **stress, hours, responsibility, and pressure**
- the satisfaction gain often **fades over time**

So the literature does **not** support a simple rule that “moving up” makes people happier.

Whether management improves well-being depends heavily on:
- whether you actually want to manage
- whether you are good at it
- whether the organization supports managers well
- whether the new role increases autonomy/impact or just adds meetings/stress

This is especially relevant because many strong ICs are happier staying ICs, while many aspiring leaders become happier once they manage.

---

## 7) On “regret”: evidence is thinner

I’m not aware of a strong body of research that directly answers:

> “Do people who choose the more interesting job later regret not taking the higher-paid/leadership job?”

The literature mostly measures:
- job satisfaction
- life satisfaction
- affect
- burnout
- turnover
- commitment

That said, the pattern from adjacent work is roughly:

- people often **adapt** to salary/status gains
- they experience the **content of their work every day**
- so daily work quality tends to matter a lot for sustained job satisfaction

So while I would not overclaim on “regret,” the evidence is more consistent with:
- **interesting, well-fitting work produces more durable satisfaction**
than with
- “you’ll wish you had taken the slightly higher salary”

---

## The most defensible summary from the research

If I had to compress the literature into a few claims:

1. **Interesting/intrinsically motivating work is a major predictor of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic job quality once compensation is already strong and fair.**
3. **Leadership roles are not universally better; they help mainly when leadership itself is part of your intrinsic motivation and identity.**
4. **Direct evidence on regret is weak, but there is better evidence that people adapt to money/status more than to the everyday character of their work.**
5. **The decision should be framed as a fit question, not just a salary question.**

---

## What seems most relevant to your exact situation

For someone already in a high-status, high-pay research role, the literature would generally predict:

- If the OpenAI role is **much more interesting day-to-day**, that likely has a strong claim on long-term satisfaction.
- If the Google role genuinely fulfills a deep desire to **lead, mentor, and build**, that could also be a strong intrinsic benefit—not just an extrinsic one.
- The salary difference, if only “a bit higher,” is probably the **least psychologically important** part of the tradeoff, unless it changes something concrete in your life.

So the research-based version of your question is probably not:

> interesting work vs money

but rather:

> technical/research interest vs leadership fit

And the literature says **either one can dominate**, depending on what is intrinsically important to you.

---

## A few references to start with

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980) — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R.** (1987) — meta-analysis on the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007) — meta-analysis on work design  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010) — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L.** (2005) — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005) — meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
- **Stern, S.** (2004) — *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?*  
- Recent reviews/meta-analyses by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** on meaningful work

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you a **more “literature review”-style answer** with:
- stronger separation of causal vs correlational evidence,
- effect-size comparisons,
- and papers specifically on **management/promotion and happiness**.

**Summarizing job satisfaction factors**

(2019) on meaningful work, and Cerasoli et al. (2014) discussing intrinsic motivation and performance. I could also add Williams et al. (2006) or Judge et al. on pay and job satisfaction. Kasser & Ryan's work on aspirations might be relevant, and Van den Broeck et al. (2016) on psychological needs would fit too.
