answer:
Yes. The short version from the research literature is:

- **Work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and uses your skills is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than those “day-to-day experience of the work” factors, especially once pay is already high enough that basic and comfort needs are well covered.**
- **Leadership/promotion is mixed:** it can increase satisfaction if it gives more autonomy, impact, and growth, but it can also reduce satisfaction if it mainly adds stress, politics, and distance from the work you actually enjoy.
- **Direct evidence on “regret” for this exact tradeoff is surprisingly limited.** The closest literature suggests people do better when their choice fits their intrinsic interests/values rather than being driven mainly by extrinsic rewards.

A bit more detail:

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to satisfaction

A very large literature in organizational psychology says that certain job features reliably predict satisfaction and motivation:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/mastery**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses have generally found that these features have **medium-to-large associations** with job satisfaction.

A useful summary from that literature is:

- **Autonomy/control** is one of the strongest predictors.
- **Interesting/challenging work** and **skill variety** matter a lot.
- **Meaning/significance** matters a lot.
- These are not just “nice extras”; they are central predictors of whether people like their jobs.

Roughly speaking, in meta-analytic work, the correlations between these intrinsic/job-design variables and job satisfaction are often in the **~0.3 to 0.5** range, which is meaningful in this kind of research.

## 2) Meaningful work predicts not just job satisfaction, but broader well-being

There is also a separate “meaningful work” literature. Across studies, people who feel their work is meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions
- somewhat better general well-being

A caveat: much of this literature is correlational, so it does not prove pure causality. But the pattern is strong and consistent enough that most researchers take it seriously.

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect

There is good evidence that **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction**, but the relationship is generally **smaller** than the relationship for intrinsic job characteristics.

Two nuances matter here:

### a) Absolute pay vs relative pay/fairness
People care a lot about:

- whether they feel **fairly paid**
- whether they feel **underpaid relative to peers/market**
- whether compensation signals status or recognition

These often matter more than raw dollars alone.

### b) Diminishing returns
At low-to-moderate income, more money clearly helps well-being. At already-high income levels, the additional well-being from more money tends to be **smaller at the margin** than changes in daily work experience.

So if the choice is:

- “much more money vs interesting work” at low income, money can dominate
- “a bit more money vs substantially more interesting work” at already high compensation, the research usually points more toward the importance of the work itself

## 4) Leadership opportunities are not just “extrinsic”

This part is important for your case.

You said leading a small team is **something you’ve always wanted to do**. In the research, that is not merely a salary/status variable. It may reflect:

- a desire for **impact**
- a desire for **growth**
- a desire for **autonomy**
- a desire for **competence/mastery in a broader role**
- identity fit (“this is the kind of career I want”)

Those are closer to **intrinsic or internalized goals** than to pure extrinsic reward.

This is why the literature does **not** say “interesting technical work always wins.” A management/leadership move can increase well-being **if it better fits your values and desired self-concept**.

But leadership is mixed because it also tends to bring:

- more role conflict
- more interruptions
- more politics
- less time on deep work
- more stress/accountability

So the research-based version is:

- **Leadership helps satisfaction when it increases fit, autonomy, and meaning**
- **Leadership hurts satisfaction when it mainly adds demands and removes the parts of work you actually enjoy**

## 5) Regret: less direct evidence, but there are adjacent findings

I don’t know of a famous, definitive line of research saying “people who chose interesting work over higher pay later regretted it less” in a clean causal way.

But several adjacent literatures point in that direction:

### a) Person-job fit / person-organization fit
People tend to do better when their job matches:

- their interests
- their values
- their preferred way of working
- their goals

Better fit predicts:

- higher satisfaction
- lower turnover intentions
- better commitment
- better well-being

### b) Intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
Research associated with **self-determination theory** and related work by **Kasser, Ryan, and others** finds that people who orient more strongly around **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) generally report better well-being than those focused primarily on **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status).

That does **not** mean money/status are bad. It means that when they are the main driver of choice, they tend to be less reliable sources of lasting satisfaction.

### c) Turnover and quitting intentions
People in jobs they find meaningful/engaging are less likely to want to leave. That is not the same as “regret,” but it is close behaviorally.

## 6) One of the strongest findings: autonomy/control matters enormously

A separate but related literature on **job control** and **job demands-control** finds that having control over your work is a big predictor of both satisfaction and stress levels.

That matters here because:

- a research role can be interesting but frustrating if autonomy is low
- a team-lead role can be less intellectually interesting but satisfying if autonomy and impact are high
- or it can be miserable if it is mostly organizational overhead without real control

So if you compare the jobs, “interestingness” alone is too narrow. Research would suggest looking closely at:

- **autonomy**
- **meaning**
- **fit**
- **learning/growth**
- **social environment**
- **manager quality**
- **workload/stress**
- **fairness of pay**

## 7) If I had to compress the evidence into one sentence

If compensation in both roles is already strong, then **the daily qualities of the work itself**—especially **interest, meaning, autonomy, growth, and fit**—usually do more for sustained job satisfaction than a modest salary increase alone.

The big exception is when the “less interesting” job actually fulfills a deeper aspiration—like leadership, broader impact, or identity fit—in which case it may not be less meaningful to *you* even if the tasks are less intrinsically interesting.

---

## Some specific literatures/papers worth looking up

If you want the research trail, these are good anchors:

### Work design / interesting work
- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — major meta-analytic review of work design and outcomes
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — early meta-analysis on job characteristics

### Meaningful work
- Reviews/meta-analyses by **Allan** and colleagues on meaningful work and outcomes
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)** — review on meaningful work

### Intrinsic motivation / self-determination
- **Deci & Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Van den Broeck** and colleagues — need satisfaction at work
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

### Pay and satisfaction
- Meta-analytic work by **Williams, McDaniel, Nguyen** and related pay–job satisfaction literature
- Work by **Clark, Frijters, Shields** on income, relative income, and satisfaction

### Job satisfaction and broader well-being
- **Judge** and colleagues on job satisfaction, life satisfaction, and work attitudes

---

## Bottom line from the research

For someone in your situation, the literature would generally predict:

1. **A bit more salary alone** is unlikely to compensate for a meaningful drop in day-to-day interest/meaning/autonomy.
2. **A leadership opportunity could absolutely compensate** if leadership is itself a deeply valued aspiration and the role offers real autonomy, growth, and impact.
3. The most predictive question is probably not “OpenAI vs Google” or “salary vs interestingness,” but:
   - Which option better satisfies your needs for **autonomy, mastery, meaning, impact, and fit** over the next few years?

If you want, I can turn this into a more concrete **research-based decision framework** with factors and weights, sticking only to variables that have decent empirical support.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is pretty consistent on one broad point:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” day-to-day job quality—especially how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-promoting the work feels—usually predicts job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than incremental pay differences do.**

That is not a personal opinion; it’s the center of gravity of organizational psychology and a fair reading of a lot of labor/well-being research.

## What the literature says

### 1. “Interesting work” is strongly tied to job satisfaction
In research, “interesting work” is usually captured indirectly through constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1970s): jobs tend to be more motivating and satisfying when they offer:

- variety
- whole/identifiable tasks
- significance/impact
- autonomy
- feedback

Later meta-analyses largely supported this. A major one:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a meta-analysis of work design research, found that motivational job characteristics were **consistently associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal**.

So on average, people in work that is more engaging/interesting/autonomous are indeed happier with their jobs.

---

### 2. Meaningful work has a large association with well-being
This is one of the strongest findings in this area.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** conducted a meta-analysis on **meaningful work** and found strong positive links with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - **lower distress / fewer negative mental health outcomes**

The exact effect sizes vary by outcome, but the overall pattern is not subtle: meaningful work is one of the strongest non-pay predictors of positive work experience.

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in practice they often travel together for knowledge workers.

---

### 3. Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is smaller than many people expect
There are two related but distinct findings:

#### a) Actual pay has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction
Meta-analytic work on pay and job satisfaction generally finds the relationship is **positive but not huge**.

- A common summary of that literature is that **actual pay level correlates only weakly-to-moderately with job satisfaction**.
- In plain English: more pay helps, but it usually does **not** dominate how people feel about their jobs.

This is especially true when comparing:
- **good salary vs slightly better salary**
rather than
- **financial stress vs financial security**.

That distinction matters a lot.

#### b) Income improves well-being, but with diminishing practical impact
In the broader happiness literature:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found a difference between **life evaluation** and **experienced emotional well-being**, with emotional well-being showing a plateau in their data.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** later argued that well-being continues to rise with income even at higher levels.

Those papers differ on the exact shape of the curve, but they agree on something relevant to your case:

**income matters, but it is not the only thing, and it rarely swamps the importance of daily lived experience at work.**

For someone already in a high-paying research role, a **small salary premium** is unlikely, on average, to compensate for a **large drop in intrinsic interest**.

---

### 4. Intrinsic motives tend to be better for well-being than extrinsic motives
A lot of research coming from **Self-Determination Theory** points in the same direction.

The most relevant result is:

- When work satisfies needs for **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness**, people report better well-being, engagement, and persistence.
- When people orient more strongly around **extrinsic rewards alone** (money, status, image), they tend to do worse on well-being than people whose work also supports intrinsic motives.

Relevant reviews/meta-analytic work include:

- **Van den Broeck et al.** on psychological need satisfaction at work
- broader SDT reviews by **Deci, Ryan**, and colleagues

This does **not** mean money is bad. It means that if you trade away too much intrinsic interest for extrinsic reward, the average well-being outcome is often worse.

---

### 5. Fit matters a lot
Another strong literature is **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analyzed fit research and found that better fit predicts:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - stronger organizational commitment
  - lower intention to quit
  - better well-being-related outcomes

This is important for your specific tradeoff, because your choice is not just:

- interesting work vs more salary

It’s also:

- **IC/research depth** vs **leading a team**
- **current organizational fit** vs **new organizational fit**
- **current work identity** vs **future work identity**

If leading a team is something you genuinely want, that is not merely an extrinsic perk. It could be an **intrinsic fit variable**.

---

## Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is where the evidence gets thinner.

I don’t know of a clean, definitive body of research saying:

> “People who choose the more interesting but lower-paid offer later regret it less than people who choose the higher-paid less-interesting one.”

The literature is more indirect.

### What we do know indirectly:
People are less likely to want out of a job—and more likely to feel good over time—when the job provides:

- meaning
- interest
- autonomy
- growth
- fit with values/identity

And they are more likely to want out when the job is:

- misaligned with interests
- low-meaning
- low-autonomy
- high-demand without enough resources

So while “regret” itself is less directly studied, the predictors of **future dissatisfaction / wanting to reverse the decision** are fairly well known.

A reasonable summary of the evidence is:

**People are more likely to feel good over time when their work matches what they find intrinsically engaging and identity-consistent, even if another option pays somewhat more.**

---

## What about becoming a manager / leading a small team?
The literature here is more mixed.

Leading a team can increase:

- autonomy
- status
- perceived impact
- sense of growth
- future career capital

But it can also increase:

- administrative burden
- interruptions
- stress
- role conflict
- distance from the work you actually enjoy

Research on the **Job Demands–Resources** model suggests that outcomes depend on the balance:

- more demands are fine if accompanied by meaningful resources
- without that, well-being often falls

So the evidence does **not** say “management makes people happier.”
It says something more like:

**Leadership roles are better when the person actually wants the leadership work itself, not just the title or career signal.**

That’s relevant because you said leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted. If that is a genuine source of meaning/interest for you, it belongs on the “intrinsic” side of the ledger, not just the extrinsic side.

---

## The most defensible research-based takeaway

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

> **For people who are already financially secure, long-run job satisfaction tends to depend more on intrinsic job quality and fit than on modest differences in compensation.**

And in your case, “intrinsic job quality” includes at least two distinct things:

1. **the work itself being interesting**
2. **the role itself fitting your identity/goals** (e.g. leading a team)

So the empirical question is not just:
- “OpenAI is more interesting, Google pays more”

It is more like:
- “Which role gives me the better combination of interest, autonomy, meaning, growth, and fit?”

That framing is much closer to how the research would analyze it.

---

## If you want a very short research answer

- **Yes**, more interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.
- **Yes**, this effect is usually stronger than the effect of small-to-moderate pay differences, especially among already high earners.
- **No**, the regret evidence is not as direct, but the predictors of lower future dissatisfaction strongly favor intrinsic fit and meaningful work.
- **Leadership opportunity** may offset lower interest in the subject matter *if* managing people is itself intrinsically meaningful to you.

---

## Useful papers / authors to look up

If you want to read the actual literature, I’d start with these:

1. **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model  
2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
3. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work  
4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
5. **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory**; and **Van den Broeck et al.** on need satisfaction at work  
6. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and well-being  
7. Meta-analytic work on **pay and job satisfaction** (e.g. **Judge et al.**, related pay-satisfaction literature)

If you want, I can give you a **more citation-heavy version** with actual paper titles and the main findings in a compact annotated bibliography.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**jobs that are more intrinsically motivating — interesting, meaningful, autonomous, growth-oriented, and well-matched to the person — tend to be associated with higher job satisfaction and often higher overall well-being than jobs that mainly differ on pay.**  
Salary matters, but usually **less for day-to-day job satisfaction** than people expect, especially once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the main issue.

A few of the most relevant findings:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is a real predictor of satisfaction

In research, “interesting” is usually broken into things like:

- **autonomy**  
- **skill variety / challenge / learning**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / sense of progress**
- **fit with your interests and values**

### Classic result: job design matters a lot
The classic **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976) argued that jobs higher in autonomy, variety, significance, etc. produce more internal motivation and satisfaction.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these kinds of job characteristics are **substantially related** to:
- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement-like outcomes
- lower withdrawal/turnover-type outcomes

This is one of the strongest and most replicated literatures in organizational psychology.

**Implication:** if one option is genuinely much more intellectually engaging/interesting, that is not a soft or sentimental factor; it is one of the core predictors of job satisfaction.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

A related literature studies **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work, e.g. **Allan et al. (2019)**, finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- stronger commitment
- better general well-being
- lower turnover intentions

This does **not** mean “meaning” solves everything — people can find work meaningful and still burn out — but meaningfulness is one of the most reliable positive correlates of work-related well-being.

If “interesting” for you partly means “this work actually matters / I care about it / it’s intellectually alive,” the literature would generally treat that as a major positive factor.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less for job satisfaction than many assume

There is a good meta-analysis on this: **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — roughly in the neighborhood of **~.15**.

That is an important result. It means:
- more pay does tend to help a bit,
- but by itself it is **not a strong predictor** of how satisfied people are with their jobs.

By comparison, things like autonomy, fit, meaning, and the nature of the work tend to show **larger** relationships.

### Income and overall happiness
The broader “income and happiness” literature is more mixed and nuanced:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found a plateau for emotional well-being in older data.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found well-being continuing to rise with income.
- A reconciliation paper (**Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers, 2023**) suggests that for most people well-being continues to rise with income, though not always equally for everyone.

But even in that literature, the key point for your question is:
- **income helps**, especially for security and reducing constraints,
- yet **job satisfaction is not mainly driven by pay** once you’re already in a well-paid range.

So if the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” the literature would not usually predict a large happiness gain from salary alone.

---

## 4) Fit matters: people do better in roles that match their interests and values

There is a big literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

Meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** shows that better fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better performance-related outcomes

More narrowly, research on **interest congruence** (whether your work matches your interests) generally finds a **real but modest** relationship with satisfaction — often around the **small-to-moderate** range in meta-analytic work.

So:
- “This work is more interesting to me” is likely beneficial.
- But “I’ve always wanted to lead a team” is also a fit variable.

That second part matters. Leadership is not just an extrinsic prestige marker; if it aligns with a genuine aspiration, it can satisfy needs for growth, autonomy, competence, and identity.

---

## 5) Work satisfaction spills into overall life satisfaction

This is relevant if your question is really about being happier, not just liking the job more.

Meta-analytic work such as **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)** found a **moderate positive relationship** between **job satisfaction** and **life satisfaction** (often cited around **~.4**).  
Longitudinal work such as **Judge & Watanabe (1993)** suggests the relationship is at least partly reciprocal over time.

So choosing the option that yields higher job satisfaction is not just about “work mood”; it often affects broader well-being too.

---

## 6) What about regret?

This is where the evidence is weaker and more indirect.

I do **not** know of a clean body of research saying specifically:

> people who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paid less-interesting one later regretted it less

At least not in a way that is as established as the job satisfaction literature.

What we do have is broader regret research:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: over the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: major regrets cluster around domains like **education, career, and romance**, often involving missed opportunities for growth or self-development.

That is **not** direct proof for your exact choice.  
But it does suggest that long-run regret often centers on **not pursuing personally important opportunities**, not just on leaving money on the table.

In your case, importantly, there are **two different kinds of “personally important opportunities”**:
1. doing more interesting work
2. getting to lead a team

So the regret literature does not resolve your tradeoff; it mainly says that what people later regret most is often **failing to act on something identity-relevant**.

---

# What the literature would imply for your specific tradeoff

If I translate the research very literally, it suggests:

### If the difference were only:
- **more interesting work** vs
- **slightly more pay**

then the research would generally lean toward **interesting work** as the stronger predictor of job satisfaction and probably well-being.

### But your actual tradeoff is:
- **more interesting work**
vs
- **slightly more pay + a first team-lead opportunity**

That second option is not just about money. It includes:
- growth
- status/advancement
- leadership identity
- possibly greater autonomy/influence

So the literature would say the right comparison is not “money vs interest,” but something more like:

> **Which role better satisfies your core psychological needs and fit:**
> - autonomy
> - competence/growth
> - relatedness
> - meaning
> - day-to-day interest
> - identity congruence

That is the research-based framing.

---

# Bottom line from the evidence

The strongest empirical takeaways are:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly than pay does.**
2. **Pay has a real but relatively small relationship with job satisfaction.**
3. **Fit matters a lot** — including fit with your interests *and* fit with aspirations like leadership.
4. **Job satisfaction affects broader life satisfaction.**
5. **Direct evidence on regret is thinner**, but broad regret research suggests people often regret not pursuing identity-relevant opportunities.

So if your question is strictly:

> “Does research suggest that people are usually happier staying in the more interesting job?”

then the answer is:

**On average, yes — if “more interesting” also means more intrinsically motivating, meaningful, and better-fitting.**  
But in your case, the leadership opportunity is itself one of those intrinsically important factors, so the research would not treat the Google offer as “just more money.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** with maybe 6–8 factors and suggested weights from the literature.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work tends to be a much stronger predictor of job satisfaction and well-being than salary differences, especially once pay is already high enough that money is not a major source of stress.**  
But in your case there’s an important twist: **leading a team is itself an intrinsic job feature**, not just an extrinsic one. So the research would not frame this as “interesting work vs money” only; it’s more like **intellectual interest vs leadership/impact/autonomy**, plus some pay.

## What the research tends to find

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics matter a lot
A large organizational psychology literature finds that people are more satisfied when their jobs have features like:

- **interesting/challenging tasks**
- **autonomy/control**
- **task variety**
- **skill use**
- **feedback**
- **sense of impact/meaning**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses generally support it.

A particularly cited meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  They reviewed a large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, and task significance had **substantial positive associations** with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and performance-related outcomes.

The overall takeaway from this literature is pretty stable:  
**Jobs people find intrinsically engaging are usually associated with higher satisfaction than jobs that mainly improve extrinsic rewards.**

---

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There is also a strong literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Meaningful work is positively associated with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - and negatively associated with **turnover intentions** and some forms of distress

So if by “interesting” you mean not just fun, but **intellectually engaging and meaningful**, that is one of the strongest positive predictors in the literature.

---

### 3. Person-job fit matters a lot
Another very robust result: people are happier when their job fits their interests, values, and strengths.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  Various forms of **person-job/person-organization fit** are meaningfully related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - lower intentions to quit

This is relevant because your dilemma is not generic. The question is not “is management better than research?” but “which role fits **your** motives better?”  
If deep technical work is central to your identity, moving away from it may reduce fit.  
If building and leading a team has become a central aspiration, then the new role may actually improve fit despite lower day-to-day technical interest.

---

### 4. Pay matters, but much less than many people expect
There is research specifically on pay and job satisfaction.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis  
  The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** exists, but it is **fairly modest**. It is typically much weaker than the relationships found for intrinsic job features like autonomy, fit, or meaningfulness.

A good rough summary is:

- **Pay matters**
- but **it is not one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction**
- and its effect is often smaller than people intuitively expect

This is especially true when the salary difference is “a bit higher” rather than life-changing, and when both options are already very well-paid.

---

### 5. Economists also find people accept lower pay for more interesting work
There’s a labor economics concept called **compensating differentials**: workers accept lower pay for jobs with desirable non-monetary attributes.

A very relevant paper for research careers:

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**  
  He found evidence that scientists value intrinsic aspects of the work—such as freedom to pursue ideas and publish—enough that they are willing to accept lower wages for them.

That does not prove they are happier later, but it strongly suggests that **interesting/intellectually autonomous work has real economic value to workers**, not just rhetorical value.

This result also shows up more broadly in mission-driven or intrinsically rewarding work: people often trade pay for meaning, autonomy, or intellectual freedom.

---

## What about regret?
This is where the literature is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous body of research that directly says:

> “People who chose the more interesting job later regretted it less than people who chose the higher-paying less interesting job.”

There is much more evidence on:
- **job satisfaction**
- **turnover**
- **fit**
- **well-being**
than on explicit long-run **regret**

So the cleanest evidence-based statement is:

- People tend to be **more satisfied and less likely to want to leave** jobs that are more meaningful, interesting, autonomous, and better matched to their values/interests.
- People do **not** seem to get very large or lasting job-satisfaction gains from pay increases alone, unless money meaningfully changes their life circumstances.

That is not the same as direct “regret” evidence, but it is the closest and strongest empirical base.

---

## What about leading a team?
This is the most important nuance in your case.

If the Google role were simply:
- less interesting work
- more money
then the research would lean pretty clearly toward the interesting job.

But your alternative includes:
- **leading a small team**
- which you say is something you’ve always wanted

That matters because leadership can satisfy intrinsic needs too:
- **autonomy**
- **impact**
- **growth**
- **mastery**
- **status/recognition**
- **mentoring/relatedness**

From a research perspective, that means the Google offer may not actually be “less intrinsic” overall. It may be:

- less intrinsically rewarding on **content**
- more intrinsically rewarding on **leadership/impact**

And the literature would predict that your satisfaction depends a lot on **which intrinsic dimension matters more to you now**.

Also, evidence on promotion/managerial advancement is more mixed than evidence on interesting work:
- management can increase control and influence,
- but also increases meetings, coordination costs, role conflict, and stress,
- and some technical people find they miss doing the work itself.

So research would not say “leadership is better” or “technical depth is better” in general. It would say **fit is crucial**.

---

## If I had to compress the research into 4 points

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work reliably predicts higher job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction, but usually weakly compared with intrinsic job features.**
3. **People often willingly trade pay for more interesting work, especially in research/science.**
4. **The biggest moderating factor is fit:** if leading a team is a deeply valued aspiration for you, that can itself be a major source of satisfaction.

---

## So what does the literature suggest for your exact choice?
Not a personal opinion—just translating the evidence:

- If the Google move is mainly **more salary + less engaging work**, the literature generally predicts **lower or not much higher satisfaction** than staying in an interesting research role.
- If the Google move meaningfully delivers a long-held intrinsic goal—**leading people, shaping a direction, having broader impact**—then the choice is less about money and more about **which intrinsic reward structure fits you better now**.

In other words, the research favors **intrinsic rewards over extrinsic rewards**, but it does **not** reduce all intrinsic rewards to “interesting technical content.” Leadership can count too.

---

## A few references you may want to look up
- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976). Motivation through the design of work.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007). *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.* Journal of Applied Psychology.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010). *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005). *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** (2019). Meta-analysis on meaningful work and well-being outcomes.  
- **Stern, S.** (2004). *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?* Management Science.

If you want, I can do one of two more research-focused follow-ups:

1. give you a **more detailed literature summary with effect sizes**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the research** rather than personal advice.

answer:
Yes. The broad research picture is pretty consistent:

**Intrinsic qualities of work** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-producing the job is — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day work well-being than pay level**.  
**Pay matters**, but its direct relationship with job satisfaction is typically **positive and fairly small**.  
**Direct evidence on “later regret” is much thinner**; most studies measure satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover intentions instead.

## Short answer

If by “interesting” you mean things like:

- intellectually engaging work
- learning/growth
- autonomy
- meaningful impact
- good fit with your strengths/interests

then the literature generally says **people in those jobs tend to report higher job satisfaction and often higher overall well-being** than people who optimize mainly for extrinsic factors like salary/status.

But there are two important qualifiers:

1. **Interesting work does not compensate for everything.**  
   If it comes with chronic overload, bad management, low autonomy, or blocked advancement, satisfaction suffers.

2. **Leadership opportunity can itself be an intrinsic good.**  
   Since you said leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted, that is *not* just an extrinsic perk; in the research language, it may satisfy growth, competence, autonomy, and impact motives.

So the research does **not** reduce to “interesting work always wins.” It’s more like:  
**intrinsic job quality usually matters a lot more than people think, and salary a bit less than people think — but role fit still dominates.**

---

# What the research says

## 1) Job content/job design predicts satisfaction quite strongly

A large organizational-psychology literature shows that features of the work itself are strongly tied to satisfaction and motivation.

### Classic line of evidence
The **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses found that jobs with more:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**

are associated with higher:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal work motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  Found that work-design variables, especially **autonomy** and **task characteristics**, had substantial relationships with satisfaction and other positive outcomes.

**Interpretation:**  
“Interesting work” often cashes out empirically as autonomy + complexity + variety + significance. Those are among the strongest and most replicated predictors of liking one’s job.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is very strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

There is now a substantial literature on **meaningful work**.

Representative conclusion from meta-analytic work:

- People who experience their work as **meaningful** report higher:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
- and lower:
  - **withdrawal intentions**
  - **psychological distress/burnout**, on average

A good reference here is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

The exact coefficients vary by study and outcome, but the overall pattern is that **meaning/interest is not a small “nice-to-have”; it is a major correlate of well-being at work.**

---

## 3) Pay is positively related to job satisfaction — but only modestly

This is one of the clearest findings relevant to your question.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis…”

Their conclusion was that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. In rough terms: knowing that one job pays more tells you **much less** about whether someone will be satisfied than knowing whether the work itself is interesting/autonomous/meaningful.

So, when people say “salary matters less than job content,” that is not just folk wisdom; it is reasonably supported.

### Broader well-being/income literature
Outside work psychology, subjective-well-being research also suggests:

- more income generally helps
- but the marginal emotional gain from additional income is often **smaller than people expect**, especially once basic financial security is already high

Relevant names:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)** and later debate/updates

This literature is about overall well-being, not just job satisfaction, but it points in the same direction: **extra money helps, but does not dominate daily experience.**

---

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to be better for sustained well-being than extrinsic motivation

Another major line of research comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and related motivation work.

The core finding:
When work satisfies needs for

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

people tend to have better well-being, more vitality, stronger motivation, and lower burnout.

Representative sources:
- **Deci & Ryan**
- **Van den Broeck et al.** reviews/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work

This matters because “interesting work” often provides:
- challenge
- mastery
- self-direction
- identity coherence

whereas pure extrinsic upgrades often give:
- more money
- more status
- less change in daily experience

And daily experience is what strongly drives whether a job feels good.

---

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another well-established finding:

If the role fits your interests, strengths, and preferences, people tend to be more satisfied and less likely to want to leave.

Key review/meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person-environment fit

This literature says that “what makes a good job” is not universal.  
For one person, leading a team is energizing; for another, it is a drain.  
For one person, deeply technical work is fulfilling; for another, influence/coordination/people development is more meaningful.

So the research would tell you to ask not merely:

- “Which job is more objectively interesting?”

but also:

- “Which role is a better fit for my motives right now?”

That matters because **leading a team**, if it’s something you genuinely want, may represent a big increase in fit.

---

## 6) Promotions/leadership roles do not automatically increase happiness

There is some evidence that promotions and status gains improve job attitudes, but often **less than expected** and sometimes only temporarily.

Why?
Because management jobs often bring:
- more responsibility
- more interruption
- more politics
- less hands-on technical flow
- more emotional labor

If someone values leadership intrinsically, this can be positive.  
If they mainly value technical immersion, promotion can backfire.

So the literature would not say “leading a team will make you happier.”  
It would say **it will help if it matches what you find meaningful and energizing**.

---

# What about regret specifically?

Here the evidence is less direct.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous literature specifically asking:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting but lower-paid job?”

Most adjacent research instead tracks:
- job satisfaction
- life satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover
- commitment
- engagement
- career success

## What can be inferred indirectly

### Likely true:
People who prioritize **intrinsic work values** (growth, meaning, interest, contribution) over purely **extrinsic values** (money, prestige) often show **better well-being**, on average.

Relevant work:
- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations

### Important caveat:
People with a strong sense of “calling” also can be more willing to tolerate:
- overwork
- poor boundaries
- exploitation
- under-reward

So “interesting” or “meaningful” work is not a free lunch.

There is a real phenomenon where people love the work but regret:
- missing leadership growth
- underinvesting in compensation
- burning out
- becoming pigeonholed

So the research does **not** say “follow interest no matter what.”  
It says **interest/meaning are major predictors of satisfaction, but only within a viable overall job package.**

---

# If you want a rough empirical hierarchy

For predicting **job satisfaction**, the literature would roughly support something like:

1. **Daily work experience / job design**
   - autonomy
   - meaningfulness
   - interesting problems
   - learning
   - feedback
   - manager quality

2. **Fit**
   - does the role match your motives, strengths, and identity?

3. **Social environment**
   - team quality
   - trust
   - culture

4. **Compensation**
   - important, but usually a weaker direct predictor than people assume, especially at already-high income levels

That is a simplification, but directionally it is pretty faithful to the research.

---

# Applied to your specific fork, in research terms

Your choice is not really:

- **interesting work vs salary**

It is more like:

- **higher intrinsic task interest + current role fit**
versus
- **higher pay + leadership opportunity + lower task interest**

And that matters because **leadership opportunity itself may be intrinsically valuable to you**.

So the research-based question is not:

> “Should I choose interesting work over money?”

but:

> “Which bundle gives me more of the intrinsic drivers that predict long-run satisfaction: interest, autonomy, growth, meaning, competence, fit, and desired future identity?”

If leading a team is “something I always wanted,” then the Google offer may be less interesting in one dimension but more aligned in another important intrinsic dimension.

---

# Practical takeaway from the literature

A fair summary would be:

- **Yes**: people in more interesting/meaningful/autonomous work tend to be happier at work.
- **Yes**: this effect is usually stronger than the effect of pay differences.
- **No**: that does not mean they never regret it.
- **And**: direct evidence on regret is weak; the better-supported outcomes are satisfaction, engagement, and burnout.
- **Most likely regret cases** occur when interesting work comes with poor boundaries, poor advancement fit, or when a person ignores a major intrinsic goal like leadership.

---

# Good papers/reviews to look at

If you want papers worth actually reading:

1. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

2. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Journal of Vocational Behavior / Applied Psychology line of work.

3. **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*  
   Journal of Management Studies.

4. **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   Personnel Psychology.

5. **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.**  
   Self-Determination Theory papers/books; plus work-specific reviews such as **Van den Broeck et al.** on psychological need satisfaction at work.

6. **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.**  
   Work on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being.

7. **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).**  
   *Jobs, careers, and callings*  
   Useful conceptually, though less decisive than meta-analytic work.

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. turn this into a **one-page research summary with citations and key effect sizes**, or  
2. help you build a **decision matrix based only on variables the literature says actually predict satisfaction**.

answer:
Yes—there is a pretty substantial literature on this, and the broad result is fairly consistent:

**On average, the intrinsic quality of the work itself**—interest, challenge, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit—**is more strongly related to sustained job satisfaction and well-being than modest differences in pay**, especially once pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the main issue.

A few of the main findings:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied through things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **challenge / learning**
- **person-job fit**

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** showed that jobs high in variety, significance, autonomy, etc. produce higher **internal motivation** and **job satisfaction**.

This has held up well in later meta-analyses:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** meta-analyzed the work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy**, **task variety**, and **task significance** had substantial positive relationships with **job satisfaction**, **internal work motivation**, and related outcomes.

In plain English: jobs that are genuinely engaging and give you room to think, create, and own work tend to produce more satisfaction than jobs that are less engaging, even if they come with more money or status.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A related literature studies **meaningful work**, which overlaps a lot with “interesting work” but is not identical.

- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analyzed research on meaningful work and found that it is positively associated with **job satisfaction**, **work engagement**, **life satisfaction**, and negatively associated with **turnover intentions** and **burnout**.

So, people in work they experience as meaningful or intrinsically valuable are, on average, better off psychologically.

## 3) Pay matters—but less than many people expect

There is also good research specifically on **pay vs job satisfaction**.

- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**—real, but much smaller than people often assume.

A rough takeaway from this literature is:

- money matters a lot when it affects **security**, **stress**, or **fairness**
- but beyond that, the link between “a somewhat higher salary” and “I am happier in my job” is usually **not very strong**

Also, people care a lot about:
- whether pay feels **fair**
- how it compares to peers
- whether the job’s conditions feel worth the pay

So the literature would usually predict that **a bit more salary** will not compensate very well for **substantially less interesting work**, particularly for someone already in a high-paying research role.

## 4) Autonomy, competence, and intrinsic motivation matter a lot

A big line of research here is **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan**; see **Gagné & Deci (2005)** for work settings.

The core finding is that well-being at work depends heavily on whether the job supports:
- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice and ownership
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Jobs that frustrate these needs tend to feel draining, even if they are prestigious or well-paid.

This matters for your case because “less interesting” often means:
- less autonomy over intellectually engaging work
- less sense of competence being used in the way you most enjoy
- less curiosity-driven motivation

That tends to be costly for satisfaction.

## 5) “Person-job fit” is a major factor

This is especially relevant because your choice is not just “interesting work vs money.” It also includes **leading a team**, which may itself be a strong intrinsic goal.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis found that **person-job fit** and related kinds of fit are strongly associated with **job satisfaction**, **organizational commitment**, and lower turnover intentions.

So the research would say:

- if you deeply value **leading people**, building a team, and shaping direction, then that is not just an extrinsic perk—it may be an **important intrinsic source of satisfaction**
- conversely, if what you most love is doing research yourself, then moving into a role with less interesting substance may reduce satisfaction even if it advances status or compensation

That is, the evidence favors **fit**, not promotion-for-its-own-sake.

## 6) On “regret”: direct evidence is thinner, but adaptation research is relevant

I’m not aware of a strong, famous literature specifically asking:

> “Do people regret choosing interesting work over a somewhat better-paid but less interesting job?”

That exact question is not studied nearly as directly as job satisfaction and well-being.

But two adjacent findings matter:

### a) People adapt to income gains
Happiness research generally finds that people often **adapt** to higher income/pay. The raise becomes the new baseline.

### b) The day-to-day experience of work keeps recurring
The quality of the work itself—whether it is absorbing, meaningful, frustrating, or deadening—shows up **every working day**. So it tends to have a more persistent effect on lived experience.

That makes it plausible, and broadly consistent with the literature, that people are more likely to feel long-run dissatisfaction from **boring or uninteresting work** than from not taking a modest pay bump.

## 7) There is also economic evidence that non-monetary job qualities have real value

Labor economists study **compensating differentials**: people accept lower wages for jobs with desirable non-pay features.

This literature is broader than “interesting work” specifically, but the basic implication is important:

- workers place **real value** on non-monetary job attributes
- these are not soft or irrational preferences; they act like economically valuable goods

So if someone accepts lower pay for more meaningful or more enjoyable work, that is perfectly consistent with standard economics, not just psychology.

---

# What the literature would predict in a case like yours

If I translate the literature into a careful, non-personal conclusion:

### If the salary difference is only modest
and both roles are already in the “highly compensated” range, then research would generally predict that:

- **ongoing interest in the work**
- **autonomy**
- **meaning**
- **learning/growth**
- **fit with your motives**

will matter **more for long-run satisfaction** than the extra pay.

### But if leading a team is something you’ve wanted for a long time
then the relevant comparison is not simply:

- interesting work vs higher salary

It is more like:

- **more intellectually interesting individual-contributor work**
vs
- **more leadership/ownership/people-building**, but less interesting research content

Research would say that the better outcome depends a lot on **which of those is more central to your identity and motivation**.

That is a person-job-fit question, not a pure salary question.

---

# Very short version

If you want the most evidence-based one-line summary:

> **For long-run job happiness, intrinsic job quality usually beats a modest pay increase.**
> But if leading a team is itself intrinsically meaningful to you, that can absolutely outweigh a loss in task-level interest.

---

# References worth looking at

A few core papers/reviews:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).** Self-determination theory and work motivation.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.

1. give you a **more technical summary with effect sizes** where I’m careful about what is causal vs correlational, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the literature** specifically for “research IC role vs early management role.”

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research relevant to this, and the broad pattern is pretty consistent:

**For people who are already well-paid enough, the day-to-day quality of the work itself tends to matter more for job satisfaction than incremental pay.**  
And among “quality of the work” variables, the most studied ones are things like:

- whether the work is **interesting / varied / challenging**
- whether it feels **meaningful**
- whether you have **autonomy**
- whether you are **learning / growing**
- whether the role fits your motives and identity

A few research lines that are especially relevant:

---

## 1) “Interesting” work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

The classic framework here is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

This is old, but it has held up well. Later meta-analytic work — especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — found that these kinds of work-design variables are **consistently associated with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and lower strain/turnover intentions**.

In plain English:  
If a job is intellectually engaging, gives you room to act, and feels consequential, people usually like it more.

So on your specific axis — “very interesting stuff” vs “less interesting” — the literature generally says that **interesting/challenging work is not a trivial perk; it is one of the central drivers of work satisfaction**.

---

## 2) Meaningful work has a stronger relationship with satisfaction than pay does

There is also a big literature on **meaningful work**. Reviews and meta-analyses in that area generally find that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **organizational commitment**
- lower **turnover intention**
- often better **general well-being**

I’m recalling from memory rather than quoting exact coefficients, but the relationship is generally **moderate to strong**, not tiny.

This does **not** mean meaningful work solves everything. There’s also research showing that people in meaningful work can tolerate bad conditions for too long, overwork, or be more vulnerable to burnout because they care so much. But if the question is simply “does more interesting/meaningful work usually make people happier in the job?”, the answer from the literature is **yes, usually**.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but its effect on job satisfaction is modest

A very relevant paper here is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on the relationship between pay and job satisfaction. The headline finding is:

**The relationship exists, but it is much weaker than many people intuitively expect.**

Higher pay does predict higher job satisfaction, but usually only **modestly**. The effect is real, just not enormous.

This fits a broader literature in economics and psychology:

- money matters a lot when it changes **security**, **stress**, or **freedom**
- beyond that, each additional increment tends to have **diminishing returns**
- people often adapt relatively quickly to higher pay
- but they do **not** adapt as fully to what they experience every day in the job itself

That last point is important.  
A salary increase is often a discrete improvement.  
A job being interesting or boring is a **repeated daily experience**.

So if both jobs already pay well, the literature generally predicts that **work content** has more impact on ongoing job satisfaction than a somewhat higher salary.

---

## 4) Wanting to lead is a real variable — and “fit” matters a lot

Your case isn’t just “money vs interesting work.” There’s another factor: **leading a small team**, which you said is something you’ve always wanted.

Research would treat that as a **person-job fit** issue, not just a title issue.

There’s strong evidence from **person-job fit / person-organization fit** research (e.g. **Kristof-Brown et al.**) that people are more satisfied when the role matches their motives, values, and strengths.

And there’s a specific literature on **motivation to lead** (e.g. **Chan & Drasgow**). The key idea is:

- some people genuinely enjoy leading and influencing others
- some mainly like the status
- some do it out of duty
- outcomes differ depending on which of those is true

So if “leading a team” is not just a prestige marker for you, but a real long-term aspiration, that matters a lot. A management role may be **less intellectually interesting in subject matter** but still more satisfying overall if it better fits an important personal motive.

In research terms, the relevant question is not just:
> “Is the content less interesting?”

It is also:
> “Is this role more aligned with a core motive I have — namely leadership?”

That alignment can offset a lot.

---

## 5) Promotions/management are not automatically happiness upgrades

The evidence here is more mixed.

Becoming a manager often brings:

- more status
- more pay
- more influence

But also:

- more people problems
- less hands-on technical work
- more coordination and politics
- more responsibility and stress

So the average effect of moving into management is **heterogeneous**. It helps some people a lot and others not at all.

A useful research-backed way to think about this is:

- If your satisfaction comes mainly from **deep technical/intellectual immersion**, moving away from that can hurt.
- If your satisfaction comes largely from **influence, mentoring, building teams, and larger-scale impact**, management can improve satisfaction.

There isn’t a universal “management is better” result. The literature is much closer to:
**management is better when it fits what you actually want from work.**

---

## 6) On regret specifically: evidence is thinner, but values/identity seem to matter more than money

This is the least cleanly answered part of your question.

I’m not aware of a famous definitive study that says:
> “People who stayed in the more interesting job later regretted it less than those who took the higher-paying less-interesting one.”

But adjacent regret research suggests a few things:

### a) Long-term regret often centers on identity and missed growth, not just money
Broader regret research (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) finds that over the long run, people often regret **not pursuing valued possibilities** more than taking action that didn’t work perfectly.

That’s not job-specific, but it’s relevant.

### b) Career regret seems strongly tied to value incongruence
In career research, regret tends to be higher when people feel they chose against:

- what mattered most to them
- who they wanted to become
- what kind of work felt authentic

That suggests that if someone highly values intellectually meaningful work and gives it up mainly for salary, regret risk may be nontrivial.  
But if someone deeply values becoming a leader and turns down a real leadership opportunity, *that* can also become a source of regret.

So the regret literature doesn’t say “always choose interestingness.” It says more:
**people regret choices that violate core motives and values.**

---

## If I compress the literature into a simple summary

For someone in a high-skill, already-high-paying career:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
2. **Higher pay** helps, but usually less than people expect unless it materially changes life constraints.
3. **Leadership opportunity** can be a major positive if it reflects a real desire to lead, not just external prestige.
4. **Regret** is more likely when a choice conflicts with your deeper values/identity than when it merely leaves some money on the table.

---

## Applied to your exact tradeoff, in research language

The literature would probably frame your decision as a trade among **three** major predictors:

- **Intrinsic work quality**: OpenAI seems higher
- **Extrinsic reward**: Google seems somewhat higher
- **Role fit / aspiration fulfillment**: Google may be higher if leading is genuinely central for you

If the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” and your current pay is already comfortably sufficient, then research would usually predict that **losing a lot of interestingness is a real cost**, not a minor one.

The strongest research-based reason to take the Google role would be:
> the leadership component is not just a nice-to-have, but an important unmet motive that may itself create meaning, growth, and fit.

So the key empirical question is probably not:
> “money or interestingness?”

It’s:
> “Which job better satisfies the deepest drivers of my own work well-being: interesting work, or leadership/impact?”

---

## If useful, I can also do one of these next:
1. give you a **short annotated bibliography** of the main papers/meta-analyses on this  
2. turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the research**  
3. summarize what the literature says specifically about **moving from IC to manager**

answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

- **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high/comfortable.**
- **What matters most is not “interestingness” alone, but overall intrinsic fit**: interest, autonomy, meaning, learning, and whether the role matches what you want to become (for example, leading people).

So if you want the research result in one sentence: **people generally report higher satisfaction in jobs that are more intrinsically rewarding, and this effect is often stronger than the effect of incremental pay differences.** Direct research on “regret” is thinner, but the evidence we do have points the same way.

## What the research says

### 1) Job design research: interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later supported by meta-analyses.

The recurring finding is that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- **skill variety**  
- **task significance**  
- **autonomy**  
- **feedback**  
- a sense of completing meaningful pieces of work

These are very close to what people mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Reviewed work-design studies and found that motivational job characteristics were **strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower burnout**.

Interpretation: if your current role is more intellectually engaging and intrinsically rewarding day-to-day, the literature would predict that this is a major source of well-being.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is robustly associated with well-being
There is a large literature on **meaningful work**—work experienced as significant, worthwhile, or aligned with one’s values.

A meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies*  
  Found that meaningful work is associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **life satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - lower **burnout/exhaustion**

This is important because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often travel together in research settings. If the OpenAI work feels substantially more intellectually alive or mission-relevant to you, that tends to predict better outcomes.

---

### 3) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic rewards for well-being
A large literature from **Self-Determination Theory** shows that well-being at work is higher when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Relevant reviews/meta-analyses include:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**, *Journal of Organizational Behavior*  
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)**, review/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work

The result, consistently: **when work is chosen for intrinsic reasons and satisfies psychological needs, people do better than when the work is mainly about extrinsic rewards like money or status.**

That does **not** mean money/status are irrelevant. It means they usually do **not** compensate fully for a poorer daily work experience.

---

### 4) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is modest
There is a well-known tendency to overestimate how much pay differences affect job satisfaction.

A meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  Found that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

The exact effect size depends on method and controls, but the key conclusion is stable:  
**higher pay increases satisfaction somewhat, but not by nearly as much as many people assume.**

This is especially relevant in your case because both options are presumably already in the “high enough that basic financial stress is not the central issue” range. In that range, **non-monetary job features often dominate**.

Related happiness research on income:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income improved life evaluation more than daily emotional well-being, with evidence of diminishing returns.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged the idea of a hard plateau, but still supports the broader point that money has **diminishing marginal utility**, especially compared with the quality of daily experience.

So: a “bit higher salary” usually matters less for day-to-day happiness than the nature of the work itself.

---

### 5) Person-job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction
This is especially relevant because your choice is not only “interesting work vs money.”  
It is also:

- current role: more interesting research
- new role: less interesting work, but **leading a small team**, which you say you’ve always wanted

Research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** shows that when roles align with a person’s motives, values, and identity, outcomes improve substantially.

Meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Found that person-job/person-organization fit are associated with higher **job satisfaction**, higher **organizational commitment**, and lower **turnover intentions**.

This matters because **wanting to lead** is itself an intrinsic preference. So the research would not say “always choose the more technically interesting job.” It would say:

> choose the role with the stronger overall intrinsic fit.

If leading people is deeply important to you, that can offset lower content-interest.

---

### 6) People do trade money for non-monetary job quality
Labor economists often infer job preferences from **compensating differentials**: people accept lower pay for better non-wage job attributes.

The exact estimates depend on the attribute studied, but the broad result is clear:  
workers value things like autonomy, flexibility, pleasant colleagues, mission, safety, and work quality enough to **give up real money** for them.

This doesn’t prove “interesting work always wins,” but it does show that people behave as if non-monetary job qualities are economically important, not just emotionally important.

---

## What about regret?

This part is less directly studied.

There is **far more evidence on job satisfaction, well-being, burnout, turnover, and fit** than on explicit long-term regret after choosing one role over another.

But the nearest research-based conclusions are:

1. **People are less likely to want to leave jobs that are meaningful, engaging, and well-fitted.**  
   Lower turnover intention is one of the clearest findings in the literature.

2. **People often adapt faster to salary/status gains than to the daily texture of work.**  
   So a salary bump can feel important initially, but the routine of the work tends to dominate longer-run experience.

3. **A mismatch between identity and role predicts dissatisfaction.**  
   If someone moves into management mainly for pay/status and later misses hands-on technical work, that is a known risk pattern, though the evidence is more indirect than “regret” surveys.

So the best research-based proxy for regret is probably:
- job satisfaction over time
- burnout/exhaustion
- turnover intention / actual turnover
- psychological need satisfaction
- fit

On those measures, **interesting/meaningful work tends to do very well**, but **leadership roles also do well when the person genuinely wants leadership**.

---

## What the literature would likely predict in your specific situation

Without giving personal advice, the research would predict roughly this:

### If the Google role is:
- only modestly higher pay
- substantially less intellectually engaging
- more managerial/coordination work than you enjoy
- chosen mainly for compensation/status

then the literature would predict **little or no gain in happiness**, and possibly worse day-to-day satisfaction.

### If the Google role is:
- less interesting technically, **but**
- gives you substantial autonomy
- lets you lead and develop others
- fits a genuine long-term identity goal (“I’ve always wanted to lead a team”)
- provides learning and growth you value

then the literature would predict that it **could increase satisfaction**, despite being less interesting in the narrow technical sense.

That is because **leadership itself can be intrinsically rewarding** if it satisfies autonomy, competence, meaning, and identity.

---

## The main research takeaway

If I compress the evidence to the part most relevant to your dilemma:

- **Interesting work matters a lot.**
- **Meaningful work matters a lot.**
- **Autonomy matters a lot.**
- **Fit matters a lot.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than those once you’re already well-paid.**
- **Direct regret evidence is limited, but the best proxies favor intrinsic quality over marginal salary gains.**

So the research answer is not exactly:

> “Always stay in the more interesting job.”

It is closer to:

> “For long-run job satisfaction, intrinsic job quality and fit usually matter more than modest pay differences. If leadership is genuinely part of your intrinsic fit, that can be as important as technical interest.”

## Key references
- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980). Job Characteristics Model.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007). *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature*. *Journal of Applied Psychology*.  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** (2019). *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis*. *Journal of Management Studies*.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010). *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*. *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005). *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit*. *Personnel Psychology*.  
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L.** (2005). *Self-determination theory and work motivation*. *Journal of Organizational Behavior*.  
- **Van den Broeck, A.** and colleagues (review/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work).  
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A.** (2010). *High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being*. *PNAS*.  
- **Killingsworth, M. A.** (2021). *Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year*. *PNAS*.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature**—for example weighting interest, autonomy, leadership fit, pay, and long-term career capital according to what research suggests actually drives satisfaction.
