answer:
Yes — there is a fairly large research literature on this, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting work vs slightly higher salary + first management role.”

### Short version

Across organizational psychology, labor economics, and well-being research, the most consistent finding is:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” day-to-day job quality tends to matter more for sustained job satisfaction than modest differences in salary.**

And by “job quality,” researchers usually mean things like:

- how interesting the work is
- autonomy/control
- opportunities to learn and use skills
- sense of meaning/significance
- fit with your values and identity
- quality of colleagues/manager
- workload and stress

A **leadership opportunity** can also improve satisfaction — but mainly when it matches what the person actually wants and is good at. Promotions often bring **more status and pay**, but also **more stress, more coordination work, and less hands-on work**, and the happiness gains are often smaller and less durable than people expect.

So if I translate the literature into your decision frame, it is **not** really “intrinsic vs extrinsic” in a pure sense. Your choice seems more like:

- **interesting research work now**
vs
- **a leadership path you intrinsically value, but with less interesting subject matter**

Research says both of those are important, but **small salary differences by themselves are usually the weakest part of the comparison**.

---

## What the research says

## 1) Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction

A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:
  - skill variety
  - task identity
  - task significance
  - autonomy
  - feedback

This framework has held up pretty well over time.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed the work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task significance, and skill variety** are consistently related to:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher internal motivation
  - lower burnout/strain
  - better performance

So on the narrow question “Do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier at work?” the answer is broadly **yes**.

Not just because “interesting” sounds nice, but because interesting work often bundles together:
- challenge
- mastery
- learning
- agency
- a sense that your work matters

Those are very robust predictors.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation and psychological needs matter a lot

Another major framework is **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan (2000)**, and later work in organizational settings, shows that well-being and motivation improve when work supports:
  - **autonomy** — feeling you have meaningful choice/control
  - **competence** — feeling effective and growing
  - **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

This matters because “interesting work” often supports autonomy and competence.  
And “leading a team,” if it is something you genuinely want, may support competence/relatedness/meaning too.

So the literature suggests that **the deeper issue is less “salary vs interest” and more “which job better supports your core psychological needs?”**

A useful consequence of this research:
- if a leadership role is appealing mainly for title/pay/prestige, it often disappoints
- if it is appealing because you genuinely want to build, mentor, coordinate, and own outcomes through others, it can be quite satisfying

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less than most people think once you’re already well paid

This is one of the most replicated findings.

A meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. In plain English: more pay helps, but not by that much.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It matters a lot when:
- you’re underpaid
- money stress is real
- the pay difference is large
- pay affects security, lifestyle, family plans, or future options

But if the comparison is “already excellent compensation” vs “somewhat higher excellent compensation,” the literature would predict **relatively small well-being differences from salary alone**.

Related happiness research also points the same way:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** argued that emotional well-being rises with income up to a point.
- Later work, especially **Killingsworth (2021)** and follow-ups, suggests happiness may continue rising with income for many people, though the gains are not necessarily dramatic and can depend on baseline unhappiness.

For your decision, the main implication is:

**At high income levels, non-pay job attributes usually become more important, not less.**

---

## 4) Promotions and leadership roles often help less than people expect

There is research on promotions showing a mixed picture.

For example:

- **Kosteas (2011)** found that promotions tend to increase job satisfaction, but the effect is not huge and is intertwined with expectations, pay growth, and other conditions.

Other findings in this area suggest:
- promotions often increase satisfaction in the short run
- they often also increase hours, stress, role conflict, and administrative burden
- the net effect varies a lot depending on whether the person actually wants the new type of work

This is especially relevant because moving into management often changes the work itself:
- less direct problem-solving
- more meetings
- more hiring/performance/coaching/conflict resolution
- more dependence on others’ output
- less time in the technical craft that may have originally been satisfying

So the literature does **not** support a simple “promotion = happier” story.

It supports something more like:

**Promotion improves satisfaction when it increases status and income *and* the person finds the managerial work itself meaningful and fitting.**

---

## 5) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors

This may be the most important result for your situation.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that various forms of **fit** — especially **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** — are strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - lower intention to quit
  - better well-being

This means the question is not only:
- “Is the work interesting?”
or
- “Is the salary higher?”

It is also:
- “Does this role fit who I am and who I want to become?”

In your case there may be **two competing forms of fit**:
1. fit with your current identity as someone who enjoys highly interesting research
2. fit with a future identity as someone who wants to lead a team

Research strongly suggests that **misfit is costly**. People are less satisfied when their role drifts away from what they value, even if the objective status or pay rises.

---

## 6) Meaningful work predicts well-being and lower burnout

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Across studies, meaningful work is associated with:
- greater job satisfaction
- stronger engagement
- lower burnout
- stronger sense of purpose

This connects to your “interesting vs less interesting” distinction, because “interesting” often overlaps with:
- intellectually engaging
- consequential
- identity-relevant
- worth caring about

That said, people can find meaning either in:
- the content of the work itself
or
- the people impact of leading others

So again, leadership is not just an extrinsic reward; it can be a meaningful-work upgrade for some people.

---

## 7) Regret specifically: the literature is thinner

On your “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job?” question:

I’m **less aware of strong, direct longitudinal research** that asks exactly that. The evidence is much stronger on:
- job satisfaction
- well-being
- burnout
- motivation
- turnover intentions

rather than explicit long-term regret.

The closest research-based conclusions I’d draw are:

### a) People often mispredict what will make them happier
Work on **affective forecasting** suggests people tend to overweight salient factors like:
- salary
- prestige
- title
- the narrative value of a role

and underweight the quality of their actual daily experience.

That is relevant here because a job is lived day by day.  
If the work itself is dull, that cost can be persistent.

### b) People adapt to extrinsic gains
Raises and titles often produce a boost, but people partially adapt.  
Daily task content and work environment keep affecting you every week.

### c) Regret likely tracks values mismatch more than maximizing pay
The regret literature in career decisions is not as clean as the satisfaction literature, but in general, people regret choices that feel misaligned with who they are or wanted to become — not just choices that left money on the table.

---

# What seems most robust from the literature

If I compress the evidence into a few research-grounded takeaways:

### 1. A modest pay increase is usually not a strong predictor of long-term happiness
especially if you are already very well compensated.

### 2. Interesting, skill-using, autonomous work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction
probably stronger than small pay differences.

### 3. Leadership can absolutely increase satisfaction
but mostly when the person wants the **actual work of leadership**, not just the symbol of it.

### 4. “Fit” is central
If leading a team is something you deeply want, that is not a trivial factor — it is itself an intrinsic motivator.

### 5. The biggest mistake is often choosing based on the wrong unit of analysis
Research suggests people should often evaluate jobs less by:
- title
- salary delta
- prestige

and more by:
- what they will actually do all day
- whether that work supports autonomy, mastery, meaning, and fit
- whether the role increases chronic stress

---

# If you want the most evidence-based framing for your decision

A good research-based way to compare the two roles is not “which is objectively better?” but:

### Which role gives you more of the following?
- **Autonomy**
- **Competence/mastery growth**
- **Meaning/significance**
- **Person-role fit**
- **Desired identity progression**
- **Low enough chronic stress to stay engaged**
- **Day-to-day enjoyment of the actual tasks**

That framing is much closer to what predicts satisfaction in the literature than raw compensation or prestige.

---

# A compact reading list

If you want papers/books to actually look at, I’d start with:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Meta-analytic work on job design and its links to satisfaction/motivation.
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000).** Self-Determination Theory.
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).** Self-determination theory and work motivation.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** Meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Meta-analysis of person-environment fit.
- **Kosteas, V. D. (2011).** Job satisfaction and promotions.
- For broader well-being/income context: **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)**.

---

If you want, I can also do one of two things:

1. give you a **more technical literature review** with stronger citation detail and effect sizes where known, or  
2. turn this into a **research-based decision checklist** specifically for “interesting IC role vs less-interesting first management role.”

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually phrased as “interesting job vs higher-paying less interesting job.” In the literature, the closest constructs are:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** like autonomy, variety, challenge, feedback, task significance
- **person–job fit**
- versus **pay**, **promotion**, and **status**

## Short version

**The strongest overall finding is that the nature of the work itself — especially how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and fitting it is — is one of the most reliable predictors of job satisfaction and well-being.**  
**Pay matters too, but usually less than people expect, especially once pay is already high enough that financial stress is low.**

So if the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” the research would generally predict that **daily happiness and job satisfaction will depend more on whether the work feels engaging and aligned with your motives** than on the salary bump.

The main complication in your case is that **leading a team is itself an intrinsically important factor** if it is something you’ve wanted for a long time. Research would treat that not just as “status,” but as part of **person–job fit, growth, autonomy, and meaning**.

---

# What the research says

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly tied to job satisfaction

A major body of organizational psychology research says that people are more satisfied when their jobs have:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn and use strengths**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analytic work strongly supports it.

### Key result
A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback** are consistently associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal work motivation**
- better **performance**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This is one of the best-established findings in work psychology:  
**people tend to like jobs more when the work itself is engaging and meaningful.**

---

## 2) Meaningful work has a robust association with well-being

Research on **meaningful work** also shows a strong link with:

- job satisfaction
- life satisfaction
- engagement
- lower burnout / lower intention to quit

There are reviews and meta-analyses showing that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report better overall functioning.

### Practical interpretation
If by “interesting” you mean:
- intellectually engaging,
- important,
- aligned with your values,
- something you’d naturally think about even without external pressure,

then that maps closely onto the “meaningful work / intrinsic motivation” literature, and that literature is generally very favorable.

---

## 3) Pay matters — but its relationship with satisfaction is modest

There is a well-known meta-analysis on this:

### Key result
**Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

That’s important because many people intuitively assume that higher pay should strongly increase job satisfaction. In fact, the link is usually much smaller than the links for:

- work design
- autonomy
- fit
- manager quality
- meaning

So, in plain language:

**salary helps, but “the work itself” usually matters more for whether people actually like their jobs.**

This is especially true when:
- both jobs already pay very well,
- basic financial security is not at stake,
- the difference is incremental rather than life-changing.

---

## 4) Income improves life evaluation, but not always day-to-day emotional experience in the same way

Outside of job satisfaction specifically, there is the broader happiness literature.

Two well-known findings:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010):** higher income improves **life evaluation** strongly, but day-to-day **emotional well-being** rises less and may level off.
- **Killingsworth (2021):** argued that experienced well-being continues rising with income, even above earlier thresholds.

These papers differ on whether there is a plateau, but they agree on something useful for your question:

### Useful takeaway
Income does matter for well-being, but it is **not the whole story**, and for many people the **quality of daily experience** matters a lot.  
Work is a major part of daily experience.

So if one job is much more interesting, that can affect you **every day**, not just abstractly.

---

## 5) Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

A very relevant literature here is **person–environment fit**: how well a job matches your motives, values, strengths, and preferences.

### Key result
A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that **person–job fit** and related forms of fit are strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit

This matters a lot for your situation because the tradeoff is not just:

- “interesting work” vs “more money”

It may actually be:

- **deeply interesting individual-contributor research**
vs
- **leadership/growth/impact that fits a long-standing aspiration**

From the literature’s perspective, the key question is:
> Which role is the better fit for your enduring motives?

If leading a small team is something you genuinely care about, that is not a trivial extra. It is part of fit.

---

## 6) Autonomy and competence are especially important

**Self-Determination Theory** is also relevant here. It says that well-being and motivation are higher when work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

The work literature broadly supports this.

### Why this matters for your choice
A job can be “interesting” because it supports competence and curiosity.  
A leadership role can be fulfilling because it supports autonomy, influence, and growth.

So the literature would not say “always choose the most technically interesting job.”  
It would say:

> choose the role that better satisfies your deeper psychological needs.

For some people that is research depth.  
For others it is building and leading.

---

## 7) Promotions and leadership can increase satisfaction — but only when they fit

There isn’t a simple universal result that “management makes people happier.”

Leadership roles often bring:

- more influence
- more status
- more growth
- more responsibility
- but also more role conflict, meetings, people problems, and stress

So the empirical pattern is mixed.

What predicts satisfaction better than “being a manager” per se is usually:

- whether the role matches your motivations,
- whether you feel competent in it,
- how much autonomy/support you have,
- and whether the added responsibility crowds out the part of work you actually enjoy.

So the fact that the Google role is “less interesting” is not automatically decisive, because **leading a team may itself be meaningful to you**. But the literature would warn against assuming that title/status alone will compensate for boring work.

---

# What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base for your exact question.

There is **less direct research** on:
> Do people regret staying in a more interesting job rather than taking a more lucrative or managerial one?

But a few regret findings are relevant.

## 1) Long-term regrets are often about missed opportunities and unlived selves

Classic regret research, especially **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**, suggests that:

- in the short term, people often regret actions
- in the long term, people more often regret **inactions** or missed opportunities

And **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found that regret is strongest in domains tied to one’s ideals and possibilities for a better life.

### How that maps here
This does **not** straightforwardly say “take Google” or “stay at OpenAI.”

It says something more subtle:

People often regret choices that feel like:
- “I didn’t pursue something that really mattered to me”
- “I drifted away from what I actually wanted”
- “I optimized the wrong metric”

So the likely regret driver is not salary alone.  
It is more often **mismatch with values, identity, and desired growth**.

That means either of these could later become regrettable:

- staying, if you later feel you avoided leadership despite wanting it badly
- leaving, if you later feel you traded away unusually engaging work for a role that didn’t fit

Research cannot resolve that for you directly, but it does suggest that **regret is more about self-congruence than money**.

---

# A research-based synthesis

If I compress the evidence:

## What predicts job happiness most reliably?
Usually:
1. **interesting/meaningful work**
2. **autonomy**
3. **fit with your motives and strengths**
4. **good colleagues/manager**
5. **fair pay**
6. **status/title**

Not always in that exact order, but that’s the general pattern.

## What does this imply for your case?
A modest salary increase alone would usually **not** be expected to outweigh a substantial drop in work interest.

But the leadership piece is important, because it is not “just compensation.” It may represent:
- growth,
- autonomy,
- identity,
- long-term career fit.

So the literature suggests the real comparison is:

### Option A
Higher daily engagement from the work itself

### Option B
Higher alignment with a long-standing leadership aspiration, plus somewhat more pay

That is a much closer empirical tradeoff.

---

# If you want the safest research-backed summary

Here’s the cleanest version:

- **Yes:** people in more interesting, meaningful, autonomous jobs generally do report **higher job satisfaction and well-being**.
- **Yes:** these factors often matter **more than salary differences**, especially once both options are already financially comfortable.
- **No strong evidence:** there is not a clean body of research saying people systematically regret choosing interesting work over somewhat higher pay.
- **Better-supported regret conclusion:** long-term regret tends to track **lack of alignment with one’s values/ideal self**, not simply lower compensation.

---

# References / names worth looking up

If you want to read the research directly, these are good starting points:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design and job outcomes  
- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person–environment fit  
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — income and well-being  
- **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and experienced well-being  
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — action vs inaction regret over time  
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — domains of greatest regret

If you want, I can also give you a **more formal literature-style summary** with 8–12 papers and a one-line takeaway from each.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly robust research base on this, and the broad result is:

**“Interesting/meaningful work” tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly than pay differences do, especially once compensation is already high enough that basic financial needs and comfort are met.**

A few important nuances:
- Research usually does **not** measure “interesting” in exactly your everyday sense. It operationalizes it as things like **intrinsic motivation, skill variety, challenge, autonomy, learning, task significance, and meaningfulness**.
- There is **much better evidence on happiness/satisfaction than on later regret**. The regret literature is thinner and less directly targeted to choices like yours.

## 1) What the literature most consistently finds

### A. Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of satisfaction
A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

The consistent finding:
- Jobs with more **autonomy**
- more **skill variety**
- more **task significance**
- clearer **feedback**
- and more **meaningfulness**

are associated with substantially higher:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- and often lower **turnover intentions**

These relationships are usually **moderate to fairly large** by behavioral-science standards.

In plain English:  
If your day-to-day work is more intellectually engaging and meaningful, that tends to matter a lot.

---

### B. Meaningful work has a strong relationship with well-being
There are meta-analyses on **meaningful work** showing that it is strongly associated with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **psychological well-being**

For example, work like **Allan et al.** on outcomes of meaningful work found that meaning at work is not just a “nice extra”; it is one of the more powerful correlates of how good people feel about their work lives.

A rough summary of the literature:
- **Meaningful work ↔ job satisfaction**: often a **moderate-to-strong** relationship
- **Meaningful work ↔ life satisfaction/well-being**: usually **moderate**

So yes: on average, people in work they find more meaningful/interesting do tend to report being happier with their jobs, and often happier overall.

---

### C. Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is smaller than many people expect
There are meta-analyses on **pay and job satisfaction** — e.g. **Judge et al.**, **Williams et al.** — and the relationship is usually **positive but modest**.

The rough pattern is:
- Higher pay is associated with higher satisfaction,
- but the effect is **much smaller** than many people intuit,
- and it tends to show **diminishing returns**, especially at higher income levels.

This fits the broader well-being literature:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that emotional well-being rises with income up to a point and then flattens somewhat.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found a more continuously increasing relationship, though still with diminishing practical importance compared with many daily-life factors.

Those papers are about income and well-being generally, not job choice specifically. But taken together with job-satisfaction research, the message is:

**A modest salary increase usually does less for day-to-day work happiness than changes in the nature of the work itself.**

---

## 2) What about leadership roles?

This is where your case gets more nuanced.

A leadership role is not just “more status” or “more money.” It can change several psychologically important things:

### Leadership can increase:
- **autonomy**
- sense of **impact**
- **growth**
- **status**
- opportunities for **mentoring/building**
- sometimes **meaning**

### But leadership can also reduce:
- time spent on the actually interesting technical work
- hands-on problem solving
- flow states
- perceived competence (if the work becomes political/managerial rather than technical)
- energy, due to more meetings/conflict/responsibility

The research on promotion/managerial roles is therefore **mixed**:
- Managers often report **higher job satisfaction on average**, partly because of autonomy and status.
- But they also report **higher stress, longer hours, and more emotional demands**.
- Promotion gains often show **adaptation**: the boost in satisfaction can fade.

So the literature would not say “leadership always wins” or “interesting IC work always wins.”  
It would say:

**Leadership increases happiness mainly when it also preserves or increases autonomy, competence, meaning, and fit.**

That maps closely to **Self-Determination Theory**:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

These are repeatedly shown to predict well-being at work. If a leadership role satisfies those needs, it can be very rewarding. If it mostly replaces interesting work with coordination overhead, it often is not.

---

## 3) What about regret specifically?

Here the evidence is weaker and less direct.

### What we do know:
#### A. People are often bad at affective forecasting
A robust line of work from **Gilbert, Wilson**, and others shows that people often mispredict what will make them happy.

Common forecasting errors:
- overweighting **salary/status**
- underweighting the texture of **everyday experience**
- underestimating **adaptation**

That matters here because:
- salary/title are salient,
- but your actual happiness is heavily driven by **what you do all day**.

This research does **not** prove that taking the more interesting job avoids regret, but it does suggest that people often undervalue day-to-day intrinsic experience relative to prestige or pay.

#### B. Person-job fit predicts satisfaction and lower turnover
Meta-analytic work on **person-job fit / person-environment fit** (e.g. **Kristof-Brown et al., 2005**) shows that better fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to leave

A role that is “less interesting” for you is often, in research terms, a worse fit. Worse fit tends to predict more dissatisfaction later.

#### C. Direct “later regret” evidence is limited
I’m not aware of a strong body of direct longitudinal research saying:

> “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership job later regretted it less.”

That exact question has not, to my knowledge, been studied nearly as cleanly as job satisfaction itself.

So the strongest research-based statement is:
- **There is good evidence that more interesting/meaningful work predicts greater satisfaction.**
- **There is weaker direct evidence about regret.**

---

## 4) A useful quantitative intuition from the literature

If you force a comparison:

### Usually stronger predictors of job satisfaction:
- interesting/challenging work
- autonomy
- meaningfulness
- learning/growth
- fit with your interests/identity
- quality of manager and colleagues

### Usually weaker predictors:
- pay level alone
- title alone
- prestige alone

That does **not** mean pay/title don’t matter.  
It means that, for well-paid knowledge workers especially, they are often **not the main determinants of day-to-day happiness**.

---

## 5) One important caution: “interesting work” can have downsides too
The meaningful-work literature also has a nuance worth noting:

People who view work as highly meaningful or as a “calling” often report:
- more satisfaction,
- but also sometimes
- more willingness to overwork,
- more identity fusion with work,
- and vulnerability to burnout or exploitation.

So “interesting” is not automatically better if it comes with:
- chronic overwork,
- poor boundaries,
- or low career development in areas you deeply value.

That matters because in your case the leadership opportunity is something you “always wanted.”  
If leading a team is itself intrinsically meaningful to you, then it is not merely an extrinsic factor. It may be part of your deeper work motivation.

---

## 6) The best research-based synthesis for your situation

If I strip this down to the most defensible conclusion from the literature:

### The evidence would generally predict:
- A move from **very interesting work** to **less interesting work** for only a **modest salary increase** is **unlikely, on average, to improve happiness**.
- However, if the new role gives you a kind of **leadership, autonomy, growth, and impact** that you deeply value, then the tradeoff is more balanced, because those are also strong well-being predictors.

So this is probably **not** best framed as:
- “interesting work” vs “money”

It is better framed as:
- **interesting technical work**
vs
- **leadership/autonomy/impact/growth**

Research says both can matter a lot.  
The key question is which set will dominate your **daily experience**.

---

## 7) Short answer to your direct questions

### “Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?”
**On average, yes.**  
Research on intrinsic job characteristics, meaningful work, and person-job fit strongly suggests that more interesting/meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction and often better overall well-being.

### “Or do they regret that?”
**There is much less direct evidence on regret.**  
But the broader literature on affective forecasting suggests people often **undervalue daily intrinsic experience** and **overvalue salary/status/title**, which implies that choosing the more interesting work may be less regrettable than people anticipate.

---

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **short reading list** of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **research-based decision framework** using only variables that are well-supported in the literature.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**The day-to-day quality of the work itself — especially interest, meaning, autonomy, skill use, and fit — is usually a stronger predictor of job satisfaction than pay alone.**

A few relevant findings:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **person-job fit**

### Classic result: Job Characteristics Model
**Hackman & Oldham (1970s)** proposed that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

That model has held up well.

### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis, **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, found that work design characteristics like autonomy, task variety, social support, and feedback have **moderate to strong associations** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout / lower withdrawal

So: if by “interesting” you mean *intellectually engaging, varied, meaningful, with autonomy*, the literature says that matters a lot.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a substantial literature on **meaningful work**.

### Meta-analysis
A meta-analysis by **Allan and colleagues** (late 2010s) found that meaningful work is associated with:

- **higher job satisfaction**
- **higher life satisfaction**
- **higher work engagement**
- **greater organizational commitment**
- **lower turnover intentions**

The association with job satisfaction is not tiny; it is generally in the **moderate-to-large** range by psychology standards.

So on your direct question:

> Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes** — especially when “interesting” overlaps with meaningfulness, autonomy, and skill use.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less than people often think for job satisfaction

Pay absolutely matters, especially for:

- financial security
- perceived fairness
- status
- optionality

But the link between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is usually **positive but modest**.

### Meta-analytic result
Work by **Judge and colleagues** found that the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **real but fairly small** relative to what many people expect.

Roughly speaking:
- higher pay tends to make people **somewhat** more satisfied
- but it explains much less of job satisfaction than intrinsic/job-design factors

This does **not** mean money is unimportant.
It means that when comparing two already-good jobs, the marginal happiness gain from salary is often smaller than the gain/loss from changing the nature of the work.

That is especially true when the pay difference is “a bit higher salary” rather than “one option solves a real financial constraint.”

---

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to be better for well-being than extrinsic rewards alone

A lot of this comes from **Self-Determination Theory** (**Deci & Ryan**).

The theory — and a large body of evidence around it — says people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that are interesting and self-directed tend to satisfy these needs better than jobs chosen mainly for extrinsic rewards.

Related literature on **intrinsic vs. extrinsic goals** (e.g. **Kasser & Ryan**) also finds that stronger emphasis on intrinsic aims like growth, interest, contribution, and mastery is generally linked to better well-being than emphasis on extrinsic aims like money/status alone.

---

## 5) Fit matters a lot — and wanting to lead is not “just extrinsic”

This is important in your case.

You describe two pulls:

1. **more interesting work** at OpenAI  
2. **leading a small team**, which you say you’ve always wanted

Research would not treat (2) as merely status or compensation. It may reflect a genuine psychological motive.

### Person-job / person-environment fit
Meta-analytic work by **Kristof-Brown and colleagues (2005)** shows that better fit between the person and the role predicts:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intention to quit

So if managing a small team is something you deeply value, that is a **fit variable**, not just a perk.

That means the research answer is not simply:
> “interesting work beats everything”

It’s more like:
> “interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction, but so does fit — and if leadership is a core aspiration, that can be a major source of satisfaction too.”

---

## 6) For scientists/researchers specifically, there is evidence people accept less pay for interesting work

There is some especially relevant economics research here.

### “Do scientists pay to be scientists?”
**Stern (2004)** found that scientists often accept lower wages for jobs that offer the opportunity to do the kind of science they value — e.g. research freedom, publication, intellectually meaningful work.

That is a form of revealed preference:
people are not just saying they value interesting work; they often **trade money for it**.

This is one of the cleaner pieces of evidence relevant to research/engineering careers.

---

## 7) On “regret”: the evidence is less direct, but points in a similar direction

There is much less clean research on **career regret** specifically than on job satisfaction and well-being.

But the pattern from adjacent literatures is:

- choices aligned with **intrinsic interests and fit** tend to produce more durable satisfaction
- gains from **salary and status** often show more adaptation than people predict
- promotions/leadership roles can improve satisfaction **if they match the person**, but can also add stress and reduce enjoyment if the daily work becomes less intrinsically rewarding

So the best-supported statement is:

**There is strong evidence on current and longer-term satisfaction, weaker evidence on regret specifically.**
And that evidence generally favors:
- meaningful/interesting work
- autonomy
- fit
over
- modest pay differences alone

---

## If I had to compress the literature into one sentence

**A modestly higher salary usually does less for job satisfaction than people expect; the everyday nature of the work and the fit between the role and your motives usually matter more.**

But in your case, **“leading a small team” may itself be part of that fit**, so the key tradeoff is probably not:

- interesting work vs money

but rather:

- **interesting IC work** vs **less interesting work that fulfills a real leadership motive**

Research supports both sides of that tradeoff more than it supports salary alone.

---

## Rough practical ranking from the literature

If the salary difference is not life-changing, the variables most likely to matter for satisfaction are probably:

1. **How much you enjoy the day-to-day work**
2. **Autonomy / control**
3. **Whether the role fits your motives and identity**
4. **Meaning / significance**
5. **Manager quality and team quality**
6. **Compensation**, after it clears your threshold of fairness/security

That ordering is broadly consistent with organizational psychology research.

---

## Key references you could look up

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Deci & Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory  
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis

answer:
Yes. The literature is not perfectly causal, but the broad result is quite consistent:

**Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than pay level does, once pay is already “good enough.”**

A few of the most relevant research streams:

## 1) “Interesting work” is a major predictor of satisfaction

### Job characteristics research
Classic work by **Hackman & Oldham** and later meta-analyses found that jobs with more:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

produce higher **internal motivation** and **job satisfaction**.

Two often-cited summaries:
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** meta-analysis
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** meta-analysis on work design

The practical takeaway: the **nature of the work itself** is not a soft factor; it is one of the central determinants of how satisfying a job feels.

## 2) Intrinsic motivation matters a lot

### Self-Determination Theory
A large literature around **Deci & Ryan** shows that people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy** (“I choose / endorse what I’m doing”)
- **competence** (“I’m growing / effective”)
- **relatedness** (“I feel connected / useful to others”)

When work is intrinsically motivating, people tend to report:
- higher engagement
- higher well-being
- lower burnout
- better persistence

This is one of the strongest reasons “interesting work” tends to matter so much: it often directly supports autonomy and competence.

## 3) Meaningful work predicts happiness and commitment

There is also a substantial literature on **meaningful work** showing associations with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- lower turnover intention

This literature overlaps with “interesting work,” but they’re not identical. A job can be fascinating but not meaningful, or meaningful but not fascinating. The best outcomes are usually when both are present.

## 4) Salary matters, but less than most people expect

A very relevant paper here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**

Their main conclusion: **pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but only modestly**.

That result is important because many people intuitively expect salary to dominate. In the data, it usually doesn’t.

Two nuances:
- **Pay fairness / perceived fairness** often matters more than absolute pay.
- Pay matters much more when it changes **security**, debt, family stress, or status comparisons.

If both offers already put you well into a comfortable range, the marginal happiness gain from “a bit higher salary” is usually not large.

## 5) Intrinsic goals tend to outperform extrinsic goals

Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and related work on intrinsic vs. extrinsic values finds that people who orient more toward:
- growth
- relationships
- community
- meaning

tend to have better well-being than people who orient more toward:
- money
- status
- image

This does **not** mean money is unimportant. It means that, beyond a baseline of adequacy, building your life around extrinsic rewards tends to be less satisfying than building it around intrinsically rewarding activity.

## 6) Fit matters a lot

A key meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-environment fit**

Good fit between the person and the job/organization predicts:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower turnover intention

This is especially relevant in your case, because your choice is **not just** “interesting work vs money.”

It is also:
- individual contributor research vs leading a small team
- domain interest vs managerial aspiration

If leading a team is something you genuinely want, that is **not merely extrinsic**. It may reflect a real preference and better fit.

So the tradeoff is not necessarily:
- intrinsic good vs extrinsic good

It may be:
- one kind of intrinsic reward (interesting research)
vs
- another kind of intrinsic reward (leadership, responsibility, growing others)

That distinction matters.

## 7) On regret specifically: less direct evidence, but some clues

There is less clean research on the exact question:
> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-status/higher-pay job?”

I don’t know of a famous definitive study answering that exact tradeoff cleanly.

But related literatures suggest:

### Affective forecasting errors
People are often bad at predicting what will make them happy. Work by **Gilbert**, **Wilson**, and **Kahneman** suggests people tend to overweight salient, easy-to-compare factors like:
- pay
- title
- prestige

and underweight:
- day-to-day experience
- how the work actually feels
- whether they enjoy the activities that fill most of the week

That implies people may systematically overvalue compensation/title and undervalue the quality of daily work.

### Regret tends to be linked to misfit more than to “not maximizing money”
In the career literature, regret is more often tied to:
- feeling stuck
- lack of growth
- poor fit
- value conflict
- burnout

than to “I should have optimized salary a bit more,” especially among already high earners.

## What the evidence would predict in a case like yours

If I translate the research into a cautious prediction:

### If both jobs are already financially very good
the literature would generally predict **more durable satisfaction** from the role that offers more:
- intrinsic interest
- autonomy
- learning
- meaning
- fit with your preferred daily activities

than from one that is only somewhat better on pay.

### The main exception
If **leading a team** is itself a deeply important aspiration for you, and not just a resume/status marker, then the Google option may score high on another powerful predictor of well-being: **person-job fit**.

In other words, the strongest empirical case *for* taking Google is not “the salary is higher.”
It is:
- “I strongly want to manage and develop people”
- “I would regret delaying leadership experience”
- “the day-to-day manager work would actually be satisfying to me”

If that is true, then the choice becomes much closer.

## A practical evidence-based summary

### Research leans toward:
1. **Interesting work matters a lot**
2. **Meaning/autonomy/fit matter a lot**
3. **Salary matters, but usually modestly once you’re already well paid**
4. **People often overweight salary/title relative to daily experience**
5. **If leadership is genuinely important to your identity, that can outweigh domain-interest differences**

## A compact way to think about it

The research suggests you should probably compare the jobs on these dimensions:

- **How enjoyable are the actual daily tasks?**
- **How much autonomy do I have?**
- **How much learning/growth is there?**
- **How meaningful/important does the work feel?**
- **How strong is the fit with who I want to become?**
- **Does the pay difference materially change my life, or is it just “more”?**

That last question is especially important. The literature treats “financially transformative” very differently from “slightly higher.”

---

If you want, I can also turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** with weights drawn from the literature, rather than giving you generic career advice.

answer:
Yes. The broad research result is:

**On average, intrinsic job quality**—work that is interesting, meaningful, gives autonomy, uses your skills, and lets you learn—**is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained well-being at work.**  
**Pay matters too, but its link to job satisfaction is usually much smaller**, especially once income is already high enough that basic needs and financial stress are not the main issue.

A few important details:

## 1) “Interesting work” is not usually studied under that exact label
Researchers usually break it into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety**
- **skill use / challenge**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **person-job fit** (does the role fit your interests and strengths?)

Those variables are very consistently related to satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout.

---

## 2) Key findings from the literature

### A. Job design / intrinsic features matter a lot
A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and a major meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design research.

Their general finding:  
**motivational characteristics of work**—especially autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback—show **moderate to strong positive relationships** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- performance
- lower withdrawal/turnover

In practical terms: when people’s day-to-day work is more inherently engaging and meaningful, they tend to be substantially happier with their jobs.

---

### B. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There are also meta-analyses on **meaningful work**. Across studies, meaningful work tends to show **moderate-to-large positive associations** with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- lower distress / burnout

The exact effect sizes vary by study, but the pattern is very robust:  
**people who experience their work as meaningful or worthwhile report better overall work-related well-being.**

---

### C. Pay has a positive but comparatively modest relationship with job satisfaction
A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** on **pay level and job satisfaction**

The headline result is that the correlation between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with their job** is **positive but small**—roughly around **r ≈ .15**.

That means:

- money is **not irrelevant**
- but it is **far from the main driver** of whether people like their jobs

This is one of the clearest findings relevant to your question.  
Compared with intrinsic job features, **salary level usually explains much less variance in job satisfaction**.

---

### D. Person-job fit matters a lot
Another strong line of evidence:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on fit

When people feel their job matches their interests, values, and abilities, that is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions

This is especially relevant in your case because “interesting work” is often really a **fit** question:  
“How much does this role match what I naturally want to spend my time doing?”

---

### E. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
A large literature in motivation psychology, especially **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci, Ryan, and others), finds that work is better for well-being when it supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have agency
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected/respected

Jobs that are “interesting” often satisfy autonomy and competence more strongly.  
That tends to predict:

- greater intrinsic motivation
- better performance quality
- less burnout
- higher job satisfaction

---

## 3) What about promotions / leadership specifically?
This is where your case gets more subtle.

Research on promotions suggests:

- promotions often increase **status** and sometimes **job satisfaction**
- but they also often increase **stress**, **hours**, and **responsibility**
- the boost in overall well-being is often **smaller and less lasting than people expect**

There is panel-data research suggesting that promotions can improve some work attitudes while also increasing mental strain, with limited durable gains in overall life satisfaction.

So the evidence does **not** support a simple rule like “take the leadership role; you’ll be happier.”  
It depends a lot on whether **leading people is itself intrinsically rewarding for you**, not just a status goal.

That distinction matters:
- If team leadership fulfills a real long-term desire for autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth, it may be intrinsically valuable.
- If it mainly sounds good in the abstract, but removes you from the kind of work you actually enjoy, it may not improve well-being much.

---

## 4) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?
This is the part where the evidence is weaker.

There is **much less direct research on “regret”** framed exactly as:

> “I chose the more interesting job over the better-paid, higher-status one—do I regret it later?”

What researchers measure much more often is:

- later job satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- engagement
- life satisfaction

Using those outcomes as the best proxy, the evidence leans toward:

**People who choose jobs with better intrinsic fit and more motivating work conditions tend to do better on long-run satisfaction-related measures.**

So while I wouldn’t claim there is a large literature specifically on *career regret* in this exact tradeoff, the closest available evidence points in the same direction: **intrinsic job quality tends to age well**.

---

## 5) How strong is this compared with money?
A fair summary of the literature is:

### If pay is already “good enough”:
- differences in **interest, autonomy, meaning, and fit** usually matter **more** for day-to-day job satisfaction than modest pay differences.

### If pay changes financial security materially:
- money matters more, because it reduces stress and increases options.

Your phrase **“a bit higher salary”** is important.  
Research would generally suggest that a **modest salary increase** is less likely to affect happiness than a **large difference in daily work quality**.

---

## 6) The best research-based interpretation for your situation
Not personal advice—just translating the literature.

Your decision is not really:

- **interesting work** vs **money**

It is more like:

- **current role:** high intrinsic interest  
vs
- **Google role:** somewhat higher pay + leadership opportunity + lower topic-interest

Research suggests all of these are real sources of utility, but **daily intrinsic work quality** is usually one of the biggest ones.

The most important empirical question is likely:

> Which option gives you the better day-to-day bundle of autonomy, competence, growth, meaning, and fit?

If leading a team is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, that could be a major intrinsic benefit, not just an extrinsic one.  
So the relevant comparison is not “interesting work beats money”; it is:

- **interesting technical work**
vs
- **less interesting domain, but more leadership/impact/autonomy**

Research strongly favors whichever role better satisfies those deeper intrinsic needs over time.

---

## 7) Bottom line
If I compress the literature into one sentence:

**Yes—people in work they find intrinsically interesting/meaningful generally are happier with their jobs, and that relationship is usually stronger than the relationship between salary level and job satisfaction.**

And on regret:

**Direct regret evidence is sparse, but the long-run satisfaction literature points more toward “good intrinsic fit pays off” than toward “you’ll wish you had taken the slightly higher pay.”**

---

## Representative papers / literatures to look at
If you want to read the actual research, these are good starting points:

1. **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model  
   Classic theory on autonomy, variety, significance, feedback.

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary…*  
   Big meta-analysis showing strong links from work design to satisfaction and motivation.

3. **Judge et al. (2010)**  
   Meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction**  
   Key finding: positive but relatively small association.

4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis on **person-job / person-organization fit**  
   Strong links to satisfaction and retention.

5. **Self-Determination Theory at work**  
   Reviews by **Deci, Ryan, Gagné**, and others on autonomy/competence/relatedness in workplace motivation.

6. **Meta-analyses on meaningful work**  
   Work by **Allan** and colleagues is a good entry point.

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **short annotated bibliography** with the most relevant papers, or  
2. turn this literature into a **decision framework** specific to your situation without adding personal opinion.

answer:
Yes. There isn’t a single clean study of “interesting job vs somewhat higher salary + first management role,” but several research literatures point in a fairly consistent direction.

## Short version

If pay is already comfortably above your needs, **the day-to-day quality of the work** — how interesting it is, how much autonomy/skill use it gives, whether it feels meaningful, and whether it fits what you want your career to be — tends to be a **stronger and more durable predictor of job satisfaction and well-being** than modest differences in salary.

At the same time, **person-role fit** matters a lot: if leading a team is something you genuinely want, then a management role can improve well-being even if the subject matter is less interesting. But promotions/leadership seem to produce **smaller and less lasting happiness gains than people often expect**, partly because people adapt and partly because stress/workload rise.

Direct “regret” evidence is relatively thin. The best indirect evidence suggests people often **overweight salary/status/promotion** and **underweight daily experience**.

---

## What the research says

### 1) “Interesting work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction
Researchers usually don’t measure “interesting” directly; they break it into things like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/master**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1970s), and later meta-analyses support it.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a meta-analysis of work design research, found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback** had moderate to strong associations with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **internal work motivation**
  - **engagement/performance-related outcomes**

In plain English: jobs that are richer and more intrinsically rewarding tend to make people like their jobs more, not just perform better.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Allan et al. (2019)** on meaningful work found that people who experience their work as meaningful report:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - higher **life satisfaction / general well-being**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - lower **psychological distress / burnout**, on average

The association with **job satisfaction** is typically quite strong by social-science standards.

So if “interesting” for you also means “intellectually meaningful” or “aligned with what I care about,” the evidence is especially favorable.

---

### 3) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors
Another important literature is **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis found that better fit is strongly associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **organizational commitment**
  - lower **intention to quit**

This matters a lot in your case, because the decision is not only “interesting research vs more money.” It is also:

- **IC/research role** vs **team leadership role**
- “what I enjoy doing” vs “what I want to grow into”

If you truly want to manage, then a leadership role may fit your deeper preferences better even if the technical content is less interesting. The research would say: **fit can matter as much as intrinsic task interest**.

---

### 4) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction
This is important because people sometimes treat job choice as isolated from overall happiness.

Across multiple studies/meta-analyses, **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately strongly correlated** — often around **r ≈ .4** (ballpark, depending on the study). That’s quite substantial for social science.

So choosing the more satisfying job is not “just about work”; it often affects overall well-being.

There is some debate about causality direction, but longitudinal evidence suggests it goes **both ways**:
- happier people evaluate work more positively,
- and better work conditions improve broader well-being.

---

### 5) Salary matters, but mostly with diminishing returns
The evidence here is nuanced but fairly consistent:

- Higher income is associated with higher well-being.
- But the marginal benefit tends to **shrink** as income rises.
- People also adapt to income increases.

Classic references:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found strong effects of income on life evaluation, but less clear continuing gains for day-to-day emotional well-being beyond a certain point.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found continuing gains at higher incomes, but still with a roughly **logarithmic** pattern — meaning each extra dollar matters less than the previous one.

For a decision like yours, the key takeaway is usually:
- If the salary difference is between **“financially constrained” and “financially comfortable,”** it can matter a lot.
- If it’s **“already very comfortable” vs “a bit more comfortable,”** the well-being effect is often smaller than people predict.

That does **not** mean salary is unimportant. It means its effect is often weaker than daily job quality once basic security/fairness is satisfied.

---

### 6) Promotions and management often help less than people expect
There is research on **promotions** showing that they do improve some things — especially pay satisfaction and status — but the happiness boost is often **temporary**.

A useful study:
- **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma (2012)**, using panel data, found that promotions tend to increase **job satisfaction in the short term**, but much of that effect fades over time.

Common pattern:
- short-run bump from status/pay/novelty
- adaptation over time
- potential offset from longer hours, more stress, more administrative work

That fits a broader well-being finding: people often **adapt** to extrinsic gains faster than they expect.

So the literature would not say “team leadership is bad.” It would say:
- if you want to lead because you genuinely enjoy developing people, setting direction, making decisions, coordinating, hiring, etc., it may be satisfying;
- if you want it mainly because it feels like “the next step,” the well-being payoff may be smaller and more short-lived.

---

### 7) Basic psychological needs at work matter a lot
Self-determination theory is another relevant line.

It says well-being is higher when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Reviews/meta-analyses in this tradition consistently find that when those needs are met at work, people report:
- higher engagement
- higher job satisfaction
- better well-being
- lower burnout

This maps closely onto “interesting research work” for many people:
- autonomy: freedom in how to work
- competence: using/stretching skills
- relatedness: being around people you respect

A management role can also satisfy these needs — but only if the actual job gives them. Some management roles reduce autonomy and competence feelings, especially if they involve lots of coordination and politics without enough genuine influence.

---

### 8) Low-quality work can hurt mental health even if it’s “better on paper”
More severe evidence comes from job-quality/mental-health research:
- poor psychosocial work conditions — low control, high strain, insecurity, mismatch — are reliably linked to worse mental health
- in some longitudinal studies, **bad jobs are not much better than unemployment** for mental health, and can sometimes be worse than expected

That’s not your situation exactly, but it supports the general point: **the quality of the work itself matters a lot**, not just title or compensation.

---

## What about regret specifically?

This is the least well-studied part in a direct sense.

I’m not aware of a strong literature saying, in a clean causal way, “people who choose the more interesting job later regret it less.” The evidence is more indirect:

### What we do know:
1. **People adapt** to higher pay and promotion/status.
2. **Daily experience** is a major driver of long-run job satisfaction.
3. People are prone to **focusing illusions**:
   - when choosing, they overweight salient differences like salary, title, prestige
   - and underweight what daily life in the role will actually feel like

That last point comes from broader judgment/decision research associated with **Kahneman and colleagues**.

So if you’re asking, “What do people systematically mispredict?” the research answer is roughly:
- they often **overpredict** the long-run happiness from extra money/status
- and **underpredict** the impact of everyday task content, autonomy, and fit

That is probably the closest research-backed answer to your “regret” question.

---

## One important caveat: “interesting/meaningful” jobs can also exploit people
There is also literature on **calling** and overcommitment suggesting that highly meaningful work can have downsides:

- people tolerate worse pay or conditions
- they may overwork
- boundaries can erode
- burnout risk can rise

So “interesting” is not automatically better if it comes with:
- chronic overwork
- poor manager
- weak advancement
- lack of autonomy
- unstable environment

In other words, the evidence favors interesting/meaningful work **conditional on the job also being healthy and sustainable**.

---

## If I compress the literature into a few practical propositions

These are evidence-based summaries, not personal advice:

1. **A modest salary increase is usually less important for long-run happiness than people think**, especially at already high incomes.
2. **Intrinsic job qualities** — interesting work, autonomy, learning, meaning — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
3. **Person-role fit matters enormously.** If you really want to become a manager, that can outweigh lower task interest.
4. **Promotions often give a temporary boost**, not a durable one, unless the new role fits what you actually like doing.
5. People often **regret choices made for salient extrinsic reasons** more than choices made for good day-to-day fit — not because of a direct regret study, but because of adaptation + forecasting errors.

---

## The research-based questions that matter most in your specific choice

If you want to use the literature rather than intuition, the key variables to compare are probably:

- **How much more money is “a bit higher”?**
  - life-changing, or just nice?
- **Which role gives more autonomy?**
- **Which role gives more skill use / mastery / learning?**
- **Which role feels more meaningful?**
- **Which role better fits your identity 3 years from now?**
  - researcher
  - research lead / manager
- **Do you want to lead a team, or do you want the option/status of leading a team?**
- **What is the expected workload/stress?**
- **Who would your manager be, and how good are they?**
- **How reversible is the choice?**
  - If management is easy to try and reversible, the research on fit suggests that lowers downside.

---

## If you want specific papers to look up

A good starting list would be:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-job/person-organization fit meta-analysis  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meaningful work meta-analysis  
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — income and well-being  
- **Killingsworth (2021)** — experienced well-being and income  
- **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma (2012)** — promotions and job satisfaction over time  
- reviews/meta-analyses on **self-determination theory at work** and **basic psychological needs**

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-page research summary with citations and the most robust effect sizes**, or I can make a **decision checklist grounded in the literature** rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly strong research literature suggesting that **the intrinsic quality of work** (interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich, challenging, learning-oriented) is usually a **stronger and more durable predictor of job satisfaction and well-being** than **pay differences** alone.

The short version is:

- **Interesting/meaningful work:** strongly linked to higher job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover.
- **Pay:** matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is usually **modest**.
- **Promotion/leadership:** often gives a **short-term boost**, but the well-being gains are less consistent and can come with more stress.
- **Best predictor:** not “prestige vs salary vs interest” in isolation, but whether the role fits your **intrinsic motivations and values**.

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A lot of this comes from two major traditions in organizational psychology:

### A. Job Characteristics Model
Classic work by **Hackman & Oldham** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These features are closely related to what people informally call “interesting work.”

Later meta-analyses, especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, found that these kinds of job-design variables have **substantial associations** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

In practical terms: jobs that are more varied, autonomous, significant, and feedback-rich tend to make people happier at work.

### B. Meaningful work / intrinsic motivation
There is also a large literature showing that **meaningful work** is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- higher life satisfaction
- lower burnout
- lower desire to quit

Meta-analytic work in this area — e.g. **Allan and colleagues** — generally finds that meaningful work is not a small “nice-to-have”; it is one of the stronger psychological correlates of work well-being.

So if by “interesting” you mean *I care about the problems, enjoy the work itself, find it intellectually alive* — research generally says that matters a lot.

---

## 2) Salary matters, but less than people often expect

The best-known meta-analysis here is **Judge et al. (2010)** on the relationship between pay and job satisfaction.

The broad conclusion was:

- the relationship is **positive but modest**
- more pay does improve satisfaction on average
- but the effect size is much smaller than many people intuitively assume

This is a very common finding: **money matters, but not nearly as much as intrinsic work quality, autonomy, relationships, and fit** once you are already in a comfortable compensation range.

That last point matters especially for people already in high-skill, high-pay jobs. The marginal happiness gain from “a bit higher salary” is often relatively small compared with a large drop in:

- interest
- meaning
- autonomy
- learning
- social fit

So if the new offer is “somewhat more money, much less interesting,” the literature would generally predict **the money alone is unlikely to fully compensate**.

---

## 3) Leadership/promotion is more mixed than people think

Your case is not just “money vs interesting work,” because the Google role also includes **leading a small team**, which you say you’ve always wanted.

Research on **promotion** and managerial advancement is more mixed than many people expect.

A recurring finding in panel-data studies is:

- promotion can raise **job satisfaction temporarily**
- but the effect often **fades**
- and promotions can also increase **stress**, workload, and role conflict

One often-cited example is work by **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma (2012)** using British panel data. Their results suggested that promotions:

- improved job satisfaction in the short run
- did **not** clearly improve broader life satisfaction in a lasting way
- were associated with **greater mental strain**

So the evidence does **not** say “management makes people happier.” It says something more like:

> management/promotion can be satisfying if it matches what the person genuinely wants, but it often brings stress and does not automatically improve overall well-being.

That nuance matters a lot in your case.

---

## 4) What matters most may be whether leadership is itself intrinsically important to you

There is a crucial distinction in the literature between:

- **extrinsic motives**: money, title, status
- **intrinsic or self-concordant motives**: interest, growth, meaning, personally valued goals

Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and **Sheldon & Elliot** suggests that people tend to do better psychologically when they pursue goals that are **self-concordant** — i.e. aligned with their genuine interests and values — rather than goals pursued mainly for external rewards.

This matters because “leading a small team” could be either:

- an extrinsic/status move, or
- an intrinsic aspiration you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time

If it is the second, then it should not be treated as just “less interesting work plus more money.” It may be **more intrinsically meaningful** than it first sounds, because it fulfills a deep goal of yours.

Research would predict better outcomes when people choose roles that satisfy core psychological needs:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and related work. If leadership gives you:
- more autonomy,
- more sense of growth/mastery,
- more connection with others through mentoring/building,

then that may offset some loss of technical or research interest.

So the real comparison is probably not:

- OpenAI = interesting
- Google = money/title

It is more like:

- OpenAI = intellectual interest
- Google = leadership aspiration + some extra money + maybe less intellectual interest

Research strongly suggests that **intrinsic aspiration vs intrinsic aspiration** is the real tradeoff to examine.

---

## 5) On regret: direct evidence is thinner, but the broad pattern is suggestive

There is less direct research on “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking more money?”

I don’t know of a clean, definitive study answering exactly that question.

But two relevant things are known:

### A. Long-term regret often centers on paths not taken
The broader regret literature, especially **Gilovich & Medvec**, suggests that over the long run people often regret **inactions** more than actions — especially missed opportunities tied to identity, growth, or meaning.

That doesn’t map perfectly onto your case, but it does suggest that people may later regret *not exploring a path they deeply wanted* more than they regret small foregone income differences.

### B. Poor person-job fit predicts turnover and dissatisfaction
A large person-job fit literature shows that when people take roles that don’t fit their interests/values, they are more likely to experience:

- lower satisfaction
- lower commitment
- higher turnover intentions
- more burnout

So if the Google role is genuinely “much less interesting” and not meaningfully compensated by the leadership part, research would predict a higher chance of dissatisfaction later.

But if leading a team is a long-standing aspiration and part of your identity, then declining it may itself become the thing you wonder about later.

So the regret literature doesn’t give a one-line answer, but it supports this idea:

> People regret violating their deeper values or not pursuing identity-relevant opportunities more than they regret modest differences in pay.

---

## 6) If you want the most research-based way to think about your decision

Based on the literature, the highest-value questions are not “Which brand is better?” or even “Which pays more?”

They are:

1. **How much intrinsic interest would you lose?**
   - How much do you care about the day-to-day work itself?

2. **Is leading a team itself intrinsically meaningful to you?**
   - Not just prestigious, but genuinely energizing?

3. **What happens to autonomy?**
   - Management can increase autonomy in some ways and reduce it in others.

4. **What happens to competence and growth?**
   - Will you feel challenged and improving, or administratively diluted?

5. **What happens to stress/load?**
   - Promotion-like roles often bring more strain.

6. **What happens to meaning?**
   - Is the mission or problem space less compelling in a way that will matter every day?

Research would generally predict that **daily lived job quality** — interest, meaning, autonomy, fit, good relationships — matters more for happiness than a modest pay bump.

But it would also predict that **a deeply desired leadership opportunity** can matter a lot if it is truly part of your values rather than just a résumé move.

---

## Condensed takeaway

If I summarize the research very bluntly:

- **Interesting, meaningful work tends to beat modestly higher pay** for long-term job satisfaction.
- **Promotions/leadership do not reliably make people happier overall**; they often add stress and give only temporary boosts.
- **The biggest exception** is when the leadership role fulfills a genuine intrinsic goal of yours.
- So the key empirical question is not “salary vs interest,” but:
  **which option better matches your core motivations over the next few years?**

---

## If you want actual papers to look up

A good starter list would be:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being
- **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)** — self-concordant goals
- **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma (2012)** — promotions and job satisfaction/stress
- **Allan et al.** — meaningful work and well-being
- **Van den Broeck et al.** — self-determination theory at work
- **Gilovich & Medvec** — long-term regret / inaction vs action

If you want, I can give you a **more formal mini literature review** with the strongest papers, short summaries, and what each one does or doesn’t imply for your specific decision.
